title: Campaign: production restore + live prod recall proof
description:
Operator directive (QUEUE.md 2026-07-02T20:07). After recall-v2 reaches terminal state, restore the production Sinex runtime. The TimescaleDB compression-lock fix is on master but the restore has never been executed.

Sequence: (1) read-only verification plan first — data integrity of the 74M events, then service bring-up order; (2) execute as explicit operator-visible control-plane operation; (3) prove: production sinexd healthy, event counts match pre-incident expectations, one recall query answered from prod data.

Every external "live substrate" claim is gated on this. Note prod boot robustness work in gh#2182-remaining (schema-apply idempotent trigger guards) — check it before bring-up; the boot-race can recur via CAGG refresh policies racing DROP/CREATE TRIGGER.

Refs: QUEUE.md, gh#2182 comment trail, prod snapshot /var/lib/sinex/.prewipe-snapshot-20260627 (kept-data recovery, no wipe).
design: Read-only restore readiness packet written: .agent/scratch/research/2026-07-03-sinex-mhk-prod-restore-readiness.md. Current evidence: prod postgresql/nats/sinexd inactive; PGDATA /var/lib/sinex/postgresql/18 exists, PG_VERSION=18, postmaster.pid absent, pg_controldata reports cluster state 'shut down' with latest checkpoint 2026-06-30T02:57:35, data checksums enabled; journal shows PostgreSQL clean shutdown and NATS clean shutdown, while sinexd timed out and was SIGKILLed after module drain. Schema boot-race prerequisite is handled by 8f25c3eb0 / sinex-yss. Pending acceptance: exact ~74M row-count proof, prod sinexd health, and one recall query from prod require an explicit operator-visible start.
acceptance_criteria: prod sinexd healthy; event counts match pre-incident expectations (~74M); one recall query answered from prod data; restore executed as an explicit operator-visible operation.
notes:
2026-07-03 runtime restore found a second production blocker after disk-backpressure fix: sinexd skipped stream bootstrap because SINEX_NATS_STREAMS_MANAGED_EXTERNALLY=true, but Nix-managed NATS topology omitted PROD_SINEX_RAW_EVENTS_CONFIRMED / prod.events.confirmed.>. Event engine persisted rows then failed fatal confirmation publish with 'no stream found for given subject'. Fix in progress: add confirmed-events stream and ACL subject to nixos/modules/nats.nix, switch, then restart and verify no confirmation_durability_gap.
2026-07-03 continuation: production restore reached healthy serving state after fixing and deploying multiple blockers. Sinex commits involved: e4faedebe schema apply hardening, e729745fa disk-backpressure requires low free space, 77c129ff9 confirmed-events stream provisioning, f3c3b69ac static git safe.directory env. Sinnix commits involved: eed0c1a confirmed stream provisioning and fb93822 persistent source-access repair + service-level Git safe.directory env. Deployed with SINNIX_SINEX_OVERRIDE=/realm/project/sinex nix develop --command switch, then restarted sinexd. Post-restart proof: systemctl shows active/running main PID 745417; service unit has GIT_CONFIG safe.directory=/realm/project/sinex; sudo -u sinex can read Atuin, Chrome, qutebrowser history DBs; journal after restart has no new dubious ownership / unable to open database file / git log exited errors. Recovery action: raw consumer was pinned at ack_floor 525 behind stale sensing/missing materials and DLQ pressure; sampled raw sequences showed mixed stale half-material shell/browser, orphaned ActivityWatch, and self-telemetry. Purged already-poisoned transport backlog intentionally to establish fresh realtime boundary: nats stream purge PROD_SINEX_RAW_EVENTS removed 11631 messages; nats stream purge PROD_SINEX_RAW_EVENTS_DLQ removed 836 messages. This drops recovery-prefix raw messages, including some recoverable half-built/fresh telemetry, and is recorded as an intentional restore tradeoff. Post-purge proof: sinexctl runtime health returned healthy=true, raw_ingest_dlq empty; PROD_SINEX_RAW_EVENTS pending=0 ack_pending=3 redelivered=0 shortly after purge; PROD_SOURCE_MATERIAL pending=0 ack_pending=4; DB/NATS/confirmation/replay/SSE components healthy. Remaining follow-up: source-material assembler still reports active sensing rotating materials and occasional long lock-hold warnings; old failed/recovered_partial materials remain historical residue; implement/prioritize paced drain, source-material defrag/cleanup, storage-growth, and DLQ/root-cause analytics Beads rather than relying on manual purge next time.
close_reason: Completed by production restore and proof packet .agent/demos/sinex/prod-restore-recall-proof-20260703T095311Z. Evidence: deployed local Sinex/Sinnix fixes, restarted sinexd.service, runtime health healthy=true/serving=true with DB/NATS/raw-ingest DLQ/confirmation/replay/SSE healthy; raw consumer caught up pending=0 ack_pending=0 redelivered=0; core.events Timescale chunk estimate 80,872,976 rows, matching/exceeding the pre-incident ~74M expectation and consistent with the earlier exact restore count around 80.8M; source_material_registry exact 34,782; persisted DLQ exact 0. Live prod recall proof: sinexctl recall --rpc-url https://127.0.0.1:9999 --window 5m --limit 10 --include-self-observation --format json returned in ~140ms with 12 events across 5 sources, including activitywatch and git material-provenance rows. Residual broad recall latency is tracked separately as sinex-0tj; source-material debt/defrag remains under existing robustness work.

title: Campaign: sinex-recall v2 demo with committed baseline arm (external proof)
description:
Operator directive (QUEUE.md 2026-07-02T19:48). Produce sinex-recall v2 on the dev runtime: multi-source (fs+git+shell+browser) reconstruction of one real work window through the shared context recall view, WITH a committed side-by-side baseline arm (raw atuin search + git log over the same window), stranger-readable README, one-command regeneration.

Rules (PROCESS.md External-Proof Campaigns): campaign outranks open-ended substrate slices; substrate repair is in scope only where this artifact would otherwise be false. Slice closure is not campaign closure — stays top priority until terminal state recorded. Capabilities may not be silos; the demo packaging may be one-off but its facts must land as composable query/view capability.

Suggested window: 2026-07-01 afternoon PR-train (ground truth checkable against merged PRs).

State: root `sinexctl recall --at/--window` exists (PR #2261 / local commits bf3b5c4fe..92fe16cea) with source caveats. Known gaps: recall windows 2h-48h returned only journald/shell.atuin/fs-watcher; browser/git source-specific queries timed out (see latency-diagnosis issue).

Refs: QUEUE.md, PROCESS.md External-Proof Campaigns, situation-brief §5.4 (demonstration crux).
acceptance_criteria: Cold-reader gate: a fresh agent given ONLY the demo directory states what it proves, names sample frame and caveats, and reproduces it with one command.
notes: Folded from the demo portfolio: the 'reconstruct my Tuesday with honest gaps' demo IS this campaign's artifact — interleaved multi-source rows each resolving via show <ref> to source bytes, with capture outages rendered as explicit gaps (the anti-cherry-picking control). Random-sample K rows and trace each to raw bytes as part of the cold-reader check.
close_reason: Completed by local demo packet .agent/demos/sinex/sinex-recall-v2-multisource-20260701T2200-0000. The packet reconstructs 2026-07-01T22:00:00+02:00..2026-07-02T00:00:00+02:00 through sinexctl recall with browser/git/shell/filesystem/derived context present, raw git and Atuin baseline arms, sample event refs, and show event drilldowns carrying material anchors or derived lineage. Regeneration command .agent/demos/sinex/sinex-recall-v2-multisource-20260701T2200-0000/regenerate.sh exited 0. Independent cold-reader pass by subagent Boole: PASS; it stated the claim/non-claims, named the sample frame and caveat, and reran the command successfully.

title: Timescale columnstore policy compresses nothing: 98G in 7 chunks, all uncompressed, job reports Success
description: Observed 2026-07-10 on sinex_prod: timescaledb_information.chunks for the events hypertable shows 7 chunks, ALL is_compressed=f, totalling 98GB (one chunk alone 68GB); yet the Columnstore Policy job (id 2879) reports last_run_status=Success, 0 total_failures, last run same night — a vacuous success that compresses zero chunks. Likely causes: compress_after horizon excludes everything (misconfigured interval), chunks pinned by continuous aggregates/locks, or the policy targets a different hypertable member of the two 'events' entries (core.events vs reflection.events). Journal-heavy row payloads should columnstore-compress extremely well (the same content compresses 16:1 under file-level zstd) — fixing this likely shrinks the 99GB database to the 15-30GB range and proportionally shrinks pg_dump sizes and dump durations.
acceptance_criteria:
- root cause identified and fixed; policy compresses eligible chunks on schedule
- one-shot backfill compression of historical chunks executed (or explicitly declined with reason)
- database size before/after recorded
notes:
## Steps to Reproduce
1. psql sinex_prod: SELECT is_compressed, count(*), sum size FROM timescaledb_information.chunks WHERE hypertable_name='events' GROUP BY is_compressed  -> 7 chunks, all f, 98GB
2. SELECT job_id, last_run_status, total_failures FROM timescaledb_information.job_stats JOIN timescaledb_information.jobs USING (job_id) WHERE proc_name LIKE '%compress%'  -> job 2879 Success, 0 failures, ran 2026-07-09 23:51Z
3. Observe the contradiction: successful policy runs, zero compressed chunks, 68GB single chunk (_hyper_1_24_chunk).

title: Journal/self-capture dominates the dataset: 85% of events and most blob volume is machine chatter
description: Consuming-host analysis 2026-07-10: core.events holds ~82M rows of which systemd (32.3M) + journald (29.7M) + sinex self-logs (6.8M) + sinexd.automaton/event_engine (1.4M) = ~85% machine chatter, much of it sinexd capturing its own log output (heartbeats are ~90% of journald volume on the host). The blob repository (167G logical) is dominated by 100MB NDJSON journal-export chunks where each one-line message carries ~1KB of repeated metadata fields; btrfs zstd stores it at 6% (11G on disk), confirming near-pure text redundancy. Directions: (a) field-trim the journal capture (drop the invariant _CAP_EFFECTIVE/_MACHINE_ID/... boilerplate per line or dictionary-encode), (b) filter or heavily sample sinexd's own heartbeat/log output at ingest (self-capture feedback loop), (c) retention tiering: journal-class events are low-value after N weeks while personal-source events are keep-forever — one retention policy for both is wrong. Related: consuming host filed the blob store's first-ever backup job 2026-07-10; smaller blobs = smaller backups.
acceptance_criteria:
- journal/self-log share of daily NEW events and blob bytes measured before/after
- self-capture loop addressed (filter, sampling, or explicit keep decision recorded)
- retention/tiering decision recorded per source class
notes: INVARIANT CORRECTION (operator, 2026-07-10): sinex is designed append-only with NO retention — the original 'retention tiering' direction in this bead is WITHDRAWN; do not build retention machinery. The valid levers are strictly ingest-side: (a) field-trim the journal capture (drop/dictionary-encode invariant per-line metadata), (b) filter or sample sinexd's own heartbeat/log output at ingest (self-capture loop) — capture-policy decisions about what ENTERS the dataset, not about deleting what is stored. If storage economics ever demand actual retention, that requires an explicit operator re-decision of the no-retention invariant first. Note: the consuming host is simultaneously raising raw journald retention to 128G on NVMe, so long-horizon journal HISTORY is preserved outside sinex regardless of what ingest filtering decides.

title: Reduce sinexd rustc peak memory — frontend-bound, profile knobs exhausted
description:
Consuming-host incident 2026-07-09: 17 earlyoom kills of the sinexd lib rustc at 8.0-11.5 GiB RSS. Analysis (full numbers + kill-log evidence in GitHub issue #2456 and its comments): the #2190 cheap-compilation profile (opt-level=1, codegen-units=256) did NOT reduce peak memory — its own baseline was ~9.8 GiB and post-mitigation observations are 8-11.5 GiB, because those knobs act on LLVM/codegen while the peak lives in the single-threaded frontend (typeck/MIR/mono collection) of the 219K-LOC single crate. Crate growth since #2190 is only ~5%, so it is structural.

Work plan (measure -> cheap levers -> structural):
1. Measure: cargo llvm-lines -p sinexd --lib (under xtask ci postgres for sqlx env) to rank monomorphization hogs; optionally nightly -Ztime-passes for per-pass RSS.
2. Cheap levers: box top monomorphization offenders (axum/tower stacks likely), derive diet (serde+schemars+validator+bon stacks; gate non-runtime derives out of prod build), move the remaining 54 query!-family macros into sinex-db, cap cargo --jobs in the packaged build (sinexd+xtask lib rustcs died in the same second — concurrency overlap), -Zshare-generics after any split.
3. Structural fallback: no compilation unit >~80K LOC — extract api/(42K)+sources/(27K)+event_engine/(23K)+automata/(7K) leaving runtime+glue ~75K, or split runtime/(70K) itself if dependency direction forbids.
Related: the 88 separate [[test]] binaries each link the full lib — an aggregating tests/main.rs harness links once; same disease, test-suite costume. Acceptance: a clean cargo build --release -p sinexd --lib measured with /usr/bin/time -v peaks under 6 GiB.
acceptance_criteria:
- cargo llvm-lines -p sinexd --lib measurement recorded in this bead (top-20 monomorphization offenders)
- a clean 'cargo build --release -p sinexd --lib' measured with /usr/bin/time -v peaks under 6 GiB rustc RSS
- consuming-host verification: a sinnix sinex-bump switch completes without any earlyoom/oomd kill of rustc (kill_event table empty for the build window)
- decision recorded: cheap levers sufficient, or the <=80K crate split is scheduled as follow-up beads
notes:
MIGRATION NOTE: GH issue #2456 (and its 3 analysis comments) will be retired per the operator's beads-full-stop decision — full analysis inlined here so the bead is self-contained; treat GH as dead.

== INLINED ANALYSIS (2026-07-10, consuming-host incident) ==
EVIDENCE: 17 earlyoom SIGTERMs of rustc on 2026-07-09 (consuming host kill_event log), all 'rustc --crate-name sinexd ... crate/sinexd/src/lib.rs', RSS 6.5-11.5 GiB, both nix-sandbox builds (opt-level=1) and cargo clippy builds. sinexd+xtask lib rustcs died in the same second twice = concurrent peak overlap at graph tail.

MITIGATION AUDIT: #2190 profile (opt-level=1, codegen-units=256, lto=false) does NOT reduce peak memory. Its own recorded baseline at defaults: ~9.8 GiB. Observed WITH mitigation: 8.0-11.5 GiB. Crate grew only ~5% since. Cause: those knobs act on LLVM/codegen; the peak is the single-threaded FRONTEND (typeck/trait resolution/MIR/mono collection) of a 219K-LOC crate (465 files — half the 464K workspace). Any '~4 GiB post-mitigation' claim was likely a cargo-check measurement (no codegen) — check flatters the mitigated phase while the frontend cost is identical.

CRATE WEIGHTS (crate/sinexd/src): runtime/ 70K, api/ 42K, sources/ 27K, event_engine/ 23K, automata/ 7K, supervisor 0.6K. sqlx query!-family: 54 in sinexd, 307 workspace (most already in sinex-db). ~757 derive sites; serde+schemars+validator+bon stack on the same types.

PLAN (measure -> cheap -> structural), same as description: llvm-lines under xtask ci postgres; box top monomorphization offenders (axum/tower suspects); derive diet (gate non-runtime derives); move 54 query! to sinex-db; cap cargo --jobs in packaged build; -Zshare-generics after split. Structural target: no unit >~80K — extract api+sources+event_engine+automata (=99K, leaves runtime+glue ~75K) or split runtime/ itself. Splitting math caution: extracting only api+sources (69K) leaves a 150K unit — NOT enough.

CACHE CONTEXT: since the #883 no-spend decision no workflow builds/pushes the nix package; the consuming desktop is builder of record and pushes its built closure to sinity.cachix.org post-activation, so each rev pays the peak at most once. Related bead: sinex-v7od (88 [[test]] binaries each link the full lib; nextest already gives per-test process isolation, so aggregation loses nothing).

title: expand_cascade() query plan abandons GIN index for large root sets, causing statement timeout
description:
## Symptom
`sinexctl ops replay execute` on a real-volume operation (200k root events, a
10-minute ActivityWatch slice) fails with:

    Database error: Failed to archive replay cascade atomically
    Database error: expand cascade graph (sqlstate: 57014)
    error returned from database: canceling statement due to statement timeout

## Root cause (confirmed via EXPLAIN, not EXPLAIN ANALYZE -- verified 2026-07-09)
`core.expand_cascade(temp_table, max_depth)` (defined in sinex-schema, called
from `crate/sinex-db/src/replay/state_machine.rs` cascade archival path) walks
child events per depth level via:

    SELECT DISTINCT e.id, ...
    FROM core.events e
    JOIN current_level cl ON e.source_event_ids && ARRAY[cl.id]
    WHERE NOT EXISTS (...)

`core.events.source_event_ids` HAS a GIN index
(`ix_events_source_event_ids`, partial WHERE source_event_ids IS NOT NULL).
For a SMALL current_level (few root ids), Postgres plans this as a nested
loop with a GIN index probe per row -- cheap. For a LARGE current_level
(tested: 200,000 rows), the planner instead chooses a Hash Anti Join across
the ENTIRE core.events hypertable (all chunks, tens of millions of rows,
Seq Scan on most of them) and applies the array-overlap as a post-join
Join Filter -- the GIN index is not used for the join condition at all in
this plan. Measured EXPLAIN cost: 268,878,704. This is not resource
contention or a missing index; it's the query shape defeating the index
once the probe-set cardinality crosses whatever threshold flips the
planner's choice.

## Reproduction
    CREATE TEMP TABLE cascade_probe (id uuid, depth int, parent_ids uuid[], processed bool);
    INSERT INTO cascade_probe SELECT id, 0, '{}', false FROM (
      SELECT id FROM core.events WHERE source_material_id IS NOT NULL LIMIT 200000
    ) t;
    EXPLAIN
    WITH current_level AS (SELECT id FROM cascade_probe WHERE depth = 0 AND processed = FALSE)
    SELECT DISTINCT e.id FROM core.events e
    JOIN current_level cl ON e.source_event_ids && ARRAY[cl.id]
    WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM cascade_probe existing WHERE existing.id = e.id);

## Impact
Blocks sinex-60r (production AW replay) and any replay/archive operation
whose scope exceeds roughly tens of thousands of root events -- exactly the
scope real backlog replays need. The oversized-NATS-payload bug (PR #2451,
#2453) that was masking this as a "timeout" is now fixed, so this is the
next real blocker, not a symptom of that bug.

## Fix direction (not yet implemented)
Restructure the per-level join so cardinality of current_level doesn't
defeat the GIN index -- likely unnest current_level's ids into a form
Postgres can hash/merge against an unnested source_event_ids array, or add
a planner hint / rewrite as a LATERAL subquery per batch of ids, or batch
current_level into chunks small enough to keep the nested-loop-with-GIN-
probe plan. Verify with EXPLAIN (not EXPLAIN ANALYZE against prod) before
and after on a realistic root-set size (100k+).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `core.expand_cascade` query plan uses the GIN index (or an equivalent
      efficient strategy) regardless of current_level row count.
- [ ] A real replay operation with 100k+ root events completes execute
      without hitting statement_timeout.
- [ ] EXPLAIN cost for the reproduction case above drops by orders of
      magnitude (not just "faster in practice" -- the plan shape must
      change, not rely on caching).
- [ ] sinex-60r's production AW replay is unblocked by this fix (may still
      need its own batching strategy for the full multi-day scope, but the
      per-batch execute must not time out).
close_reason: Fixed via PR #2455 (query rewrite, array_agg instead of per-row JOIN, ~100x EXPLAIN cost reduction) + PR #2457 (permanent schema-apply drift-detection fix, closing the gap that required a manual psql patch to apply #2455's fix to sinex_prod tonight). Verified end to end: sinex-60r's production AW replay (30,000 events, desktop.activitywatch, 2026-06-12T03:58-03:59) completed successfully with correct app/title/url data in the replayed events.

title: CommitFrontier<Atom>: one typed progress-frontier primitive for sources, automata, and invalidation
description: The mechanism half of Batch A (r6d.11 receipts are the durability half; this is the progress half). Today frontier logic is hand-rolled and scattered: adapter_source.rs alone has 75 cursor/checkpoint touchpoints and advances a composite cursor via recursive JSON overlay (merge_cursor_update:2123-2155) that can leap failed records; automaton progress lives in three more files (adapter/{process,run,state_io}.rs) with its own ordering bugs; invalidation acks are a third ad-hoc notion of progress. The primitive: CommitFrontier<A: ProgressAtom> — atoms enter in-order, complete out-of-order (receipts), the committable frontier advances only over contiguous terminal atoms; holes (failed/pending atoms) block commitment past them and are queryable for debt routing; snapshot/restore is the checkpoint payload. One implementation, property-tested once (monotonicity, hole-blocking, restore equivalence), used by: source cursor commits (r6d.4), multi-intent record atoms (w4i), automaton input batches (vxu), invalidation scopes (r6d.7). Kills the class where each caller reinvents ordering and gets it differently wrong.
design:
DESIGN (2026-07-08, receipt backend (A) final):

API: CommitFrontier<A: ProgressAtom> — submit(atom) -> AtomTicket (admission is IN ORDER; the ticket is the ordering witness); complete(ticket, TerminalOutcome) arrives OUT OF ORDER (receipts settle whenever); frontier() -> highest contiguous terminal position + a typed CheckpointPayload; holes() -> non-terminal atoms blocking the frontier, with age (this is the debt/observability surface). TerminalOutcome = exactly the r6d.11 progress-unlocking receipt states (PersistedConfirmed | Suppressed/Superseded-with-evidence | DurableDebt | NoOutputSettled | SpoolAcceptedLossless-post-r6d.5). Nothing else advances the frontier.

ATOM FAMILIES (caller-defined, per r6d.11 AC): SourceRecord (checkpoint payload = source cursor fragment), AutomatonInputBatch (payload = last_input_event_id + state identity), InvalidationScope, WindowFlush{flush_id}.

CHECKPOINT = SERIALIZED FRONTIER: the cursor is DERIVED from the frontier, never merged past holes — this deletes merge_cursor_update recursive JSON overlay (adapter_source.rs:2123-2155) rather than wrapping it, and kills the failed-record-skipped class (r6d.4 window b) structurally. Failed records: per-source policy from r6d.8 LossAuthority decides Hole (halt frontier) vs DurableDebt (advance WITH durable debt evidence). DeferredSourceMaterial is non-terminal unless a durable pending record exists (r6d.4 AC).

PLACEMENT: pure sync core (data structure + invariants) in sinex-primitives — it needs no tokio/NATS — with a thin async notify adapter in sinexd runtime. The pure core is what 87hu property-tests share as the reference model.

CRASH RECOVERY: restore frontier from checkpoint; post-frontier inputs re-enter via normal replay/redelivery; the r6d.11 request_id ledger reconciles receipts for atoms that were in flight (duplicate completion must be idempotent by ticket).

PROPERTY INVARIANTS (the 87hu model): monotonicity; out-of-order completion never reorders commits; a hole blocks everything after it regardless of completion order; checkpoint round-trip = identical frontier; duplicate complete() is a no-op.
acceptance_criteria: Primitive lands in sinexd runtime (or primitives if dependency-free) with property tests for monotonicity, out-of-order completion, hole blocking, and checkpoint round-trip; adapter_source cursor commits and automaton processed-input marks route through it (JSON-overlay merge deleted, not wrapped); frontier state is the checkpoint payload for both; debt surface exposes blocking holes; Batch-A caller beads cite it as their implementation vehicle.
close_reason: Merged sinex/#2422: CommitFrontier<C> landed standalone in crate/sinex-primitives/src/commit_frontier.rs — submit()/complete()/frontier()/holes() API, TerminalOutcome carrying the r6d.11 receipt-state vocabulary, pure sync core (no tokio/NATS/clock). Verify: xtask test -p sinex-primitives --lib -E 'test(commit_frontier)' -> 8 passed (monotonicity, out-of-order completion, hole-blocking, checkpoint round-trip, duplicate-complete no-op x2, empty-frontier, proptest over random completion orderings). NOT wired into any caller (adapter_source.rs cursor commits, automaton input-batch marks, invalidation scopes) — that's r6d.4/vxu/r6d.7/w4i, deliberately deferred (see r6d.11 notes for sequencing).

title: Civil-time contract: operator-local day/hour semantics for every calendar-shaped product
description: VERIFIED: hourly/daily automata bucket in UTC by declaration ('completed UTC-hour/UTC-day summaries', hourly.rs:4, daily.rs:4, floor_to_hour/floor_to_day on raw timestamps) and no timezone concept exists anywhere in sinexd/primitives (only per-source parse assumptions). The operator lives in CET/CEST: the 'daily' boundary sits at 01:00-02:00 local, so late-evening work lands in the NEXT civil day, and DST shifts the boundary twice a year. Constructs silently broken: daily brief 'today' (a4w.2), reconstruct-my-Tuesday (cem.8), routine baselines per hour-of-week (a4w.3.6 — UTC hour-of-week smears across DST), plan.vs.actual (calendar events are civil-time, a4w.3.8), any weekly review. nbi.5 already fixes one instance (entity enricher active-hours) — this bead owns the GENERAL contract so per-surface fixes stop being invented. CONTRACT SHAPE: ts_orig stays a UTC instant everywhere (storage/identity untouched); civil time is a RENDERING/BUCKETING concern parameterized by a single declared operator timezone (config, not per-query guess); civil buckets use timezone-aware day/hour floors with explicit DST-fold rules (spring-forward gap hours = empty, fall-back repeated hour = one 25h day, documented); hour-of-week baselines keyed on civil hour; calendar joins compare instants but LABEL in civil time. Storage-level CAs stay UTC (partition math untouched) — civil bucketing happens in automata that already bucket (hourly/daily semantics_version bump) or at the read/projection layer per the 8y8/1wm precedent; choosing which is part of this bead.
acceptance_criteria: Operator timezone is a single declared config with one source of truth; the contract doc specifies DST-fold rules and which layer applies civil bucketing (automata re-derive under a semantics_version bump vs read-layer projection — decision recorded with migration/replay cost); hourly/daily/baseline/brief/calendar surfaces all cite the contract; a DST-transition fixture proves the 23h and 25h days aggregate correctly and hour-of-week baselines do not smear; nbi.5 is reconciled as an instance of this contract.

title: Dedup contract matrix: three idempotence regimes across source modalities
description: Idempotence in Sinex is deliberately NON-uniform (operator-confirmed 2026-07-07): the design must name which regime governs each surface instead of pursuing blanket dedup. REGIME 1 — interpretation plane, deliberately non-idempotent: replay mints new ids/ts_coided by design; every reading is preserved (live or archived); nothing may 'dedupe interpretations'. Supersession (n9a) is NOT idempotence — it is single-live-interpretation-per-occurrence with full archival. REGIME 2 — occurrence plane, idempotence desired: the same real-world occurrence re-observed (re-import, re-scan, overlapping exports, mutable-row growth) must not yield two live rows claiming to be distinct occurrences. REGIME 3 — operator-adjudicated ambiguity: when occurrence identity is genuinely uncertain (two providers rendering the same conversation, near-duplicate files, the same email via IMAP and via export), the system must NOT auto-decide — it surfaces candidates with evidence through the curation plane and the judgment is durable and reversible. The deliverable is a per-modality contract matrix (in docs/architecture.md or crate docs) with columns: occurrence anchor, dup-detection point (source cursor / admission equivalence / object-level), re-import behavior, revision behavior (SuppressDuplicate vs SupersedeOnChange vs adjudicate), cross-source identity rule. Rows at minimum: append-only file (byte anchor), mutable-row DB (AW — anchor bucket_event_timestamp), API sync (provider id/etag), export re-import (provider id + content hash), overlapping exports from DIFFERENT providers of the same events (regime 3), live stream with no natural id (capture-time synthetic anchor — document that re-capture is a new occurrence by definition), directory snapshot (path+content fingerprint). Every source contract's checkpoint_family + occurrence_identity declaration must be reconciled against its row (h3g showed declaration/implementation drift is real).
acceptance_criteria: Matrix committed with every registered source contract mapped to a row and regime; each row names the enforcing mechanism at its ladder level (cursor / admission policy / object-level / curation) and its re-import + revision behavior; contradictions between declared checkpoint_family/occurrence_identity and actual query behavior are enumerated with fix beads; regime-3 rows define what evidence the adjudication surface presents; y8v, n9a, ecy, h3g are cited and consistent with the matrix.
notes:
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.
2026-07-08: y8v RATIFIED and closed — the matrix may now cite it as settled rather than pending. Bindings for the three regimes: (1) replay = deliberately NON-idempotent (new interpretations, new ids/ts_coided; occurrence coordinates stable); (2) occurrence idempotence = achieved by admission RevisionPolicy (n9a): SuppressDuplicate default, SupersedeOnChange for revisable kinds (intervals/sessions/windows, start-anchored keys per ecy); (3) ambiguous identity (overlapping spans, DIFFERENT start anchors, disagreeing sources) = operator adjudication via the judgment seat (7z0.4), never auto-decided. The matrix rows should now state, per source modality, WHICH RevisionPolicy its event types declare — that is the machine-checkable form of this bead.

title: Admission RevisionPolicy: SupersedeOnChange — same occurrence key, changed content, archive + replace
description: Implementation carrier for the y8v ruling. Extend admission equivalence handling (admission.rs:505-526 suppress-on-live-row) with a per-event-type RevisionPolicy: SuppressDuplicate (default, exact current behavior) | SupersedeOnChange (key matches live row: identical content-hash -> suppress; changed content -> archive live row via the existing archive machinery, admit the new interpretation, and fire the replay-style scope invalidation for derived descendants). Interval-class event types (state.interval, activity.window.summary, activity.session.boundary, attention.span) opt in. Without this mode, occurrence-stable keys (ecy) would make admission silently DISCARD every legitimate revision — the two beads only work together.
acceptance_criteria: RevisionPolicy declared per event type at the schema/registry level (typed, not string convention). SupersedeOnChange path: content-hash comparison, archive-before-insert (single-live-interpretation upheld), scope invalidation fired for derived descendants, all within admission settlement semantics (interacts correctly with r6d.12 aggregate settlement). Suppression/supersession outcomes are distinct in telemetry and in the receipt state machine (r6d.11 Suppressed vs a new Superseded terminal state). Tests: identical re-emit suppresses; changed re-emit supersedes exactly once; descendants invalidated; SuppressDuplicate types unchanged.
notes: Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.

title: Decision: interval revision contract — what happens when we learn an interval was longer, later, or wrong
description: No mechanism exists for revising an emitted interval outside a full replay cascade. Verified instances: (a) ActivityWatch extends event rows in place via heartbeats, but the source reads ORDER BY events.id with a ROWID cursor (activitywatch.rs:308) — ingested duration freezes at scan-time and the system NEVER learns the interval grew; (b) if a grown row were re-read, the new interpretation gets a new trigger UUID and interval-lift keys include that id (interval_lift.rs:419-421) -> overlapping duplicate interval, no supersession; (c) late imports covering an already-lifted span duplicate as well. Choose the contract: (1) supersede-by-occurrence — new interval with same occurrence key archives the old row (requires the occurrence-stable keys bead); (2) correction events (interval.revised) that consumers must fold; (3) intervals immutable-once-emitted, revision pushed entirely to projection rebuild (68c plane). The choice binds ALL interval producers and defines what downstream joins/projections must do on revision. Lens: handwaved implementability — every consumer bead currently assumes intervals are final.
design:
RULING (2026-07-07, recorded under the operator instruction to encode best solutions; veto window open — flag objections on this bead):

1. INTERVAL OCCURRENCE IDENTITY IS START-ANCHORED. Identity = (state_kind, subject_id, start_anchor) where start_anchor = the material occurrence coordinates (source_material_id, anchor_byte) of the start-evidence event when material-provenance, else the start parent's own occurrence identity per its contract. Ends are NEVER part of identity — ends move as evidence grows; starts are the stable coordinate. This is the doctrine-conformant fix for keys that today embed trigger UUIDs (interval_lift.rs:419-421).

2. REVISION MECHANISM = SUPERSEDE-BY-OCCURRENCE AT ADMISSION. New per-event-type RevisionPolicy: SuppressDuplicate (default, current behavior) | SupersedeOnChange. Under SupersedeOnChange, an incoming event whose equivalence key matches a live row: identical content-hash -> suppress; changed content -> archive the live row (existing audit.archived_events machinery) and admit the new interpretation. One live interpretation per occurrence is preserved; the archive keeps every superseded reading.

3. REJECTED ALTERNATIVES. (a) Correction events (interval.revised): pushes folding logic into EVERY consumer — joins, projections, recall, exports — and violates single-live-interpretation-per-occurrence; consumers already speak archive+invalidation. (b) Immutable-once-emitted + projection-rebuild-only: intervals are canonical derived events feeding FURTHER derivation (episodes, attribution) — stale live rows poison the derivation DAG and projection rebuild cannot fix derived children.

4. CONSUMER FOLDING RULE. Supersession fires the SAME scope-invalidation path as replay: archive cascade for derived descendants of the superseded row + invalidation signals. One mechanism, no new plane. Projections react via 68c.4 staleness propagation. Joins re-derive like any replay.

5. TRIGGER TAXONOMY. (a) Grown mutable source row (AW, h3g): re-read emits a new interpretation with the SAME start anchor -> supersede. (b) Late import overlapping an already-lifted span: same start anchor -> supersede; DIFFERENT anchor but overlapping time -> not an identity match — routes to overlap-conflict detection (dedup contract matrix) with operator adjudication when sources disagree. (c) Replay/re-scan outside a full cascade: same start anchor -> supersede — this ALSO resolves the partial-rescan duplicate problem (idempotence-by-supersession).

6. WATERMARK INTERACTION. A superseding interval may extend past a join watermark; joins consume only closed-and-settled intervals (pq2 decision input) and re-derive on invalidation.
acceptance_criteria: Ruling recorded (done — see design). Remaining scope: the implementation carrier (admission RevisionPolicy bead) lands; a fixture demonstrates the AW grown-duration case end-to-end (grow endtime after first scan -> exactly one live interval with the grown duration, superseded reading in audit.archived_events, dependent derived rows re-derived via invalidation); pq2 and the dedup contract matrix cite this ruling.
notes:
2026-07-07: decision content settled this session; bead stays open only for the fixture + citations. Veto window: operator may overturn by note here before the implementation bead starts.
2026-07-07 operator clarification (mid-session): idempotence is deliberately non-uniform — replay stays NON-idempotent (new interpretations by design); occurrence-level idempotence is wanted; ambiguous identity is the OPERATOR's call with system assistance. The ruling above is consistent: SupersedeOnChange operates on occurrence keys only, preserves every superseded interpretation in the archive, and routes non-identity overlaps to adjudication instead of auto-deciding. The three-regime framing is owned by the dedup contract matrix bead.
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.
RATIFIED 2026-07-08 (operator, decision-brief batch): veto window CLOSED, supersede-by-occurrence ruling FINAL. Remaining AC delegated: implementation carrier = n9a (open, spec-complete); AW grown-duration e2e fixture = h3g (its fix is exactly that scenario); pq2 design and the 908 matrix now cite this ruling. Closing this decision bead.
close_reason: Decision FINAL and operator-ratified 2026-07-08: interval revision contract = start-anchored occurrence identity + supersede-by-occurrence at admission (RevisionPolicy::SupersedeOnChange); correction-events and immutable-only REJECTED; consumer folding = the replay invalidation path. Full ruling in design. Remaining AC delegated with explicit notes: implementation = sinex-n9a (spec-complete), AW grown-duration e2e fixture = sinex-h3g (note added), citations = pq2 design + 908 matrix. Verify: bd show sinex-y8v (design RULING + ratification note); bd show sinex-h3g (fixture delegation note).

title: Derived interval/session identity is processing-order, not occurrence: counter equivalence keys collide and silently suppress
description: VERIFIED: analytics window equivalence key = 'activity-window-{counter}' (analytics.rs:286,311); session key = 'session-{counter}' (session.rs:165,192); attention span key = 'attention-span:{window_id}' inherits it (attention.rs:47,67). These keys encode per-checkpoint PROCESSING ORDER, not occurrence content. Replay resets counters; checkpoint loss/reset restarts them -> fresh derived events mint keys colliding with unrelated live rows -> admission equivalence dedup (fail-open, suppress-on-live-row) silently DROPS new derived data. Compounding: the declared OccurrenceIdentity contracts are built on parent EVENT ids (interval-lift Uuid5From '(...,start_parent_event_id,end_parent_event_id)' interval_lift.rs:741-743; attention '(source,parent_event_id)'; session '(source,parent_event_ids)') — parent ids are interpretation identity, minted anew each replay, so the declared occurrence identity is false under the identity doctrine. Lens: construct validity (equivalence key MUST be occurrence-derived per doctrine).
design:
KEY DERIVATION SPEC (per y8v ruling — start-anchored occurrence identity):
- state.interval (AdjacentTransitions / StartStopPair): key = hash(state_kind, subject_id, start_material_id, start_anchor_byte, semantics_version). Requires the automaton context to expose the trigger event's material occurrence coordinates (today only trigger_uuid is exposed — plumbing gap, note in implementation).
- state.interval (ObservedDuration / heartbeat bout): start anchor = material occurrence of the FIRST evidence row of the bout (post-zs6, a bout is one interval).
- activity.window.summary: key = hash(automaton, semantics_version, first-contributing-event material occurrence). Replay that shifts window boundaries changes which event is first -> different identity: ACCEPTABLE — stale windows are removed by cascade archival of their parents, or by time-overlap supersession fallback (y8v trigger b).
- activity.session.boundary: same rule via the first contributing window (transitively the first event anchor). session_counter survives ONLY as a display ordinal, never identity.
- attention.span: key = f(consumed window key), inheriting stability.
MIGRATION: new key format never collides with old counter keys under SuppressDuplicate (no false suppression). Old-format live rows become unreachable-by-key: retire them via replay cascade or accept until replay; document the choice in the PR.
acceptance_criteria: Equivalence keys for activity.window.summary, activity.session.boundary, attention.span, and state.interval derive from occurrence-stable content (state_kind/subject + input occurrence coordinates + boundary ts_orig values + semantics_version), never counters or parent interpretation ids. Counter fields may remain as display ordinals but not as identity. OccurrenceIdentity declarations on derived contracts either reference occurrence-stable inputs or are reclassified as interpretation lineage. Migration note covers dedup behavior across existing live rows (key format change = no false suppression, no dup storm). Regression: replay a fixture slice twice with a checkpoint reset between — zero silent suppressions of non-identical occurrences, zero duplicate live rows for identical ones.
notes:
2026-07-07: design settled per y8v ruling; the SupersedeOnChange admission mode is the companion implementation bead.
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.

title: Canonical JetStream topology spec: one source of truth for Nix bootstrap and runtime
description: VERIFIED: nixos/modules/nats.nix has ZERO reflection-lane coverage (streams or auth subjects) while sinexd starts both lane consumers and runtime bootstrap is suppressed in prod (SINEX_NATS_STREAMS_MANAGED_EXTERNALLY, sources.nix:593-594) — prod can start without streams/subjects the runtime consumes. Nix stream schema also cannot express discard/allow_direct/max_message_size and diverges on retention (168h vs runtime 72h/24h). AcquisitionManager::bootstrap_streams_with_namespace mutates SOURCE_MATERIAL with no external-management check (acquisition_manager.rs:302-350). Canonical activity+reflection stream tables in new-gpt-pro 07 report + 07-canonical-stream-topology.json; contract ledger in 07-nix-runtime-contract-table.csv. Source: new-gpt-pro 07 findings 1-4.
acceptance_criteria: One canonical spec covers activity lane, reflection lane, SOURCE_MATERIAL, and shared-client auth subjects with every runtime-required field (retention, storage, max_messages/bytes/age, discard, duplicate_window, allow_direct, max_message_size). Nix bootstrapStreams and Rust bootstrap are generated from or checked against the same Rust-exported JSON; a gate fails when nats.nix cannot express a required field. External-management mode verifies every required stream before sinexd serves — loud failure, never skipped bootstrap. Reflection streams + auth subjects provisioned with the same env/namespace prefixing as JetStreamTopology::for_lane. AcquisitionManager/MaterialAssembler honor external management or verify-without-mutating.
notes: 2026-07-07 DeepResearch input: dr-09-jetstream-operational-envelope.report.md — single-node JetStream limits: retention semantics, consumer fan-out, stale durables, sync_interval=always durability tradeoff, bounded dedupe windows, double-ack. Key for the spec: acked publish can precede per-message fsync under default file sync; dedupe window is rolling/bounded — never the durability authority (matches r6d.11 doctrine).
close_reason: Merged sinex/#2423: added discard/allowDirect/maxMessageSize options to the Nix stream schema (previously inexpressible — the actual gap that let discard:old silently regress to CLI default in externally-managed mode), corrected every activity-lane value to match the Rust bootstrap exactly (raw retention=work/maxAge=72h, DLQ/processing-failures maxAge=72h not 168h), added the five reflection-lane stream declarations + shared-client auth subjects (previously ZERO reflection coverage in Nix despite sinexd starting that consumer unconditionally). bootstrap.rs's externally-managed branch now calls verify_externally_managed_streams_present() (fails loud, names every missing stream) instead of skip-and-hope; acquisition_manager.rs's SOURCE_MATERIAL bootstrap gets the same external-management guard. Verify: xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E 'test(verify_externally_managed_streams)' -> 1 passed; nix flake check --no-build evaluates the module cleanly. Residual (not done, tracked here): Rust-exported JSON canonical spec consumed by both Nix+Rust as single source of truth (the full original architecture); live-NATS preflight CLI command diffing deployed topology against the spec. The manual value-matching this PR did closes the actual safety gaps (loud failure on drift, correct discard policy) without that larger code-gen undertaking.

title: Primitives: DerivedProductClass, ClaimSupport vector, and output declarations
description: Blueprint child ip.1 (04-interpretation-plane-blueprint.report.md has the exact enum/struct shapes).
acceptance_criteria: DerivedProductClass enum exactly covers canonical_derived_event, projection_row, analysis_claim, report_artifact, semantic_candidate, operator_judgment. ClaimSupport vector: support_level, source_coverage, temporal_quality, adjudication, evidence_event_count, evidence_material_count, support_family_count, counterevidence_count. Adjudicated ClaimSupport constructors require a typed judgment reference or are private to authority helpers. VERIFY: xtask test -p sinex-primitives -E 'test(derived_product_class|claim_support|derivation_output_declaration)'.

title: xtask stale-binary fallback can verify newer xtask source (false-pass P0)
description: VERIFIED on master: the devshell wrapper allowlists proof-producing commands (schema|check|test|build|deps|doctor|infra|run|docs|fix at flake.nix:1448) for existing-binary fallback, and rebuild failure falls back to the existing binary (flake.nix:1915-1944). If current xtask source cannot rebuild, the OLD binary still runs the gate and claims success over a tree whose verifier code it does not include. With no hosted CI, the local gate IS the gate — a stale verifier is a systemic false-pass. Detection: xtask check -p xtask vs SINEX_XTASK_FORCE_REBUILD=1 xtask check -p xtask; any pass printing 'Using existing xtask binary'/'Falling back' while forced rebuild fails. Source: new-gpt-pro 03-xtask-meta-plane-audit P0.
acceptance_criteria: Proof-producing commands (check/test/build/schema/docs check) refuse existing-binary fallback after rebuild failure; fallback remains only for observability/help commands. Every proof records verifier binary hash + rebuild status. Regression: with a deliberately broken xtask source tree, xtask check fails loudly instead of gating via the stale binary.
close_reason: Merged sinex/#2417: both existing-binary fallback sites now refuse (exit 101) for any command outside the existing is_observability_command safe set instead of silently gating on a stale/broken binary; every checkout-local invocation stamps binary hash + rebuild status to .sinex/state/xtask-binary-provenance.jsonl. Verified: nix flake check clean, functional test (broke xtask/src/main.rs, confirmed refusal, confirmed observability commands still degrade gracefully, restored and rebuilt clean). Verify: git -C /realm/project/sinex show 32879293d --stat; nix develop --command bash -c 'xtask status --summary'.

title: Confirmed-events stream publishes pre-redaction event image, diverging from the persisted row
description: VERIFIED on master: persist_batch_optimized clones PreparedEvent.event into admitted_batch, redacts the CLONE via policy_engine.redact_batch, persists the redacted refs — but confirmation publishes the ORIGINAL prepared.event (persist.rs confirmed_event_futs publish prepared.event; redaction chokepoint operates on admitted_batch clone). The confirmation contract (confirmation.rs:59-65) claims the body is the event exactly as persisted post-redaction and consumers do not refetch from Postgres — so downstream automata/SSE derive from data that was never persisted. Ranked high not as a privacy hole (privacy is optional here) but because the authority stream lies about the persisted image. Source: new-gpt-pro 02 EE-02.
acceptance_criteria: Persistence returns/stores the final persisted event image keyed by event id and confirmation publishes exactly that image (redacted payload + any persistence-time field changes such as resolved ts_orig). Regression test: DB-backed redaction rule active, assert confirmed payload equals persisted row. Existing confirmation durability-gap behavior unchanged.
close_reason: Merged sinex/#2421: persist_batch_optimized now captures the post-redaction admitted_batch per event_id (PersistBatchResult.redacted_events) BEFORE the admission plan filters duplicates/tombstones; the confirmation loop publishes that redacted image (with an .expect()-enforced invariant, not a silent fallback) instead of the pre-redaction prepared.event. Verify: xtask test -p sinexd --test '*' -E 'test(confirmation_publishes_redacted)' -> 1 passed — new test asserts confirmed payload matches persisted row exactly, not just 'some redacted string'. Note for future test authors: JetStreamConsumer::new() defaults to a noop policy engine; DB-backed redaction tests need .with_policy_engine().

title: Event-engine raw-message settlement must be aggregate-safe for multi-event intents
description: VERIFIED on master: EventIntent.events is a Vec (admission.rs:148-150) and the batcher packs whole chunks of events into one intent (event_transport.rs publish_intent_chunk), yet prepare_events clones the same jetstream::Message into every PreparedEvent (prepare.rs:30-38) and the engine settles per child: rejection routes the shared raw message to DLQ+ACK while a valid sibling exists only in memory (crash there = silent loss); tombstone/confirmed ACKs are per prepared child with durability-gap returned after ACK; not-ready material siblings NAK while ready siblings proceed (mixed ACK/NAK on one physical message); bisection-isolated poison rows DLQ the child's cloned message; DLQ identity uses events[0].id not the failed child (dlq.rs:24-43). Distinct from sinex-w4i (producer-side partial-record re-emit) — this is consumer-side raw-message settlement. Source: new-gpt-pro 02-event-engine-audit EE-01 (critical).
design:
2026-07-08 session: investigated, did not implement — recording the design
analysis since the bead had none yet.

CONFIRMED on master: prepare_events (jetstream_consumer/prepare.rs:21-63) pushes
one PreparedEvent per admitted child with message: msg.clone() — multiple
children from one EventIntent share clones of the SAME physical JetStream
message. Rejected/QuarantineNeeded children call route_validation_failure ->
route_to_dlq_and_ack(&msg, ...) INSIDE the per-decision loop, immediately
ACKing the shared raw message while OTHER admitted siblings from the same
message are still sitting unprocessed in the `prepared` vec, to be persisted
later by persist_and_confirm_batch. If a sibling's later persistence needs a
NAK (retryable failure), NAKing its message clone is a no-op: the underlying
JetStream delivery was already permanently acked via the rejected sibling's
path. Redelivery never happens; that sibling's data is silently lost. This is
the exact "mixed ACK/NAK/DLQ outcomes on one physical message" bug named in
this bead's AC.

TWO CANDIDATE FIXES, evaluated:
(A) Enforce singleton EventIntent.events (never batch >1 event per physical
    JetStream message). Simple, low-risk, eliminates the bug class by
    construction. REJECTED as the default fix: event_transport.rs's
    send_batch_nats/publish_intent_chunk chunking exists specifically to
    amortize per-message JetStream overhead for high-frequency small events
    (window-focus-change volume etc). Forcing singleton multiplies physical
    message count by the current average chunk size — a real throughput/
    ack-overhead regression, and sinex-9u4's whole saga (2M-message stream
    jam) is evidence this system is NOT slack on JetStream message-count
    pressure. Do not take this path without operator sign-off that the
    throughput cost is acceptable.
(B) Per-physical-message settlement coordinator: an Arc<RawEnvelopeSettlement>
    shared across every PreparedEvent cloned from one raw message, tracking a
    countdown of children + a needs_redelivery flag. Rejected/not-ready/
    persisted/tombstoned children each report their outcome to the
    coordinator instead of acting on `msg` directly; the coordinator acks
    the shared message exactly once, only after every child has reported a
    terminal outcome, and NAKs (redelivery, not silent loss) if any child
    needed it. This preserves current batching/throughput and is the fix
    the AC actually describes ("one settlement coordinator per physical
    JetStream message"). PREFERRED, but genuinely invasive: every existing
    ack/nak/dlq call site that currently acts on `prepared.message` directly
    needs rerouting through the coordinator — prepare.rs (all-suppressed-ack,
    route_validation_failure), persist.rs (settle_unready_source_material_event
    NAK, the confirmation ack loop, settle_admission_skips), and the
    bisection/poison-row DLQ paths. This is conceptually an "AND-join of N
    children" sibling to the r6d.11/4as3 CommitFrontier's "FIFO frontier"
    concept, but NOT the same primitive — CommitFrontier assumes strict
    submission order or a checkpoint payload per atom; this needs a simple
    countdown + sticky-worst-outcome, no ordering. Could share the
    TerminalOutcome enum vocabulary from commit_frontier.rs (sinex-4as3,
    landed 2026-07-08) for consistency, but needs its own coordinator type,
    not CommitFrontier itself.

RECOMMENDATION: implement (B) as its own small primitive (e.g.
RawEnvelopeSettlement in jetstream_consumer/), then rewire the ~6 call sites
listed above one at a time, each covered by the AC's four named test
scenarios (valid+rejected siblings, tombstone+confirmation-failure siblings,
not-ready+ready siblings, bisection-isolated poison siblings) before moving
to the next site. Do not attempt all sites in one PR — ack/nak correctness
bugs here are exactly as dangerous as the bug being fixed.
acceptance_criteria: Raw ingress either enforces singleton EventIntent.events or introduces one settlement coordinator per physical JetStream message. No raw message can receive mixed ACK/NAK/DLQ outcomes. A raw message is ACKed only after every admitted child is persisted+confirmed or every failed child is durably DLQed without endangering siblings. DLQ headers/dedupe identify the failed child or explicitly mark the whole envelope. Tests cover valid+rejected siblings, tombstone+confirmation-failure siblings, not-ready+ready siblings, and bisection-isolated poison siblings.
notes:
2026-07-09 follow-up session: added the two still-missing multi-child settlement test
scenarios the bead's own AC requires, via PR https://github.com/Sinity/sinex/pull/2440
(branch test/event-engine/r6d12-multi-child-settlement-coverage, not yet merged).

Added to crate/sinexd/tests/event_engine/jetstream_consumer_test.rs:
- multi_child_intent_settles_tombstone_and_confirmation_failure_siblings: forces exactly
  CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS (3) confirmation-publish failures so the confirmation-failure
  sibling exhausts every retry and is left unsettled per the documented
  left_unacked_for_redelivery contract. Asserts the tombstone sibling never persists, the
  confirmation-failure sibling DOES persist (durability gap is publish-only, post-commit), no
  confirmed event is ever published for it, and the shared raw message stays ack-pending
  rather than acking early because its tombstone sibling settled cleanly.
- multi_child_intent_settles_bisection_isolated_poison_siblings: the existing single-child
  "poison" tests all use anchor_byte=-1, which admission.rs:592 rejects PRE-PERSIST
  (AdmissionRejectionKind::NegativeAnchor) -- none of them actually exercise
  is_isolatable_batch_persistence_failure/bisection, confirming this bead's own gap note was
  correct. This test instead sets ts_orig 5s ahead of the id's own UUIDv7-derived ts_coided:
  inside admission's 1h future_ts_skew tolerance but past the DB's
  "ts_orig <= ts_coided + INTERVAL '1 second'" CHECK constraint -- passes admission, fails
  only at the DB. Asserts the healthy sibling persists, the poison sibling reaches DLQ, and
  the shared message's ack-pending count returns to 0 only once BOTH children report a
  terminal outcome (consumer keeps running).

Verified: xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(multi_child_intent_settles)' -- 4/4 passed (both
pre-existing scenarios + the two new ones), both in isolation and batched together (one
batched run hit a pure DB-template-provisioning timeout under concurrent host load,
unrelated -- isolated + re-batched reruns were clean). xtask check -p sinexd clean.

No production code changed (test-coverage-only, per task scope).

Discovered but NOT fixed (filed as sinex-r6d.13): the pre-existing
jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery test in
events_consumer_integration_test.rs deterministically fails on master --
fail_confirmations(3) exactly exhausts CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS (3), so it always hits a
durability gap instead of the in-process recovery its name claims. Predates PR #2425
(confirmation.rs/run_loop.rs untouched by it) -- orthogonal to RawEnvelopeSettlement, not
fixed here.

All four of the bead's named AC test scenarios (valid+rejected, not-ready+ready,
tombstone+confirmation-failure, bisection-isolated poison) are now covered pending PR #2440
merge. Leaving the bead open for the orchestrating session to review/merge and close.
close_reason: Mechanism (RawEnvelopeSettlement coordinator) merged via PR #2425 (2026-07-09). All four AC-named test scenarios now landed and passing via PR #2440: valid+rejected siblings and not-ready+ready siblings (already existed pre-#2440), tombstone+confirmation-failure siblings and bisection-isolated poison siblings (added by #2440). Verify: xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(multi_child_intent_settles)' -> 4/4 pass. Discovered along the way: a pre-existing, orthogonal test bug (fail_confirmations(3) off-by-one against CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS) filed as sinex-r6d.13, not blocking this closure since it predates and is untouched by PR #2425.

title: Preflight: static LLM cost fuse for the embedding worker
description: Small preflight gate before sinex-5v6 — NOT the full budget-policy automaton (a4w.5). Sets a local hard ceiling, model allowlist, and batch cap so the first embedding run cannot accidentally sweep the whole store uncapped. Token-class awareness (fresh vs cached vs output) can be a stub field here; the real ledger semantics live in a4w.5.
acceptance_criteria: Embedding worker config has a hard max events/materials per run, a model allowlist, a max estimated cost/token ceiling, and a dry-run/estimate command; a fixture or dev dry-run proves crossing the ceiling aborts BEFORE model calls; closing comment records the chosen first-run cap.

title: Preflight: quarantine agent-session content before the first embedding worker run
description: Small preflight decision gating sinex-5v6. The full sinex-4j2.8 ownership decision needs real cost/cache measurements which only 5v6 can produce — so 5v6 must start with agent-session/Polylogue content excluded or quarantined rather than waiting on the full cross-repo policy. Replaces the former hard blocks 5v6<-4j2.8 and 5v6<-a4w.5 (removed 2026-07-07 grooming: they were false full-blockers that deadlocked the LLM plane activation).
design: PROPOSED RULING (2026-07-08, encoded for operator ratification): fail-closed ALLOWLIST, no quarantine lane in v1. First 5v6 activation embeds ONLY event/material families on an explicit allowlist recorded in the worker config; anything not listed is skipped and counted (skip counts visible in the worker report). Agent-session/Polylogue-origin content (polylogue.* sources and any material whose source contract is the agent-session lane) and the ENTIRE reflection lane are not on the allowlist. Rationale: allowlist fails closed when new sources land mid-flight (a denylist silently sweeps them); a quarantine lane is machinery 4j2.8 may obsolete — do not build a lane to hold content we may never embed here. The dry-run fixture (AC) proves selection excludes agent-session content by listing selected families + per-family counts before the first real run. 4j2.8 remains the full ownership decision; if it lands (c) shared effect-ledger, the allowlist simply widens.
acceptance_criteria: A recorded preflight rule states exactly which event/material families 5v6 may embed on first activation; agent-session/Polylogue content is either excluded by filter or routed to a quarantined lane with no default sweep; a fixture/dry-run shows no agent-session content selected; sinex-4j2.8 remains the follow-up full ownership/cost decision once 5v6 produces measurements.
notes: RATIFIED 2026-07-08 (operator, decision-brief batch): fail-closed allowlist rule is FINAL — 5v6 may start against it. Bead stays open for the dry-run fixture proof at first activation.

title: Durable emission receipt: one barrier for source cursors, automaton checkpoints, and invalidation acks
description: EventEmitter::emit is an in-memory mpsc handoff (runtime/stream/handles.rs:364-413) yet three progress markers treat it as a durable commit: (1) adapter-source cursor checkpoints persist after enqueue but before NATS publish — EventBatcher holds events in a plain Vec for up to 100 events/1s (event_transport.rs:296-354); (2) automaton process_batch can durably save the adapter checkpoint BEFORE the caller emits outputs (process.rs:231 record_processed_input; :389-424 save inside process_batch; adapter/mod.rs:517-527 emit after return) — and the bridge KV checkpoint layer (#2358) is INERT for adapter automata (manages_own_checkpoints=true, adapter/mod.rs:479), so restart catch-up resumes from the poisoned checkpoint; (3) invalidation messages are acked at receive (adapter/mod.rs:244-267) before recompute. All verified on master eedc05256 — see .agent/scratch/021-grok-2026-07-07-audit-verification.md. This bead builds ONE shared primitive: emit_batch_durable(..) -> DurableEmissionReceipt where receipt means accepted-and-recoverable-after-crash (DB confirmation or durable spool write — NOT mpsc/NATS-publish). r6d.4, vxu, r6d.7, and w4i then reorder against this single boundary instead of inventing three incompatible notions of durable.
design: Shape (new/sinex_urgent_correctness_systematic_v2.md packet 1, verified): receipt API next to EventEmitter; narrow adapters for the three caller families (source record emission, automaton output emission, invalidation recompute outputs). Receipt semantics: after DB confirm OR durable recovery-spool write; never after mpsc send or unacked NATS publish alone. Killpoint tests: fake durable emitter blocking before receipt — assert no cursor/checkpoint/ack advances. Land the primitive FIRST, then reorder callers (r6d.4, vxu checkpoint reorder, r6d.7, w4i) against it — do not patch call sites individually.
acceptance_criteria: Receipt API exists and returns per-progress-atom, per-event terminal outcomes. Progress-unlocking states are PersistedConfirmed, Suppressed/Deduplicated/Tombstoned with durable evidence, DurableDebt, NoOutputSettled, and SpoolAcceptedLossless only after r6d.5; RawAccepted / mpsc send / EventBatcher acceptance are never sufficient for cursor/checkpoint/ack movement. Direct and NATS backends expose the same receipt state machine (DirectAdmissionFn must return item outcomes, not RuntimeResult<()> — event_transport.rs:24-36 erases suppression/tombstone outcomes today). Progress atoms are caller-defined: SourceRecord, AutomatonInputBatch, InvalidationScope, WindowFlush; reflection/telemetry gets a separate best-effort API that never unlocks progress. Per-source/per-automaton FIFO commit frontiers: receipts may complete out of order but cursor/checkpoint commits only advance over contiguous terminal atoms. Batching/pipelining preserved (no per-event await serialization against the 100/1s batcher and publish semaphores). Producer crash recovery: a request_id ledger lets a restarted producer reconcile in-flight receipts instead of blind re-emit. Tests prove adapter_source, automaton live/historical/timer/shutdown paths, and invalidation delivery handles do not advance before receipt.unlocks_progress(); r6d.9 killpoints cover source, automaton, invalidation, spool, and reflection separation.
notes: 2026-07-09 (orchestrating session): reviewed PR #2445 directly (both automated reviewers hit rate-limit quota, no substantive findings available) — clean, well-tested mechanism-only SettlementRegistry, matches the scoped ask exactly (register/resolve/await_batch, correct concurrent-timeout handling, cleanup invariant documented and tested, 'partial == not a commit permission' rule proven). Merged. Correcting this bead's own prior note: PR #2445 is now MERGED, not left open.

title: Restore exported NixOS VM checks for xtask test vm
description: xtask test vm --category smoke/integration is documented and used as a deployment-shape gate, but current flake.nix sets vmCheckOutputs = { }, so xtask test vm --list shows no exported checks and the smoke gate cannot prove anything. Re-enable exported checks from tests/e2e/nixos-vm/default.nix, or deliberately rewrite the VM verification contract and docs. Acceptance: xtask test vm --list shows at least smoke/basic and replay-smoke for x86_64-linux; xtask test vm --category smoke runs real exported checks instead of an empty catalogue; x9i can then use this as deployment-shape closure evidence.
notes:
2026-07-05T00:50+02:00 progress: focused exported replay-smoke proof passed via `env NIX_CONFIG="max-jobs = 1
cores = 1" nix build -L .#checks.x86_64-linux.sinex-vm-replay-smoke --no-link`. The VM runtime built successfully with the VM-only low-memory release profile; replay archived 8 old rows, finite file-drop replay emitted 2 current fs-watcher events, replacement recording wrote replacement_count=4 old_events=8 new_events=2, replay status reached Completed, and final event consistency saw replay_test_0.txt + replay_test_1.txt. Follow-up classifier change treats non-live derived-parent validation as an isolatable row failure instead of poisoning replay batches. Full `xtask test vm --category smoke` is running as job 2001757 / invocation 2005721 and discovered 2 exported smoke checks, proving the empty-catalog regression is gone.
close_reason: Implemented and verified. VM checks are exported from the flake again, `xtask test vm --list` enumerates real smoke/integration/performance/chaos/production-shape checks, and `xtask test vm --category smoke` ran real checks instead of an empty catalog. Verification: job 2001757 / invocation 2005721 passed 2/2 VM tests (`basic` and `replay-smoke`); focused direct proof `nix build -L .#checks.x86_64-linux.sinex-vm-replay-smoke --no-link` also passed with replay reaching Completed and final fs-watcher consistency over replay_test_0.txt + replay_test_1.txt.

title: xtask run timeout can leave cargo build subprocess alive
description: A bounded live smoke on 2026-07-04 used timeout --signal=INT around xtask run module interval-lift --logs. The timeout killed the xtask wrapper while cargo build -p sinexd and rustc remained alive as orphaned processes (PIDs 3907410/3907414) until manually TERM'd. This makes bounded runtime smoke tests unsafe and undermines the managed child-process contract before the runtime binary even starts. Investigate signal/process-group handling for xtask run's build phase and ensure timeout/INT/TERM tears down cargo/rustc descendants.
notes: 2026-07-04T11:14:06+02:00 implemented signal cleanup for xtask managed child process groups. Root cause: ProcessBuilder children were registered and normal completion/internal xtask timeout reaped them, but external SIGINT/SIGTERM could bypass run_cli's cleanup path, matching the interval-lift smoke where timeout left cargo/rustc alive. Change: install_registered_process_group_signal_cleanup registers SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers in run_cli; on signal it terminates registered groups then exits with shell-standard 130/143. Added test_signal_cleanup_terminates_registered_process_groups to exercise the exact cleanup body against a real background descendant. Verification: xtask test -p xtask --lib -E 'test(test_signal_cleanup_terminates_registered_process_groups) | test(test_terminate_registered_process_groups_handles_exited_group_leader)' --no-reuse --bg job 2001580 passed; xtask check -p xtask --bg final-source job 2001582 passed; git diff --check passed. Bounded external smoke: timeout --signal=INT 6s xtask run module interval-lift --logs returned timeout_status=124 and left no new cargo/rustc/sinexd/xtask processes after 2s, but did not reach a runtime banner, so treat it as process-lifecycle smoke rather than full d9v proof.
close_reason: Merged PR #2326 as e8b6fc405. xtask run_cli now installs SIGINT/SIGTERM cleanup for registered managed child process groups; focused regression job 2001580, final xtask check job 2001582, git diff --check, and pre-push changed-strict all passed. Bounded timeout smoke left no cargo/rustc/sinexd/xtask residue after interrupt; full live runtime composition remains tracked by sinex-d9v.

title: Dev runtime sinexd modules exhaust checkout Postgres clients
description: During sinex-8y8 systemd interval-lift proof on 2026-07-04, running xtask run module interval-lift --bg plus xtask run core --bg opened dozens of idle checkout-local Postgres backends; a subsequent xtask run module system-source --bg failed during schema apply with 'sorry, too many clients already'. ps showed two sinexd processes and many idle sinity/sinex_dev sessions. This blocks live proof composition and makes module-level runtime dogfooding brittle. Investigate per-module pool sizing/sharing and xtask run orchestration so a small proof stack can run core + one source + one automaton without exhausting max_connections.
notes: 2026-07-04 focused runtime selection slice: second live smoke proved the dev stack no longer exhausts checkout Postgres clients. Code changes: xtask core run now sets SINEX_AUTOMATA_ENABLED='' and SINEX_SOURCE_BINDINGS_PATH='' so core is event-engine/API only; xtask automaton runs set SINEX_AUTOMATA_ENABLED=<name>, SINEX_EVENT_ENGINE_ENABLED=false, SINEX_API_ENABLED=false, SINEX_SOURCE_BINDINGS_PATH='' so focused automata do not construct event-engine/API configs or host sources; sinexd serve now loads EventEngineConfig/GatewayConfig only when their component is enabled; supervisor distinguishes unset SINEX_AUTOMATA_ENABLED (default all) from empty (disable). Verification: xtask test -p xtask --lib -E runtime-selection contract tests job 2001600 passed; xtask check -p xtask job 2001601 passed; xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E test(automata_enabled_arg_distinguishes_unset_from_empty) job 2001605 passed; xtask check -p sinexd job 2001606 passed; git diff --check passed. Live proof: baseline Postgres sessions TOTAL=16; core+system-source+interval-lift stack TOTAL=26, down from earlier 58; post-stop TOTAL=16. Core job 2001607 env had SINEX_AUTOMATA_ENABLED='' and logs 'no automata enabled' plus 'sinexd running automata=0 source_bindings=0'. Interval-lift job 2001609 env had SINEX_EVENT_ENGINE_ENABLED=false/SINEX_API_ENABLED=false/SINEX_AUTOMATA_ENABLED=interval-lift and logs one automaton with no event_engine::config validation and no ingestion startup. All runtime jobs stopped; xtask jobs active returned 0.
close_reason: Merged PR #2328 as 09e2544ac. Runtime component selection now keeps dev core as event-engine/API only and focused automaton runs as one automaton with event-engine/API/source hosting disabled. Verification: xtask runtime-selection contract tests job 2001600 passed; xtask check -p xtask job 2001601 passed; supervisor empty-vs-unset test job 2001605 passed; xtask check -p sinexd job 2001606 passed; pre-push changed-strict passed for sinexd and xtask. Live proof: baseline Postgres sessions TOTAL=16; core+system-source+interval-lift TOTAL=26, down from earlier 58; post-stop TOTAL=16; core job 2001607 logged automata=0/source_bindings=0; interval-lift job 2001609 logged exactly one automaton and no event-engine config/ingestion startup.

title: Extract inline Rust test modules into sibling files
description: Sinex still has hundreds of true inline #[cfg(test)] mod tests blocks inside production Rust source files, making source files hard to read and obscuring production/test attribution. Programmatically split true inline test modules into sibling src/foo_test.rs-style files where visibility permits, preserving semantics by leaving only #[cfg(test)] mod foo_test; declarations in production files. Do this in large coherent batches rather than one manual file at a time; do not widen production APIs just to externalize private white-box tests.
close_reason: Completed current true inline-test extraction. Current brace-aware audit reports inline_files=0 after moving crate/sinexctl/src/mcp.rs tests into crate/sinexctl/src/mcp_test.rs with a #[path] gated module declaration. xtask/src/sandbox/orchestrator.rs was already split; its apparent match was a doc-comment false positive plus #[path] module declaration. Verification: job 2001487 passed xtask test -p sinexctl --lib -E 'test(mcp)' with 90 passed; job 2001488 passed xtask check -p sinexctl.

title: xtask jobs can remain running after nextest success
description: Job 2001482 completed nextest successfully and imported test metadata, but xtask jobs status continued reporting it as running while the root xtask process PID 3198039 sat in D-state. This makes agents wait indefinitely after proof is already available and weakens job observability. Investigate the post-nextest finalization path, IO waits, history/JUnit import, and job status transition so successful commands always publish terminal state promptly or expose the blocking syscall/resource.
close_reason: Completed by xtask job query/status fix: running jobs with a terminal exit_code marker now synthesize completed/failed/cancelled status even while the tracked PID still exists, and active job listings omit those terminal-marker jobs. Regression proof job 2001484 passed query-manager tests; package check job 2001485 passed xtask check -p xtask.

title: xtask test filters should avoid unrelated integration-bin compilation
description: A focused handler test run (xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(source_status)') compiled and linked unrelated sinexd integration binaries such as material_assembler_test, peaking around 19 GiB process-tree memory and high IO pressure before cancellation as job 2001474. The correct operator intent for src sibling/unit tests is closer to --lib, but xtask does not warn or infer this when the filter maps only to library tests. This directly harms devloop velocity and host responsiveness. Implement target inference or a warning/plan mode that avoids compiling unrelated integration targets for name-filtered library tests, while preserving explicit --test and broad package test semantics.
notes: 2026-07-04 implementation slice: fixed xtask background test target inference so the spawned command carries the same inferred target shape as the proof planner. Explicit package filters now use package-scoped lib inference; sibling src/*_test.rs stems are recognized as nextest name fragments; background invocations append inferred --lib/--test flags. Live proof: xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(source_status)' --dry-run --json now reports lib=true, db_pool_size=2, runtime_binary_requirements=[], test_binaries=[]. Focused tests in job 2001482 passed all 3 regression tests but exposed a separate stuck-success xtask job finalization bug, tracked as sinex-5zx; job was cancelled after nextest output showed 3 passed. Package check job 2001483 passed xtask check -p xtask.
close_reason: Completed by PR #2308 / merge commit 64fe04159. Background xtask test invocations now carry inferred --lib/--test target flags; dry-run proof for source_status reports lib=true and runtime_binary_requirements=[]; xtask check -p xtask passed as job 2001483. Follow-up stuck-success finalization bug tracked as sinex-5zx.

title: Judgment seat: interactive curation triage surface (the adjudication workflow)
description: 7z0 owns the judgment bottleneck by operator ruling: adjudication is the only path to truth and has NO workflow. The machinery exists end-to-end (curation.proposal/judgment/finalized events, authority.rs Proposal/Judgment with apply(), FinalizerRegistration::human_required) but there is no surface where pending proposals are seen, compared, and judged — so nothing heuristic can ever become adjudicated, and the promotion gate (evidence-tier, sinex-8cr) has no operator. Evidence: MCP exposes sinex_curation_proposals read-only; no triage command exists in sinexctl.
design: sinexctl triage (and/or a TUI panel + MCP write tool): list pending CurationProposals (grouped by proposal_kind, ranked by confidence x volume), render candidate payload + evidence (evidence_event_ids via provenance join), record CurationJudgmentPayload with decision accept/reject/modify/defer. Batch judgment for homogeneous proposals (accept-all-above-threshold with explicit operator confirmation — a judgment per proposal, never a silent bulk flip). Agent-recorded judgments follow the actor-kind decision made in sinex-0vx. PITFALL: judgments are events (durable, replayable) — the surface writes through the normal event pipeline, no side-channel DB writes. PITFALL: render tier + confidence + rationale; never auto-accept on confidence (confidence is not authority — authority.rs doctrine).
acceptance_criteria: A pending proposal can be listed, inspected with its evidence, and judged from sinexctl; the judgment lands as a curation.judgment event; a finalizer emits the canonical output with derived provenance pointing at proposal+judgment.
