2026-07-09 session (6th pass): investigated confirmed-consumer-behind-retention properly
(previously only grepped; this pass traced the actual scan->subscribe code path) and found a
DIFFERENT, narrower issue than the bead's own framing implied — worth recording precisely
rather than either overclaiming a bug or dropping the thread.

CONFIRMED (not a concern): the bead's own framing ("confirmed-consumer falls behind, stream
retention evicts messages before it reads them") is a NON-ISSUE by design. The automaton
continuous-mode entry point (automaton_runtime.rs run_automaton_continuous_mode ->
run_automaton_event_bridge, ~line 246-266) always does a DB-backed historical scan
(self.module.scan(catchup_from, TimeHorizon::Historical{end_time: Timestamp::now()}, ...))
BEFORE subscribing the live JetStream consumer with DeliverPolicy::New
(automaton_consumer_config, automaton_runtime.rs:524). Durability comes entirely from
Postgres (the archive), never from the confirmed-events STREAM's retained messages — matches
the architecture doctrine "Postgres is the archive, [confirmed-events] is a bounded delivery
bus" already documented in this repo's CLAUDE.md.

REFINED HYPOTHESIS (narrow, not yet fully proven, needs its own investigation session): a
scan-snapshot-vs-consumer-creation race. `Timestamp::now()` for the historical scan's
end_time is captured BEFORE the (potentially slow) DB scan query runs; the live JetStream
consumer's actual DeliverPolicy::New cutoff is only established when create_or_get_consumer
(jetstream_consumer.rs:209-228) -> ensure_pull_consumer (stream/kernel.rs:68+) actually
creates it on the server, which happens AFTER the scan completes (scan -> construct consumer
struct -> tokio::spawn -> consumer.run() -> run_inner() -> create_or_get_consumer). An event
confirmed/published in that window would be missed by both paths: after the scan's end_time
snapshot, before the live consumer's actual creation moment.

MITIGATING NUANCE (found while verifying, narrows this a lot): ensure_pull_consumer first
checks `stream.consumer_info(&spec.durable_name)` — this is a DURABLE, NAMED consumer that
persists its own delivery position on the JetStream SERVER across process restarts. If the
consumer ALREADY EXISTS (the ordinary automaton-restart-after-crash case, which is what this
whole r6d.9/r6d.11/vxu chain actually cares about), DeliverPolicy::New only mattered at that
consumer's ORIGINAL creation, long before this specific restart's scan ran — the existing
consumer just resumes from its own tracked position regardless of scan timing, closing the
gap for restarts. The race, if real, would ONLY apply to a genuinely FRESH automaton's very
first-ever startup (durable consumer doesn't exist yet) — a materially narrower, less
operationally significant window than "any restart."

NOT resolved: whether the fresh-deployment window is actually exploitable in practice (exact
NATS consumer-creation timing vs scan duration under real load), and whether it's worth a
fix (e.g., create/ensure the durable consumer with DeliverPolicy::New BEFORE running the
historical scan, so its cutoff is pinned earlier, then let the scan's end_time snapshot come
after) or is acceptable given the narrow blast radius (fresh-deploy-only, and even then only
a race measured in scan-duration + spawn latency, likely milliseconds).

Given the narrow scope and remaining uncertainty, not filing this as a new bead yet — recording
here so a future investigation of this bead's 4th named scenario starts from this evidence
instead of re-deriving it, and can decide whether it's worth a dedicated bead once the
fresh-deploy timing question is actually measured.
2026-07-09 session (final pass): built the FOURTH crash-window scenario for real —
spool-rename-before-dir-fsync — using genuine VM-level crash tooling (QEMU power-cut via
NixOS testers.nixosTest's machine.crash(), not a process-level fail point). Corrects an
earlier claim in this same session that VM-level crash-consistency tooling was "not
available/built" — that was wrong; sinex already has a working nixosTest VM harness
(tests/e2e/nixos-vm/) and the NixOS test-driver's machine.crash() ("simulate a sudden power
failure... tell the VM to exit immediately" via QMP `quit`) is exactly the mechanism needed
to drop the guest kernel's page cache, which a process-level exit() cannot do.

RESULT: CONFIRMED, with reproducible evidence — ABSENT. A crash between
tokio::fs::rename(&temp_path, recovery_spool_path) and Self::fsync_dir(parent_dir) in
store_recovery_spool_events_at_path (event_transport.rs) LOSES the renamed spool file on
reboot, even though the rename() call itself had already returned successfully. This is the
first proof in this codebase of a genuine KERNEL-level (not just in-process-ordering) crash
window, and it validates that fsync_dir's presence in the current code IS load-bearing —
removing or reordering it would silently reopen this exact data-loss window.

Built:
- crate/sinexd/src/runtime/event_transport.rs: SINEX_TEST_SPOOL_RENAME_MARKER fail point —
  after rename() succeeds, if the env var is set, writes a marker file then blocks forever
  (std::future::pending), letting an external VM test driver crash the whole machine at
  exactly that point. cfg(any(test, feature="testing")), zero effect otherwise. Also exposed
  EventBatcher::test_only_write_recovery_spool (feature="testing" only) so a standalone
  harness binary can exercise the real production write path without needing the full
  service + a forced NATS-publish failure to reach it.
- crate/sinexd/src/bin/spool_rename_crash_harness.rs: ~50-line standalone binary
  (required-features = ["testing"], never built for production) that constructs one
  synthetic event and calls the above.
- flake.nix: sinexVmRuntime (the VM-ONLY package derivation, never services.sinex.enable's
  production sinexRuntime) now builds with --features sinexd/testing. testing is an empty,
  cfg-only feature (no new deps), so this shares cargoArtifacts safely and has zero effect on
  any existing VM scenario's behavior.
- tests/e2e/nixos-vm/test-scenarios/chaos-spool-rename-durability.nix: new nixosTest —
  virtualisation.emptyDiskImages (a REAL persistent qcow2-backed disk, not the default tmpfs
  root — load-bearing, since tmpfs has no fsync-vs-page-cache distinction at all) formatted
  ext4, mounts it, launches the harness armed via the marker env var, waits for the marker,
  calls machine.crash(), restarts the SAME machine (reusing the same persistent disk image —
  confirmed via emptyDiskImages' qemu-img create-if-missing startup script), fscks, remounts,
  and checks file presence.
- xtask/src/commands/vm.rs: registered as chaos-spool-rename-durability in CHAOS_TESTS.

Iteration notes (for anyone extending this pattern): (1) the VM's root filesystem is
tmpfs-backed and resets completely across crash()+start() — only the emptyDiskImages-backed
disk persists; the mount point directory must be recreated post-restart. (2) nixosTest's
machine.succeed() runs commands under `set -euo pipefail`, so `cmd; true` does NOT swallow
cmd's failure (set -e aborts before reaching `true`) — use `cmd || true` instead. (3) a
successful nix build caches the WHOLE VM test result; rerunning identical script content
returns the cached PASS instantly without re-executing the VM — use `nix log <result-path>`
to retrieve the full prior run's test-driver output (including Python print() calls) rather
than assuming a rerun is needed.

Remaining named r6d.9 scenarios: invalidation-ack-before-recompute has a fail point landed

semantic question); confirmed-consumer-behind-retention was found to be a non-issue by design.
That leaves the fsync_dir scenario (this one) as the SECOND fully-proven scenario alongside

title: Ack invalidations only after recompute and checkpoint
description:
Checkpoint-loss matrix follow-up from sinex-r6d.1. The invalidation consumer can ack before recompute/checkpoint. A crash in that window can lose the invalidation and leave derived state stale until another invalidation happens.

## Steps to Reproduce
Crash-window scenario (r6d.9 injection point ack-before-recompute): publish a scope invalidation; recv_invalidation acks on receipt (runtime/automaton/adapter/mod.rs:254, re-verified 2026-07-06); SIGKILL during debounce/recompute; restart; the invalidation is consumed-and-lost, derived state stays stale.
design: From checkpoint-loss packet .agent/scratch/research/2026-07-03-sinex-r6d-1-checkpoint-loss-window.md. Affected paths: crate/sinexd/src/runtime/automaton/adapter/mod.rs recv_invalidation acks immediately; crate/sinexd/src/runtime/automaton/adapter/run.rs buffers/debounces; crate/sinexd/src/runtime/automaton/adapter/invalidate.rs prepares/recomputes/applies/checkpoints. Current risk: scan-driven invalidation consumer can ack a JetStream invalidation before debounce, recompute, archive, output emission, and checkpoint. A crash in that window loses the invalidation. Implementation target: remove this legacy scan-driven direct path if deployed event-bridge/operation recovery supersedes it, or move ack after successful recompute and durable checkpoint/pending-invalidation persistence. Also update crate/sinex-primitives/docs/transport.md so invalidation durability claims match the code.
acceptance_criteria: Invalidation receive returns a delivery handle and does not ack at receive. Debounce coalesces payloads but retains every delivery handle until durable settlement. Ack occurs only after an InvalidationSettlementReceipt covering: durable recompute output receipt, archive/replacement DB operation settlement, and checkpoint or durable pending-invalidation state. Malformed payloads create durable invalidation debt before ack/term; transient failures NAK/redeliver. The output-before-archive ordering (invalidate.rs:342-353 chose it deliberately) keeps a duplicate/stale window on redelivery: recompute outputs need operation-scoped dedup or durable pending operation ids.
notes:
2026-07-06 re-verified on master 91c6e3735: recv_invalidation still acks immediately on receipt (crate/sinexd/src/runtime/automaton/adapter/mod.rs:254), before debounce/recompute/apply/checkpoint in adapter/run.rs + invalidate.rs. Claim current; bead executable as written.
2026-07-07 audit integration: verified exact on master — recv_invalidation copies payload and acks at receive (adapter/mod.rs:244-267), debounce/recompute/archive-finalize/checkpoint all after ack. Fix shape: return a delivery object (payload + msg handle); debounce may coalesce scopes but retains settlement handles; ack each handle only after durable recompute settlement via the r6d.11 receipt; malformed payload → durable invalidation-debt then term; transient failure → NAK/redeliver.
2026-07-07 red team: recv_invalidation must return {payload, delivery_handle} — the current copy-payload-and-ack-immediately shape (adapter/mod.rs:244-267) makes any later settlement receipt unable to ack the original message.
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): execution packet = .agent/scratch/new/sinex_urgent_correctness_systematic_v2.md section 5 (invalidation ack delay); red-team report = new-gpt-pro/01-batch-a-receipt-red-team.report.md. Re-verify line anchors against master on claim.
2026-07-08 session: not started. Hard-blocked on sinex-r6d.11 per this bead's own DEPENDS ON. The recv_invalidation -> {payload, delivery_handle} return-shape change (adapter/mod.rs:244-267) is well-scoped and could theoretically start independently of the receipt primitive itself, but the AC's ack-only-after-InvalidationSettlementReceipt requirement needs r6d.11's receipt states to exist first — implementing the handle-return shape alone without the receipt to gate the eventual ack would be half-finished work that still acks at the wrong point.
2026-07-09 session: landed the fail-point injection mechanism (mirroring sinex-r6d.9's
checkpoint-before-output pattern) AND, while building the crash+restart test around it,
found a genuinely significant architectural finding that reframes this bead's severity —
recording both.

LANDED (real, compiling, zero production behavior change): recv_invalidation
(adapter/mod.rs) now takes an Option<&Arc<AtomicBool>> fail-point parameter, threaded through
its run.rs call site via a new always-present AutomatonRuntime::invalidation_ack_fail_point()
accessor (cfg-gated field, None unconditionally in production builds — same pattern as
fail_point_after_checkpoint). Triggers std::process::exit(98) immediately after
msg.ack().await succeeds, before the payload returns to the caller for debounce/recompute.

FINDING (verified via async-nats 0.47.0 source, not assumption): the specific consumer this
bead's window applies to — run_continuous's invalidation subscription (adapter/run.rs:54-108)
— is an EPHEMERAL push consumer (no durable_name) with `..Default::default()` for the rest of
its config. Checked async-nats's own defaults: AckPolicy::default() == Explicit (confirms the
explicit .ack() call is meaningful, not a no-op) and, critically, DeliverPolicy::default() ==
All ("receive the oldest messages still present in the system"). Ephemeral consumers get a
server-generated name each creation (stream.create_consumer with no explicit name/durable_name
set) — each restart of run_continuous therefore creates a GENUINELY NEW consumer with NO
inherited ack/delivery state from any previous (crashed) instance. Combined with the
invalidation stream's own retention (bootstrap.rs:119-129: RetentionPolicy::Limits, max_age
24h — acks do NOT cause early removal under Limits policy), this means: an invalidation acked
by a crashed process, whose recompute never ran, is NATURALLY RE-DELIVERED to any restart's
fresh consumer as long as that restart happens within the stream's 24h retention window —
which covers essentially every realistic crash-restart scenario (seconds to minutes, not
hours). The genuinely lossy case is narrower than this bead's description implies: an
automaton that crashes and does NOT restart for >24 hours.

This does NOT mean r6d.7 is a non-issue (unlike the confirmed-consumer-retention finding,
which WAS a non-issue by design) — the ack-before-recompute window is real, and the AC's other
requirements (delivery handle returned instead of ack-at-receive, InvalidationSettlementReceipt
gating the eventual ack, durable invalidation debt for malformed payloads) are still valid
correctness improvements independent of the self-healing-on-restart property. But it DOES mean
the "silent, permanent loss" framing in this bead's DESCRIPTION overstates the risk for
run_continuous's specific consumer shape, and any r6d.9 killpoint scenario for this window
should assert "redelivered and correctly reprocessed on restart within retention" as the
CURRENT (self-healing) behavior, with a SEPARATE, narrower scenario for "no restart within 24h
-> genuinely lost" if that's still considered worth proving.

NOT built: the actual crash+restart test (unlike sinex-r6d.9's checkpoint scenario, which has
one). Held back because designing a CORRECT assertion requires resolving one more NATS-specific
uncertainty I could not fully verify from source alone: whether `deliver_group` (queue-group)
sharing across ephemeral consumer instances has any cross-instance state effects I'm not
accounting for. Recommend a future session verify this empirically (spin up two ephemeral
consumers with the same deliver_group against a live NATS server, confirm independent delivery
tracking) before trusting the "self-heals on restart" conclusion enough to close this out.
2026-07-09 session (empirical confirmation): built the actual crash+restart test the prior
note flagged as blocked on "one NATS deliver_group semantic question" — that question is now
resolved empirically, not just from reading async-nats source.

New test: r6d9_invalidation_ack_fail_point_fires (adapter_test.rs), same cross-process shape
as sinex-r6d.9's checkpoint scenario (one #[sinex_test] function serving both outer-harness
and inner-scenario roles via env-var-gated re-invocation, sharing the parent's NATS server by
explicit URL). Child: publishes nothing itself, just consumes+acks a pre-published
DerivedScopeInvalidation via a real ephemeral push consumer against the real
SINEX_RAW_EVENTS_DERIVED_INVALIDATIONS stream, hits the SINEX_TEST_SPOOL... no — the
recv_invalidation fail point (landed PR #2436) — fires exit(98) right after the ack. Parent:
after confirming exit(98), creates a FRESH ephemeral push consumer against the SAME stream
(simulating run_continuous resubscribing after a real restart) and polls for the same
invalidation payload with a 10s timeout.

RESULT: CONFIRMED — self-heals. The fresh consumer sees the redelivered invalidation every
time (4/4 consecutive runs, ~0.6s each, no flakiness). This empirically proves the theoretical
conclusion from the prior session's async-nats source review: ephemeral consumers get no
inherited ack/delivery state across instances, and DeliverPolicy::All (the config default)
delivers everything still present in the stream, so a restart's fresh subscription correctly
recovers an invalidation that a crashed instance had acked-but-not-recomputed.

This does NOT close sinex-r6d.7's remaining AC (delivery handle instead of ack-at-receive,
receipt-gated ack, durable debt for malformed payloads — those are still valid, independent
correctness improvements), but the "silent, permanent loss" framing in the bead description is
now DISPROVEN with direct evidence, not just narrowed by theory. sinex-r6d.9 now has this as
its THIRD scenario with real evidence (2 proving genuine data loss — checkpoint-before-output,
spool-rename-before-fsync; 1 proving self-healing rather than loss — this one), alongside the
4th (confirmed-consumer-behind-retention) already resolved as a non-issue by design. All 4
named r6d.9 scenarios now have a definitive, evidence-backed answer.

title: Make automaton confirmed-event catch-up provably lossless
description: Checkpoint-loss matrix follow-up from sinex-r6d.1. Confirmed-events delivery currently depends on a Limits/DiscardOld stream with comments noting lagging consumers can lose tail unless DB catch-up repairs the gap. Either switch retention/consumer semantics or add per-automaton DB catch-up that detects retention gaps from checkpoint to current.
design: From checkpoint-loss packet .agent/scratch/research/2026-07-03-sinex-r6d-1-checkpoint-loss-window.md. Affected paths: crate/sinexd/src/event_engine/jetstream_consumer/bootstrap.rs confirmed-events stream config; crate/sinexd/src/runtime/jetstream_consumer.rs durable pull consumer creation and handle_confirmed_message; crate/sinexd/src/runtime/stream/runner/automaton_runtime.rs run_automaton_event_bridge and startup DB historical scan; crate/sinexd/src/automata/registry.rs. Current risk: confirmed-events stream uses Limits/DiscardOld, while new/recreated consumers can use DeliverPolicy::New and the DB catch-up proof is not audited across first start, existing checkpoint, retention gap, and all 14 automata. Implementation target: either change retention semantics so messages cannot discard before durable automaton ack, or keep Limits/DiscardOld and make every automaton detect sequence gaps/consumer lag from durable checkpoint and run DB catch-up to current before acknowledging newer live events.
acceptance_criteria: Each registered automaton has a proven recovery path from its durable checkpoint to current even if confirmed-events retention drops messages. A test simulates lag/retention gap and proves no parent event is skipped or the runtime fails loudly with a repairable status.
notes:
2026-07-04 catch-up proof slices: PR #2299 made the generic automaton confirmed-event bridge require supports_historical and run DB historical catch-up even from Checkpoint::None before subscribing with DeliverPolicy::New; focused bridge tests passed as job 2001447 and sinexd check passed as job 2001448. Follow-up branch feature/runtime/automata-bridge-contract adds an AutomatonSpec contract probe derived from each concrete registered runtime type and a registry invariant test proving every registered automaton is bridge-backed, continuous, and supports historical catch-up. Verification after final assertion tweak: job 2001451 passed CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_TEST_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(registered_automata)'; job 2001452 passed CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask check -p sinexd.

2026-07-04 widened checkpoint-start closure: feature/runtime/automaton-lossless-catchup handles r6d.6 + vxu together under greedy-batch policy because both remaining AC are the same generic automaton confirmed-event bridge proof family. Bridge-managed automata now load the saved bridge checkpoint before DB historical catch-up and before subscribing to the live confirmed-event tail. Regression automaton_bridge_runs_historical_catchup_from_saved_bridge_checkpoint proves a preloaded bridge checkpoint is passed to the historical scan instead of Checkpoint::None. Verification jobs: 2001684 passed xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E 'test(automaton_bridge)' --no-reuse; 2001685 passed xtask check -p sinexd. Artifact: .agent/demos/sinex/automaton-lossless-catchup-20260704T1356Z.
close_reason: Completed generic automaton confirmed-event catch-up recovery path. Bridge-managed automata now load the saved bridge checkpoint before historical catch-up and before subscribing to DeliverPolicy::New. Regression automaton_bridge_runs_historical_catchup_from_saved_bridge_checkpoint proves a preloaded bridge checkpoint is passed to the historical scan instead of Checkpoint::None. Existing registry invariant proves registered automata are bridge-repairable. Verification: job 2001684 passed xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E 'test(automaton_bridge)' --no-reuse; job 2001685 passed xtask check -p sinexd. Artifact: .agent/demos/sinex/automaton-lossless-catchup-20260704T1356Z.

title: Remove recovery-spool discard cap or make overflow durable
description:
Checkpoint-loss matrix follow-up from sinex-r6d.1. The recovery spool has a bounded remaining-lines path that can permanently discard entries after sustained publish failure or malformed backlog. Replace the discard cap with a bounded but lossless quarantine/segmented spool/operator backlog so overflow is observable and replayable.

## Steps to Reproduce
Fill the recovery spool with >1000 unpublishable lines (sustained NATS outage or malformed backlog); restart transport; recover_recovery_spool_events (event_transport.rs:375 MAX_REMAINING_LINES=1_000) keeps 1000 lines and permanently discards the overflow.
design: From checkpoint-loss packet .agent/scratch/research/2026-07-03-sinex-r6d-1-checkpoint-loss-window.md. Affected path: crate/sinexd/src/runtime/event_transport.rs, especially EventBatcher::recover_recovery_spool_events and store_recovery_spool_events_at_path. Current risk: recover_recovery_spool_events preserves only MAX_REMAINING_LINES=1000 malformed/still-unpublished lines and discards overflow permanently. Implementation target: remove the discard cap as a loss mechanism. If bounded disk behavior is required, overflow must move to a durable quarantine/backlog file with operator-visible accounting. Malformed lines are preserved verbatim with metadata sufficient to inspect/retry/delete by explicit operator action; no automatic discard.
acceptance_criteria: No code path permanently drops recovery-spool entries because a line-count cap is exceeded. Overflow moves to a durable quarantine/backlog with operator-visible accounting. Tests cover cap overflow and malformed-entry handling without silent loss.
notes:
2026-07-03 codebase-audit note: crate/sinexd/src/runtime/event_transport.rs:~538 carries a stale/misleading comment ('The original code had retry logic here... For now, we'll attempt to send once') that reads as a durability gap. It is NOT — send_batch routes failed batches to the recovery spool (store_recovery_spool_events, same file ~583) which is exactly the mechanism this bead hardens. Fix the comment while here so future readers don't mistake it for silent loss; the actual bounded-discard risk is the spool cap this bead addresses, not the send path.
2026-07-07 audit integration: verified on master — MAX_REMAINING_LINES=1000 (event_transport.rs:375); malformed lines over cap discarded (:392-403), publish-failure lines over cap discarded (:448+), both logged 'permanently lost'. Also: temp-file rewrite fsyncs the file (:668-670, :703-705) but never the parent dir after rename. Fix shape: streaming rewrite (bounded memory, zero discard), malformed-line quarantine file (original bytes + line no + error + ts), terminal classification writes durable debt before removal, parent-dir fsync (or documented platform caveat + test hook), lost_total becomes zero-or-fatal.
2026-07-07 red team adds: spool writes fsync the temp file then rename but never fsync the parent directory (event_transport.rs:635-670) — rename can be lost on crash. r6d.11 now depends on this bead: SpoolAccepted may not unlock progress until the spool is non-discarding AND parent-dir durable.
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): execution packet = .agent/scratch/new/sinex_urgent_correctness_systematic_v2.md section 2 (spool lossless); killpoint rows in new-gpt-pro/01-batch-a-receipt-red-team.report.md section C. Re-verify line anchors against master on claim.
2026-07-09: sinex-r6d.9's VM crash-window harness now provides direct, reproducible proof
that the fsync_dir() call this bead added is load-bearing, not defensive/theoretical — a real
QEMU power-cut between rename() and fsync_dir() loses the renamed spool file on reboot
(confirmed ABSENT via tests/e2e/nixos-vm/test-scenarios/chaos-spool-rename-durability.nix).
No code change needed here; this is closing-evidence for a design decision already shipped.
close_reason: Merged sinex/#2419: recover_recovery_spool_events rewritten as a streaming pass — malformed lines move to a durable quarantine file (original bytes + line no + error + ts) instead of a capped-and-discarded list; publish-failed-but-valid lines stream into the rewritten spool incrementally (bounded memory, no unbounded Vec buffering). Added fsync_dir() after every recovery-spool rename (both replay-rewrite and the original write path) closing the red-team-flagged parent-dir-fsync gap. Fixed the stale misleading comment on send_batch. Verify: xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E 'test(recovery_spool)' -> 6 passed, including a new test seeding 1,100 malformed lines past the old 1,000-line cap and asserting zero discard.

title: Make source emit durable before cursor checkpoint
description:
Checkpoint-loss matrix follow-up from sinex-r6d.1. Adapter-backed live/continuous sources can accept an event into the in-memory EventEmitter/EventBatcher, advance their cursor, and then crash before durable NATS publish or spool. Gate cursor/checkpoint advancement on durable transport acceptance, or introduce an explicit durable handoff confirmation for cursor-bearing sources.

## Steps to Reproduce
Crash-window scenario (r6d.9 injection point after-emit-before-durable): run an adapter-backed continuous source; have EventEmitter accept a batch into the in-memory batcher; SIGKILL sinexd before the batcher flushes to NATS/spool but after persist_stream_checkpoint_if_due advanced the cursor; restart; the accepted events are gone and the cursor is past them.
design: From checkpoint-loss packet .agent/scratch/research/2026-07-03-sinex-r6d-1-checkpoint-loss-window.md. Affected path: crate/sinexd/src/runtime/parser/adapter_source.rs around AdapterBackedSource::drain_adapter, prepare_pending_adapter_record, materialize_adapter_batch, and persist_stream_checkpoint_if_due, plus runtime event transport boundary in crate/sinexd/src/runtime/event_transport.rs. Current risk: EventEmitter::emit accepts into the runtime channel/batcher, while cursor advancement and checkpoint persistence can happen before NATS/direct admission/spool durability. Implementation target: introduce an explicit durable handoff result for cursor-bearing sources and allow cursor advance only after NATS publish ack, direct admission commit, or failed publish durably fsynced into spool/quarantine. The test must be able to pause after emitter acceptance and before durable handoff and prove the cursor has not advanced.
acceptance_criteria: For cursor-bearing sources, one source record is the progress atom: material anchor durability + all converted intents + event/debt/no-op settlement must be terminal before parser checkpoint/cursor merge. Failed records halt the per-source commit frontier or create durable debt; later cursor merges cannot skip them (merge_cursor_update overlay at adapter_source.rs:2123-2155 must respect the frontier). Suppressed/tombstoned terminal receipts advance the cursor; RawAccepted does not. DeferredSourceMaterial is not a progress-unlocking terminal state unless a durable pending record exists.
notes:
2026-07-07 audit integration: mechanism CORRECTED vs the external audit — on master the cursor does NOT persist before parse (adapter_source.rs:1493-1505 advances only on record_processed). The real windows: (a) cursor checkpoint persists after mpsc enqueue but before NATS publish (EventBatcher in-memory Vec, event_transport.rs:296-354) — crash loses enqueued events past a persisted cursor; (b) a FAILED record ('parse_record error — skipping' :1441-1451, 'emit failed' :1469-1490) is permanently skipped when a LATER record in the same drain succeeds and merges a newer cursor (merge_cursor_update :2123-2154) — the ':1503 can be retried' log is misleading unless the failure is at drain tail. Fix against the r6d.11 receipt boundary: one progress commit point per record, after durable material + durable event/debt acceptance; failed records leave durable debt or halt cursor merges past them.
2026-07-07 red team: convert ALL intents for a record before submitting; submit the record as one ProgressAtom::SourceRecord; commit cursor only when every item is terminal. Materialization durability is part of source progress (append_many_with_anchors at adapter_source.rs:1034-1098 happens before parse; the event receipt alone does not cover the material witness).
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): execution packet = .agent/scratch/new/sinex_urgent_correctness_systematic_v2.md section 3 (r6d.4+w4i record reorder); red-team amendments = new-gpt-pro/01-batch-a-receipt-red-team.report.md. Re-verify line anchors against master on claim.
2026-07-08 session: not started. Hard-blocked on sinex-r6d.11 (receipt boundary) per this bead's own DEPENDS ON — r6d.11 itself only got its mechanism half (4as3 CommitFrontier) landed this session, not the receipt API or any caller wiring. See r6d.11's 2026-07-08 note for the recommended next-session sequencing (r6d.9 harness -> receipt API slice -> per-caller reorder, one at a time). Do not attempt the merge_cursor_update rewrite (adapter_source.rs:2123-2155) without that harness in place first — it is the exact mechanism this bead needs to prove against.

title: Make SQLx schema bootstrap safe against Timescale CA refresh jobs
description: Starting all-sources after dev infra restart failed during cargo SQLx bootstrap with PostgreSQL XX000 tuple concurrently deleted while schema apply tried DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW sinex_telemetry.file_activity_summary. Timescale CA refresh jobs were active after increasing background workers. Fix schema/bootstrap orchestration so apply either serializes with Timescale jobs, disables/pauses CA policies during destructive CA replacement, or avoids destructive reapply when schema is current.
design: Fix slice in progress: schema apply now guards steady-state trigger/function DDL in crate/sinex-schema/src/apply.rs. The intended no-change path skips public updated-at function replacement, dlq/privacy triggers, per-table trigger bundles, operations/cascade/lifecycle/jsonb/search function bundles, and embedding-index trigger bundle when the expected pg_proc/pg_trigger objects already exist. This targets the Timescale CA refresh race by avoiding unnecessary AccessExclusive-trigger DDL and CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION during SQLx bootstrap. Verification: xtask check -p sinex-schema job:2001346.
notes: Implemented local fix in crate/sinex-schema/src/apply.rs: steady-state apply now skips destructive telemetry read-model rebuild when check_telemetry_drifts is empty, skips telemetry CA/policy SQL unless drift exists, makes add_continuous_aggregate_policy calls idempotent, and guards boot-sensitive function/trigger DDL. Verification: xtask check -p sinex-schema job 2001349 exit 0; xtask test -p sinex-schema -E guard-contract-tests job 2001350 exit 0 with schema-apply, nextest-compile, and nextest-run stages successful.
close_reason: Fixed by guarded/idempotent schema apply in crate/sinex-schema/src/apply.rs. Proof: xtask check -p sinex-schema job 2001349 exit 0; targeted guard-contract tests job 2001350 exit 0, including successful schema-apply stage after prior reproductions failed on Timescale CA drop/policy re-add.

title: Fix xtask cancel to terminate child process trees
description: xtask jobs cancel reported job 2001309 as canceled but left cargo-nextest and rustc children alive under the user session. This causes hidden compile/I/O load after an agent believes verification stopped. Fix cancellation to terminate the full process tree/process group and add a regression around orphaned child cleanup.
design: Implemented local fix: JobManager::cancel now terminates the descendant process tree for the background xtask PID before signaling the top-level job process group; process-tree termination uses process-table start-tick matching and sends TERM/KILL to descendant process groups where possible. Regression added: test_cancel_terminates_nested_process_group spawns a nested setsid sleep and proves cancel kills it.
acceptance_criteria: xtask jobs cancel terminates nested child process groups, not only the top-level xtask process group. Regression test test_cancel_terminates_nested_process_group passes. Existing cancel behavior for missing processes and SIGTERM escalation remains unchanged.
close_reason: Fixed locally. Verification: xtask test -p xtask -E 'test(test_cancel_terminates_nested_process_group)' job 2001352 exit 0; nextest compile/run succeeded and the regression proved a nested setsid child is killed by cancel.

Align hourly summary ts_orig with the latest included input timestamp instead of the hour_end boundary, mirroring the daily-summarizer fix, and verify no new hourly future-ts DLQ appears after runtime restart.

title: Impact planner: staged-hunk blindness + no evidence staleness gating (false-skip vectors)
design:
INVESTIGATION FINDINGS (Fable, 2026-07-03, verified against source — fix, do not delete):

DEFECT 1 — staged-hunk blindness (realistic false-skip):
- changed_files() = `git diff --name-only HEAD` (staged+unstaged) + untracked (xtask/src/affected.rs:354-412).
- changed_hunks() = `git diff --unified=0 -- '*.rs'` — UNSTAGED ONLY (xtask/src/impact.rs:~505).
- A fully-staged .rs modification therefore arrives with hunks=[]; then collect_coverage_impacts and collect_manifest_impacts skip all line filtering (`if !hunks.is_empty()` guards, history/db/impact.rs:230,334) and changed_item_fully_covered returns TRUE on empty hunks (impact.rs:477-487). Balanced mode then narrows to the file's historical tests regardless of what the staged change touches. Agents routinely `git add` before testing — this is a live workflow, not an edge case.
FIX 1: compute hunks against the same baseline as changed_files: `git diff HEAD --unified=0 -- '*.rs'`; represent untracked .rs files as whole-file hunks.
FIX 2 (defense in depth): changed_item_fully_covered returns FALSE for a changed .rs file with empty hunks (evidence gap -> package fallback), never true.

DEFECT 2 — evidence never goes stale:
- coverage_regions and test_execution_manifests queries filter ONLY on file_path (history/db/impact.rs:203-213,312-318); test_dependency_edges has a fingerprint column that is WRITTEN on upsert (:502-517) and never read by any query.
- The planner computes content_hash per ChangedItem (impact.rs:240,469) and uses it for nothing.
- Consequence: line ranges seeded at content X are overlap-matched against hunks of content Y after edits shift lines — unsound both directions (false-narrow when the covering function moved; false-broad on unrelated overlap). Evidence is immortal.
FIX 3: record the subject file's content hash (or git blob oid) at seed time for coverage regions + manifests (the fingerprint column pattern already exists); at query time compare with the current file hash; on mismatch demote the evidence to stale (counts as an evidence gap -> package fallback) instead of trusting shifted ranges. Optionally: a seed-refresh hint in the plan output listing stale-evidence files.

SOUND PARTS (verified, keep): balanced mode falls back to package scope when ANY changed file lacks evidence; aggressive mode is opt-in and records accepted risks + audit sampling; exact-proof reuse keys on current_scoped_tree_fingerprint (content-based) and is not affected by these defects.

VALIDATION (after fixes): the merged-PR replay experiment (meta-plane bead) measures the residual false-skip rate of the FIXED planner — run it then, not before, so the number reflects the shipped logic.
acceptance_criteria: Fix 1: a fully-staged .rs change produces identical plans to the same change unstaged (test both states); Fix 2: empty-hunk changed .rs files are evidence gaps (unit test); Fix 3: editing a seeded file invalidates its coverage/manifest evidence (plan shows the gap + stale hint) until re-seeded; existing impact tests green; xtask impact explain output shows the new gap reasons. Then the PR-replay validation experiment runs against the fixed planner.
close_reason: Implemented impact-planner soundness fixes: staged and unstaged Rust hunks now use git diff HEAD, untracked Rust files become whole-file hunks, empty Rust hunk sets are evidence gaps, coverage/manifest evidence records content hashes and stale evidence is ignored with a plan hint. Verification: impact-focused test job 2001422 passed; xtask check -p xtask job 2001423 passed.

title: attention.stream: the unified attention timeline (THE recall substrate)
design: RECIPE: wm focus spans ⊕ browser visits ⊕ terminal bursts ⊕ fs activity -> one interleaved attention timeline (derived interval/point events). All inputs LIVE today. Implementation: Windowed automaton doing interval lifting (wm transitions -> focus spans is L2 prework where missing) then time-join; emits attention.span events with contributing-source refs as parents. Strictly better than any single source for 'what was I doing at T'. NOTE: quality currently degraded by the attention-source bug (activitywatch empty content, hyprland bulk snapshots) — build against the fixed streams or terminal/browser/fs first with wm joining when repaired. Volume budget declared up front (rule 6); semantics_version from day one.
acceptance_criteria: attention.span events queryable and consumed by recall (replacing single-source timeline assembly); every span's parents resolve to the contributing raw events; a window with a known capture gap produces an honest hole, not interpolation; focused automaton tests + live dev-store proof.
notes:
2026-07-04 first implementation slice on branch feature/attention/stream: add attention.span as a real derived event over existing derived.activity-window/activity.window.summary outputs. This deliberately uses the already-live multi-source activity-window layer as the first attention substrate so recall/demo code can consume a stable event kind now; richer interval-lifting/focus-span enrichment remains follow-up rather than blocking the first artifact.

Parent sample resolved 100/100 to activity.window.summary. Demo artifact: .agent/demos/sinex/attention-stream-live-20260704T0526Z. Live proof artifact .agent/demos/sinex/attention-recall-live-20260704T0555Z: sinexctl recall against dev gateway returned attention_spans=15, sessions=9, source_count=5, total_events=50, first attention span parent_refs=1. Verification: job 2001525 passed focused sinexctl recall tests; job 2001526 passed xtask check -p sinex-primitives -p sinexctl; job 2001527 passed xtask build -p sinexctl. Remaining scope: direct raw-parent expansion, known-gap rendering, interval lifting (sinex-8y8), and zero-duration span/source-window quality.
2026-07-04 recall support-ref slice: ContextAttentionSpanView now exposes parent_refs (immediate derived parents) and support_refs (material/raw ancestors from events.lineage) so recall consumers do not have to unpack latest_event to find evidence. Live proof artifact .agent/demos/sinex/attention-recall-support-live-20260704T0619Z: attention_spans=15, attention_spans_with_parent_refs=15, attention_spans_with_support_refs=12, first support ref webhistory/page.visited; bounded caveat recall.attention_lineage_bounded is expected at cap=12. Verification: job 2001530 passed focused sinexctl recall projection test; job 2001531 passed xtask check -p sinex-primitives -p sinexctl; job 2001532 passed xtask build -p sinexctl. 2026-07-04 recall interval evidence slice: ContextSummaryView now exposes intervals: Vec<ContextIntervalView>, and sinexctl recall populates it from derived.interval-lift/state.interval cards alongside attention_spans. Projection preserves parent_refs/latest_event and recovers concrete state_kind from payload_preview.state_kind or, when the bounded preview omitted that field, from interval_id (interval:<state_kind>:...). Live proof artifact .agent/demos/sinex/recall-interval-evidence-live-20260704T1242Z: sinexctl recall --window 6h returned interval_count=1, attention_span_count=1, source_count=10, total_events=27, interval_state_kinds=[desktop.activitywatch.window]; first interval includes parent_refs and latest_event. Remaining sinex-a4w.3.1 scope: richer attention.stream raw-parent expansion, known-gap rendering, zero-duration/source-window quality, and broader interval types as sinex-8y8 grows.

This collapses recall consumers onto one inspectable evidence timeline while preserving specialized arrays for current consumers.
