title: Attention sources unusable: activitywatch empty content, hyprland bulk-snapshot timestamps
description:
The two operator-attention sources ingest unusable data — this is what blocks a genuine multi-source "what was I doing around T" demo:
1. activitywatch: 2,397,839 events, EVERY one has empty app AND title (buckets ingest empty strings; url also empty).
2. wm.hyprland: 582K events bulk-imported at duplicate timestamps (107,344 at one second) — periodic full-snapshot dumps, not focus transitions; titles are kitty spinner frames.

Fix: (a) repair activitywatch extraction to populate app/title/url from aw buckets; (b) switch wm.hyprland to a real-time Hyprland IPC focus-event stream with true per-event timestamps.

Directly improves the recall-v2 campaign (fs+git+shell+browser still work without it, but attention reconstruction needs these). Verification SQL in gh#2197. Notes: .agent/scratch/research-crosssource-demo.md.
design: TWO INDEPENDENT FIXES: (a) activitywatch — the extraction reads aw bucket rows but lands empty app/title/url on 2.4M events; diagnose whether the aw SQLite datastr JSON keys are mis-addressed (aw stores event data as JSON in datastr; keys differ per watcher: app/title for window watcher, url/title/audible for web watcher) vs empty-at-source; fix the parser to per-bucket-type key mapping and REPLAY the material through the normal pipeline (this is exactly what replay is for — the 2.4M bad interpretations archive, fresh correct ones mint); (b) wm.hyprland — replace bulk snapshot ingestion with a real-time IPC subscription driver (Hyprland socket2 events: activewindow/workspace/focusedmon) emitting per-transition events with RealtimeCapture ts_orig; the 582K snapshot-era events with duplicate timestamps get archived via replay-with-current-rules or an explicit lifecycle archive (decide by whether any consumer wants them — likely archive, they are attention-useless). SEQUENCING: (a) unblocks attention.stream's browser/app legs immediately; (b) is a new driver following the source-pattern (register_source_contract + continuous run loop on the IPC socket). Verification SQL in gh#2197.
acceptance_criteria: activitywatch events carry non-empty app/title (and url for web buckets) — SQL: count FILTER (WHERE coalesce(payload->>'title','')='') drops to ~0 on new ingestion; wm.hyprland becomes a real-time IPC focus-event stream with true per-event timestamps (no more 100K-events-per-second snapshot clusters); a cross-source reconstruction over a live window shows attention data interleaved with terminal/journald. The activitywatch fix lands via replay — closing comment records archived-count vs fresh-count for the material; the hyprland driver emits single events per focus transition with distinct timestamps under live window switching.
notes:
2026-07-09 session (part b, hyprland IPC driver): AUDIT CORRECTION + test-coverage
closure. The 2026-07-08/07-09 session notes claiming part (b) "remains untouched...
new source implementation... warrants its own dedicated session" were WRONG — verified
against master (per this repo's own doctrine: check git log/gh, don't trust stale
notes) that the real-time Hyprland IPC driver already landed and is deployed:

- HyprlandParser + UnixSocketStreamAdapter (crate/sinexd/src/sources/source_contracts/
  desktop/window_manager.rs, crate/sinexd/src/runtime/parser/adapters/
  unix_socket_stream.rs) — line-delimited .socket2.sock reader, RealtimeCapture
  per-event timing, exponential-backoff reconnect (50ms..2s cap) — landed via PRs
  #2315 (2026-07-04, "Ref sinex-60r" explicitly in the commit body), #2385, #2390.
- Wired into nixos/modules/sources.nix as the "desktop.window-manager" binding,
  gated on activationProfile="full" — sinnix-prime's hosts/sinnix-prime/default.nix
  sets exactly that, so it is deployed in prod, not just dev.
- Live-verified THIS session: `systemctl status sinexd` + `journalctl -u sinexd`
  on sinnix-prime show desktop.window-manager actively bound and leader-elected
  right now (2026-07-09 15:01 CEST). A read-only 55s passive capture of this host's
  real .socket2.sock (no writes, no compositor interaction) confirmed the parser's
  activewindow/activewindowv2/windowtitle/windowtitlev2 line formats match the live
  Hyprland instance exactly — including the kitty-spinner title churn the original
  bug report described ("titles are kitty spinner frames"): that's real per-frame
  windowtitle/windowtitlev2 traffic, correctly deduped to one window.title_changed
  per v2 line by the existing parser (v1 hint suppressed).
- gh#2197 (closed 2026-07-05) comment trail independently confirms this: PRs
  #2380/#2383/#2384 built a scalable material-scoped archive path and used it to
  archive 13 failed/orphaned desktop.window-manager materials IN THE DEV STORE
  (115,837 cascade rows), then proved 8 live window.focused events with 8 distinct
  ts_orig under a controlled focus switch. This dev-store cleanup is NOT the same
  as the prod 582K-row cleanup this bead's AC still requires — dev and prod are
  separate databases and only dev was touched.

Genuine gap found and closed this session (test(sources) PR, no production code
changed): reconnect_on_eof=true — the ONLY mode used in production config — had
ZERO test coverage; every existing UnixSocketStreamAdapter test used reconnect:
false. Parser-level dispatch also lacked direct unit tests for 9 of its 10 IPC
event-type arms (only activewindow/activewindowv2 merge was covered). Added:
- connect_mode_reconnects_after_producer_closes_mid_stream (real socket, EOF,
  second producer connection, monotonic frame/byte offsets across reconnect)
- 14 parser tests: openwindow, closewindow, movewindow, windowtitlev2,
  windowtitle-v1-suppression, workspace/workspacev2, non-integer workspace id
  error, focusedmon/focusedmonv2, unknown-event wm.unhandled passthrough,
  malformed-line handling (including flushing a stale buffered activewindow).
Verification: xtask check -p sinexd job 1 exit 0; xtask test -p sinexd --lib
-E 'test(window_manager) or test(unix_socket) or test(hyprland)' jobs 2+3
(second run --no-reuse to rule out socket-timing flakiness) both 36 passed/0
failed; xtask test -p sinexd --test production_path -E
'test(desktop_window_manager) or test(desktop_activitywatch) or
test(desktop_clipboard)' job 4, 9 passed/0 failed. Pre-push changed-strict
passed. PR: https://github.com/Sinity/sinex/pull/2449 (open, not merged —
left for review per standing live-socket/reconnection-code caution).

REMAINING operator action for this bead's AC (production-data-affecting,
NOT executed this session, needs explicit go-ahead like the AW replay did):
archive the pre-2026-07-04T04:59:39Z (PR #2315 merge time) wm.hyprland bulk-
snapshot rows in sinex_prod. Use the SAME mechanism already proven on dev
(gh#2197, 2026-07-05T07:04:54Z comment), NOT a lifecycle/event-count archive:

  sinexctl sources list --source desktop.window-manager --limit 200
  # identify material ids created/completed before 2026-07-04T04:59:39Z
  # (the PR #2315 merge time) — these are the pre-realtime-driver bulk
  # materials; the post-#2315 continuous-stream material(s) must be excluded.
  sinexctl sources archive <material-id> --dry-run --reason "sinex-60r: archive pre-realtime-driver bulk-snapshot Hyprland material (gh#2197)"
  # review the cascade preview, then repeat without --dry-run per material id.

Verify afterward with the bead's own gh#2197 SQL (duplicate-ts_coided cluster
count should drop to ~0; live wm.hyprland events should only carry distinct
per-transition ts_orig going forward). Do not use `sinexctl ops lifecycle
archive --source` for this — the material-scoped `sinexctl sources archive`
path is what was actually validated against this exact failure mode on dev
and is the more surgical/correct primitive (whole bad material, not an
arbitrary event-count/duration slice).

state.captured (declared in HyprlandParser's source_meta/manifest event-type
list, HyprlandStateCapturedPayload in sinex-primitives) is NOT emitted by
current dispatch_hyprland_event — confirmed this is the legacy full-state-
dump payload shape from the pre-fix bulk-snapshot era, kept only so schema
validation still passes for the still-live archived rows. Leave it declared
until the archive above lands; removing it now would break validation for
data that still needs archiving.

Bead-level status: part (a) [activitywatch] proof-matrix done, replay
pending operator go-ahead (see 2026-07-09 earlier note this session). Part
(b) [hyprland] driver + tests done and PR open; prod archive pending
operator go-ahead. Neither replay/archive action has been executed — this
bead should stay open until both land and are verified.

comments.author: Sinity
comments.text:
2026-07-10 session: part (a) [activitywatch] replay pipeline VERIFIED end to end in production, not just proof-matrix. Blocked all night on two real bugs, both now fixed and merged: (1) replay-control response leaked root_event_ids (hundreds of thousands of UUIDs) in every response carrying a ReplayOperation, not just Preview -- PR #2451 fixed Preview, PR #2453 closed the remaining Status/List/Approve/Submit/Execute/Cancel gap; (2) core.expand_cascade's per-level join defeated its own GIN index once the root set grew large (200k events -> EXPLAIN cost 268M, statement timeout) -- PR #2455 rewrote it to a single array_agg predicate (~100x cost reduction), recorded as sinex-o1mg (now closed). PR #2455's fix additionally required a manual psql patch to reach sinex_prod because schema-apply's existence-only guard never reconciles changed function bodies -- permanently fixed via PR #2457 (hash-based drift detection).

Executed a real production replay end to end: plan -> preview -> approve -> execute on desktop.activitywatch, 2026-06-12T03:58:00Z to 2026-06-12T03:59:00Z (one minute, chosen as a dense proof slice: 30,000 events, cascade depth 3, 191 derived events). Verified directly against sinex_prod: window.active events (9,465) now carry real app/title ("kitty", "⠦ polylogue" -- actual window titles, not empty strings); browser.tab.active events (534) carry real url/title (actual Chrome tab URLs and page titles). This is exactly the bug this bead's part (a) describes, confirmed fixed for freshly-replayed data.

NOT done: the FULL activitywatch backlog (2,397,839 events per this bead's original count) has NOT been replayed -- only this one proof slice. The remaining ~2.37M events still need a batched replay across the full date range. Given expand_cascade is no longer the bottleneck, the next constraint is DB connection pool sizing under concurrent replay+live-ingestion load (SINEX_API_POOL_MAX_CONNECTIONS defaults to 4 via nixos/modules/default.nix connectionPool.maxConnections, shared with SINEX_EVENT_ENGINE_POOL_SIZE) -- a 200k-event preview transaction held that small pool long enough to starve even routine periodic telemetry sampling. Not fixed tonight (deliberately deferred: it's a global per-service pool-size knob feeding into Postgres's own max_connections computation across ~16 services, not a quick tweak, and two nixos switches already happened tonight). Batching the full replay in smaller windows (similar to tonight's 1-minute proof slice) sidesteps this without needing the pool-size fix, at the cost of many more plan/preview/approve/execute round-trips.

Part (b) [hyprland] driver landed (PR #2449, prior session) but the pending prod bulk-snapshot archive (582K rows) was not executed this session either.

title: Source checkpoint silent loss: remaining prongs (page-0, journalctl cursor, bash dedup, browser context)
description:
STATUS CORRECTION vs the GitHub body: the headline prong (cursor advanced+persisted BEFORE emit in adapter_source.rs, = Fable packet P1) is FIXED on local master commit 6b07b0078 "fix(runtime): advance adapter cursors after emit" — NOT yet pushed/PR'd (see the master-reconcile issue). Verify that commit covers snapshot+historical+continuous paths, then treat prong 1 as done.

Remaining prongs:
2. Page-0 crash indistinguishable from source-exhausted (api_cursor.rs:413,459): page-0 records carry cursor_after=None; a mid-page-0 crash checkpoint equals fully-drained -> rest of page 0 skipped forever. A test currently ASSERTS the broken behavior.
3. Synthetic frame:N cursor corrupts journalctl resume (journalctl_stream.rs:188) — passed later as --after-cursor=frame:N which journalctl rejects/mis-seeks.
4. SharedJournalctlStream always spawns cursor=None, ignoring persisted checkpoints.
5. bash dedup window not checkpointed -> re-emits on rotation crash.
6. browser history declares Metadata at contract but emits Clipboard per-event (misrouted URL redaction).

Privacy chokepoint itself audited CLEAN (redact_batch gates the sole insert path). Per-finding file:line+scenario+fix+AC: .agent/scratch/research-audit-ingestion.md.
design: Settled per-prong fixes (research-audit-ingestion.md has file:line + scenario + draft AC per finding): page-0 -> make an explicit 'page 0 incomplete' cursor state distinguishable from exhausted, and fix the test that asserts the broken behavior; journalctl -> never synthesize frame:N cursors, treat missing __CURSOR as un-checkpointable record (emit but do not advance, or route to debt); SharedJournalctlStream -> spawn from the persisted checkpoint; bash dedup window -> include window in checkpoint state; browser -> emit with the contract-declared Metadata context. Note the generic cursor-before-emit fix landed at 6b07b0078 — pattern-match its shape for the remaining prongs.
acceptance_criteria: Per prong: a focused test simulating the fault (crash mid-page-0, missing __CURSOR, rotation crash) shows no silent skip — record re-read or visible in DLQ/debt. The page-0 test asserting broken behavior is inverted. xtask test -p sinexd on the touched test files passes; changed-strict passes.
notes:
2026-07-05 Codex partial implementation: closed prong 3 by refusing to synthesize journalctl frame:N cursors when __CURSOR is absent, with test_cursor_after_missing_cursor_is_not_checkpointable proving the record is not checkpointable as a fake cursor; closed prong 6 by aligning browser history per-event privacy_context and parser manifest to ProcessingContext::Metadata, with browser_history_intent_uses_metadata_privacy_context proving emitted intents match the contract. Verification: CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E 'test(journalctl)' passed invocation 2005751 (19 tests); CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask test -p sinexd --test browser_history_parser_test passed invocation 2005752 (6 tests); CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask check --changed-strict origin/master passed, affected package sinexd. Remaining prongs: page-0 incomplete/exhausted state, SharedJournalctlStream persisted checkpoint wiring, bash dedup checkpoint window.

2026-07-05 Codex cursor batch: closed prong 2 by adding explicit ApiCursorPosition.page_incomplete state, so a page-0 mid-page checkpoint with last_cursor=None re-fetches page 0 instead of being treated as terminal exhaustion; added page_zero_mid_page_checkpoint_refetches_page_zero and legacy-terminal compatibility coverage. Closed prong 4 at the shared-stream API boundary by adding SharedJournalctlStream::from_cursor / with_capacity_from_cursor and passing the recovered JournalctlCursor into the underlying JournalctlStreamAdapter::open path instead of hardcoding None; existing journalctl_args_resume_after_runtime_cursor coverage proves runtime cursor precedence over config/default start. Verification: CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E 'test(api_cursor) | test(journalctl)' passed invocation 2005764 (37 tests); CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask check --changed-strict origin/master passed (sinexd only). Resource note: first two attempts were earlyoom-killed during sinexd rustc at available RAM ~=5%; pausing lynchpin-materialize plus btrfs metadata/btrbk timers/services gave enough headroom. Remaining sinex-jda scope: bash dedup window checkpointing.
2026-07-05 Codex final checkpoint batch: closed remaining bash dedup window prong by adding parser-local checkpoint hooks to MaterialParser, persisting AdapterModuleState.parser_checkpoint alongside adapter cursors, and wiring bash/zsh/text history parsers to serialize/restore their ContentHashWindow. Added rollback so parser-local state is restored when parse/emit/conversion fails and the cursor stays behind. Also fixed merge_cursor_update so JSON null from one ChainedCursor leg means no update instead of clearing the existing leg. Regression proof: checkpointed_dedup_window_suppresses_rotation_overlap_after_restart, snapshot_round_trips_recent_hashes, adapter_source_updates_parser_checkpoint_after_successful_parse, adapter_emit_failure_rolls_back_parser_checkpoint, adapter_cursor_update_preserves_chained_leg_state. Verification: focused broad probe CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask test -p sinexd --lib -E 'test(adapter_source) | test(dedup) | test(bash_history)' compiled and ran 49 tests; invocation 2005768 had 48 pass and exposed the chained-cursor merge bug, then targeted rerun invocation 2005769 passed 5/5 after the fix. CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask check --changed-strict origin/master passed: changed-strict: 2 packages checked, all passed (sinex-primitives, sinexd). All sinex-jda / gh-2198 named prongs are now implemented.
close_reason: Completed: all #2198 source-checkpoint loss prongs are fixed and covered by focused tests; changed-strict passed for sinex-primitives and sinexd.

title: Replay crash between archive-commit and re-ingest loses events permanently
description:
CRITICAL silent data loss, verified against source. Archive cascade commits in one TX (collect.rs:333, correct), but a crash between the committed archive and re-emission (replay_writer.rs:324) is unrecoverable: commit_recovery (state_machine.rs:1152/1193) only sets state=Failed, never restores the archived cascade. Events survive only in audit.archived_events; nothing re-creates them. Host has demonstrated OOM-kill history.

Related HIGH in same subsystem: core.execute_cascade_restore uses ON CONFLICT (id) — occurrence-blind; a restore against re-emitted events can produce TWO live interpretations per occurrence.

Fix: on crash recovery of stale EXECUTING/COMMITTING, detect archive-committed-but-not-reemitted and restore the archived cascade (same path as in-process abort) before marking Failed — or make the boundary durable (op-log-driven resume that re-issues the scan). Key restores on (source_material_id, anchor_byte), not id.

Verify: sed -n '1152,1245p' crate/sinex-db/src/replay/state_machine.rs; rg 'restore_archived_cascade' crate/sinex-db/src/replay/. Full notes: .agent/scratch/research-audit-provenance-replay.md. = Fable packet P4.
design: DECISION: on crash recovery of a stale EXECUTING/COMMITTING op, detect archive-committed-but-not-reemitted and restore the archived cascade (compensation pattern already exists: abort_before_scan_ack / restore_archived_cascade) before marking Failed — or make the boundary durable via op-log-driven resume that re-issues the scan. The restore keys on occurrence (source_material_id, anchor_byte), never ON CONFLICT (id) — the id-keyed restore is how you get two live interpretations per occurrence. TARGETS: state_machine.rs:1152-1245 (commit_recovery), replay_writer.rs:324 (re-emission), core.execute_cascade_restore.
acceptance_criteria: kill -9 between archive-commit and scan-ack -> restart restores archived events or resumes the scan; never permanent loss; no occurrence ends up with two live interpretations (test both recovery orderings). VERIFY: replay e2e (tests/e2e replay tests) + targeted state_machine unit tests; xtask test -p sinex-db -E 'test(replay)'.
close_reason: Completed on master via PR #2207 / commit bd27d6147 and reverified 2026-07-03. Code evidence: ReplayStateMachine::commit_recovery restores archived replay cascades from the durable scope-invalidation journal before marking stale operations failed; execute_cascade_restore is occurrence-safe and skips archived material rows when the same (source_material_id, anchor_byte) is already live. Verification: job 2001453 passed CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_TEST_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask test -p sinex-db -E 'test(cascade_restore_skips_material_occurrence_already_live)'; job 2001454 passed CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_TEST_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(recover_stale_executing_restores_archived_cascade)'.

title: Catch dev event-engine up to realtime on the new work-dir; clear orphan residue; remeasure
description:
Continuation of the interrupted recovery lane. Local core was restarted under SINEX_EVENT_ENGINE_WORK_DIR=/realm/project/sinex/.sinex/state/event-engine (assembler/content-store state off /tmp, which sat on a 92%-full root). Raw + DLQ streams were purged once but orphan residue from already-lost material frames persisted.

Refs: OPERATING-LOG.md 2026-07-03T05:22/05:45/05:57; xtask analytics store; gh#2204 for the structural retention fix.

title: Current-master sinexd full-suite failure inventory (2026-07-10)
description: First full sinexd suite runs in ~2 months (invocations 2006052/2006055 in xtask-history.db, during sinex-v7od verification) surfaced an accumulated failure mass that predates and is independent of the binary consolidation: 1984 tests, 64/68 failures across two runs with heavy churn (22 newly failing / 18 fixed between back-to-back runs). Classes: (1) DB-slot-pool exhaustion, 33-37 per run, message 'Failed to acquire database after 100 attempts' (xtask/src/sandbox/db/pool/mod.rs:1488) — unstable set, likely contention-shape sensitivity; needs a quiet-host run + slot/pool sizing analysis before treating any of them as code bugs. (2) ~20 pre-existing deterministic failures incl. the known sinex-r6d.13 confirmed-republish fixture. (3) Generated-artifact drift: privacy-coverage matrix stale — code removed 'clipboard' from browser-history parser privacy_contexts, docs/sources/privacy-coverage.generated.json never regenerated (regen: sinexd export-privacy-coverage-matrix). (4) Revived-orphan drift: terminal/atuin required-input-keys tests fail on unmodified sources (declared keys 'command/timestamp' vs expected 'history.command/history.timestamp'; atuin missing-required-key now Degraded caveat instead of blocking readiness) with NO prior pass history under any name. (5) Deterministic lib-target assertion failures: email source-contract tests (email_test.rs:136,164), material_assembler finalize/stale-cleanup, acquisition_manager oversized-slice, preflight discover_unit_files, runner drain test. All lib-target failures provably not caused by v7od (tests-only diff).
acceptance_criteria: Each cluster either turns green on current master or is owned by a dedicated bead linked from here; pool-exhaustion class gets a quiet-host baseline run + a sizing/test-group decision; matrix artifact regenerated; no cluster silently dropped.
notes: Evidence: .agent/scratch/2026-07-10-agent-dialogue.md E8; /realm/tmp/v7od-listed-tests.txt; jobs 2001865/2001867 logs. Discovered during sinex-v7od by fable session 1bb6d090.

title: Declare per-source criticality tiers so the trust-sinex-with-deletion flip is explicit
description: Operator framing 2026-07-10: the sinex dataset is deliberately treated as non-critical (helps dev speed), but nobody had verified whether it already holds irreplaceable data. Verified answer: currently it does NOT — 85% is journal/self-chatter, and every personal source in core.events (activitywatch, shell.atuin, wm.hyprland, canonical.terminal, webhistory, keylog-class) still has its own canonical store in the /realm/data lake or the source app's DB; extractor/derived sources are rebuildable by replay. The only sinex-exclusive copies are expired-journal history (low value). THIS WILL SILENTLY CHANGE the moment an external ephemeral source (IMAP email, comms) comes online — the dataset flips to partially-irreplaceable with no ceremony. Wanted: a per-source criticality declaration (source registry field or config) with an explicit gate: adding a source whose upstream is ephemeral requires marking it critical, which should be visible in health/backup tooling. Until then the consuming host's daily blob borg job (added 2026-07-10) is the insurance.
acceptance_criteria:
- per-source criticality field exists and is populated for all active sources
- adding an ephemeral-upstream source without a criticality declaration fails loudly (validation)
- the current all-reconstructible status is recorded and re-checkable by query

title: Retire GH-issue-native verify-closure.yml + xtask verify closure subsystem, or repoint at beads
description:
GitHub Issues have been retired as the sinex task substrate (2026-07-10, operator directive): all 7 open issues closed as not-planned, cross-referenced to covering beads. Beads (bd) is now the sole durable task substrate.

Remaining GH-issue-native machinery that has no bead-native equivalent yet:
- .github/workflows/verify-closure.yml: triggers on `issues: closed` or manual dispatch with an issue number, runs `xtask verify closure <N>`, posts a `gh issue comment` back with fabricated-closure evidence if found.
- xtask/src/commands/verify.rs (~L1065-1972): execute_closure, fetch_issue_closure_payload, ClosureMatrixItem, extract_closure_matrix_items etc. -- fetches issue bodies/comments via `gh` and parses closure matrices, evidence manifests, AC status.
- .github/issue-operating-model.md: full governance doc defining issue kinds/readiness/closure-matrix rules, GH-issue-native, zero beads awareness.
- .github/pull_request_template.md:93 references "This branch is tied to an issue..."
- .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ (5 templates + config.yml).

This is the closure-fabrication guard (CONTRIBUTING.md's #1081/#987 postmortems) -- beads currently has no automated verifier equivalent. Do not silently drop this safety net when retiring the GH-issue-native version; either port the closure-verification logic to read from `bd show <id> --json` (design/acceptance_criteria/close_reason fields) or explicitly design its bead-native replacement before deleting the GH-side implementation.
acceptance_criteria:
- Decision recorded: repoint verify-closure.yml + xtask verify closure at bead closure evidence, or replace with a bead-native closure-fabrication verifier.
- CONTRIBUTING.md and .github/issue-operating-model.md rewritten (or archived with a superseded-by-beads pointer) to describe beads as the task substrate.
- .github/pull_request_template.md and ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ updated so nothing routes new work back into GH issues.
- No regression in the closure-fabrication safety net: whatever replaces xtask verify closure must still be able to catch a bead closed with fabricated/absent verification evidence.

title: Consolidate 88 per-file [[test]] binaries into aggregated harness(es)
description: sinexd/Cargo.toml declares 88 separate [[test]] targets (one per file). Under cargo-nextest — the repo's actual runner — per-file binaries add no isolation: nextest executes every test in its own process regardless of binary layout. What the 88 targets cost, measured on the consuming workstation 2026-07-10: 88 link jobs each against the full 219K-LOC sinexd lib (concurrent mold linkers filled an 8G zram tier during a 3-test-file verification run), any lib change relinks all 88, and 'cargo nextest -E' filters at runtime so the full target set builds first. Consolidating into one aggregated harness (tests/main.rs + mod per file), or a few grouped by fixture profile, links once with identical per-test process isolation. Watch for: rare tests relying on binary-level state (ctor/statics/env-at-load), and any deliberate use of --test <name> selective builds that grouping would coarsen. Related: sinex-qabz (same monolithic-lib disease amplifies both).
acceptance_criteria:
- test binary count reduced from 88 to <=5 with all tests passing under cargo nextest
- a lib-touching rebuild's test-relink wall time and peak RAM measured before/after and recorded here
- no test behavior change attributable to shared binary state (full suite green in CI-equivalent local gate)

title: SINEX_API_POOL_MAX_CONNECTIONS=4 starves under concurrent replay+ingestion load
description:
## Symptom
A replay preview transaction covering ~200k events holds a connection from
the API gateway's own pool long enough to starve routine periodic work
sharing the same pool -- confirmed live 2026-07-09/10: "Replay telemetry
sample failed err=Timeout(pool timed out while waiting for an open
connection, timeout_reason: pool_exhausted)" fired repeatedly, and
`ops replay preview`/`execute` themselves failed the same way.

## Root cause
`nixos/modules/default.nix:436` — `services.sinex.database.connectionPool.
maxConnections` defaults to 4. This single knob feeds BOTH
`SINEX_API_POOL_MAX_CONNECTIONS` and `SINEX_EVENT_ENGINE_POOL_SIZE`
(nixos/modules/sources.nix:578,603) per service, and factors into Postgres's
own `max_connections` sizing formula (`perServiceConnections *
totalServiceCount + 25`, database.nix:60-66). 4 connections is tight for the
API gateway's own concurrent workload: a preview/execute cascade transaction,
periodic replay telemetry sampling, and ordinary RPC traffic all compete for
the same 4 slots.

## Reproduction
Run `sinexctl ops replay preview` on a scope covering 100k+ events while
sinexd is under any concurrent load (live ingestion, another RPC caller) --
the transaction holds a connection for the duration of cascade expansion +
safety analysis, starving the other 3 slots.

## Why not fixed immediately (2026-07-10 session note)
This is a GLOBAL per-service pool-size knob, not something to bump for just
the API pool in isolation -- it recomputes Postgres's own max_connections
across ~16 services (event engine + API + ~14 automata). Changing it needs a
NixOS switch + Postgres restart to take effect, and two switches already
happened during the session that found this (diagnosing a separate swap/
earlyoom tuning issue). Verified the underlying bug (sinex-o1mg,
expand_cascade GIN index) is unrelated and already fixed -- this is a
distinct, lower-urgency resource-sizing issue. Sidestepped for the immediate
need by replaying in a much smaller window (1 minute / 30k events instead of
the full multi-day backlog), which doesn't hold the pool long enough to
matter.

## Fix direction (not yet implemented)
Either: (a) give the API gateway pool a larger dedicated `maxConnections`
override distinct from the shared per-service default (the module already
supports per-service overrides in principle, needs the option threaded
through), or (b) make `preview_cascade_impact`/cascade-analysis queries not
hold a single long-lived transaction across the whole expand+dependency-load
sequence, or (c) simply raise the global default and re-verify Postgres's
`max_connections` still fits comfortably (currently 109, computed from the
current default of 4 -- raising to e.g. 12-16 would need recomputing and
confirming no other constraint is hit).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A replay preview/execute over a real multi-hundred-thousand-event scope
      does not starve concurrent routine API traffic (telemetry sampling,
      other RPC calls) for the duration of the operation.
- [ ] Decision recorded on which fix direction was taken and why.
- [ ] Verified live against sinex_prod under realistic concurrent load, not
      just a quiet host.

title: replay_event_sources() alias table doesn't know per-source dotted SourceContract ids, only 4 legacy umbrella names
description: Live incident 2026-07-09 (sinex-60r's AW replay attempt): ReplayScope::replay_event_sources() (crate/sinex-db/src/replay/state_machine.rs) maps a replay scope's source_name to the actual events.source value(s) it should match, via a hand-maintained match statement. It only has cases for 4 legacy umbrella names (filesystem-watcher, terminal-watcher, desktop-watcher, system-watcher) -- NOT the current per-source dotted SourceContract ids (desktop.activitywatch, desktop.clipboard, desktop.window-manager, terminal.atuin-history, etc, per crate/sinexd/src/sources/source_contracts/**) that are what operators actually pass to --source today and what sinexctl sources list/show surface as the canonical identifier. A replay scoped to any such id that has NO case in the match (the overwhelming majority -- grepped ~35 registered contract ids, only 1 legacy umbrella maps a subset) silently matches ZERO events: no error, no warning, preview just reports total_events=0 regardless of the time window, indistinguishable from 'this source genuinely has no data in this window'.
design: Two paths: (1) stopgap, already applied for desktop.activitywatch -> activitywatch as cases are discovered live (same pattern the existing 4-case table already uses) -- does not scale, will keep silently under-matching for every other untested contract id; (2) structural fix -- derive replay_event_sources() from the actual SourceContract registry (crate/sinexd/src/sources/source_contracts/**, the id/source_id declared per contract) instead of hand-maintaining a parallel list in sinex-db, which is a different crate with no current dependency on the runtime source registry. This is the SAME failure shape as sinex-dffy (sinnix's stream-topology mkForce override silently diverging from sinex's nats.nix defaults) -- a hand-maintained alias/list table drifting from its authoritative source. Consider whether both deserve the same general fix pattern (a canonical exported mapping both sides consume) rather than solving each one-off.
acceptance_criteria: Every registered SourceContract id resolves correctly via a replay scope's --source flag without needing a hand-added case; OR replay creation validates source_name against the registry and rejects/warns on an id that resolves to zero possible events.source matches, rather than silently previewing/executing an empty scope. A regression test proves at least one currently-broken contract id (before the desktop.activitywatch stopgap, or a different untested one) now resolves correctly.
notes: Discovered and stopgap-fixed live while attempting sinex-60r's ActivityWatch replay. Every sinexctl ops replay preview attempt against --source desktop.activitywatch returned Direct Events: 0 regardless of time window (tested 24h, 1-day, 1-week, 27-day windows, both before and after cancelling/replanning) until this was traced to the alias gap -- cost significant diagnostic time because the failure mode (silent zero-match) is indistinguishable from 'no data in this window'.

title: sinnix's mkForce stream-list override silently shadows sinex's own nats.nix defaults
description:
## Steps to Reproduce
1. Add or modify a stream declaration in sinex's nixos/modules/nats.nix bootstrapStreams.streams default list (e.g. sinex-bor's PR #2423 adding 5 reflection-lane streams).
2. Bump sinnix's flake.lock sinex input to a rev including that change.
3. nix develop --command switch on a host whose services.sinex config sets bootstrapStreams.streams via lib.mkForce (modules/services/sinex/bridge.nix).
4. Observe: sinex-nats-bootstrap.service runs and exits 0, but the new/changed streams are never created -- mkForce replaced the whole list, sinex's new defaults never entered the merge at all.
5. sinexd (if the runtime hard-gates on topology completeness, per sinex-bor) refuses to start; or (if it doesn't) silently runs against an incomplete topology.
design: Two paths sinex-bor's own close reason already named as deferred: (A) change bootstrapStreams.streams (or a sibling option) to support per-stream-name field overrides merged against sinex's own defaults, so a downstream flake only declares deltas (e.g. sinnix's maxBytes=null workaround for natscli's 32-bit limit on the raw/confirmed streams) instead of the whole topology; (B) sinex-bor's original larger-scope idea -- a Rust-exported canonical JSON stream-topology spec consumed by both nix and the runtime bootstrap, with downstream flakes only able to express recognized deltas against it, so an omitted stream is a build-time/eval-time error rather than a silent runtime refusal six days later. Either path should make 'sinex changed its stream defaults, sinnix wasn't updated' impossible to express, not just 'harder to get wrong'.
acceptance_criteria: A change to sinex's nixos/modules/nats.nix stream defaults (add/modify a stream) is either automatically inherited by sinnix's bootstrapStreams config, or produces a loud eval-time/build-time signal that sinnix needs updating -- never a silent runtime gap discovered only when sinexd refuses to serve. sinnix's bridge.nix no longer hand-copies stream definitions that are byte-identical to sinex's own defaults.
notes: Discovered and worked around live during the 2026-07-09 correctness-closure-batch session while fixing the 6-day sinexd outage (also caused by a separate, already-fixed timescaledb package-bump issue). Operator explicitly asked to 'simplify sinnix setup where viable... if we can instead do the setup within sinex itself (non-machine-specific)' -- this bead is that ask, scoped as its own reviewable follow-up rather than rushed into the incident fix.

title: jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery deterministically fails: fail_confirmations(3) exhausts CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS
description: Discovered while adding sinex-r6d.12's missing multi-child settlement test coverage (PR #2440).
design:
crate/sinexd/tests/event_engine/events_consumer_integration_test.rs::jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery
currently fails deterministically on master (verified 2026-07-09, isolated rerun, exit 101):

  Error: no message available in stream ..._CONFIRMED for subject ...confirm-retry...: no message found

Root cause: the test calls `TestHooks::builder().count_deliveries().fail_confirmations(3)`.
`fail_confirmations(3)` forces the counter-decrementing failure hook in
crate/sinexd/src/event_engine/jetstream_consumer/confirmation.rs::publish_confirmed_event to
fail on every call while the counter is > 0. `publish_confirmed_event_with_retry` attempts
exactly CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS = 3 tries (confirmation.rs). With only ONE confirmable
event in the test and a forced-failure budget of exactly 3, ALL 3 in-process retries are
consumed and fail -- there is no successful attempt left. The batch then returns a
confirmation_durability_gap_error, which is_fatal_batch_processing_error() classifies as
fatal in run_loop.rs, so the consumer's run() returns Err and the task exits. The test then
waits (Timeouts::SHORT) for a confirmed-events stream message that will never appear, since
nothing is polling anymore, and fails on timeout.

The test's own name/intent ("republishes ... without raw redelivery") clearly wants: force
FEWER failures than the retry budget, so the LAST in-process attempt succeeds and the
confirmed event ships without needing JetStream to redeliver the raw message. That requires
`fail_confirmations(2)`, not `fail_confirmations(3)` -- off-by-one against
CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS.

Predates sinex-r6d.12: confirmation.rs's CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS and run_loop.rs's fatal
error handling were NOT touched by PR #2425 (r6d.12's RawEnvelopeSettlement landing) --
verified via `git show 4cf3a90bc --stat`. This is a pre-existing, orthogonal test bug, not a
regression from the settlement coordinator work.

Likely fix: change `.fail_confirmations(3)` to `.fail_confirmations(2)` in
events_consumer_integration_test.rs (single-line test fix), then re-verify the test actually
exercises in-process retry-then-succeed as its name claims.

Verification commands run:
  xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery)'
  (isolated run, single-threaded target) -> FAILED, exit code 101, as described above.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria
- jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery passes in isolation
  and asserts genuine in-process retry-then-succeed (delivery count stays 1, confirmed event
  is published, idempotent persist count == 1).
- Root cause (off-by-one between fail_confirmations() budget and
  CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS) is fixed at the test call site, or CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS
  is deliberately reconsidered if the intended contract differs from "N < max forces recovery,
  N >= max forces a durability gap".
notes:
## Steps to Reproduce
1. On current master (verified at commit 0ad01fcca / after PR #2425 sinex-r6d.12 landed):
   env DATABASE_URL=<live dev DB> nix develop --command xtask test -p sinexd \
     -E 'test(jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery)'
2. Observe: FAILED, exit 101, "Error: no message available in stream ..._CONFIRMED for
   subject ...: no message found" (crate/sinexd/tests/event_engine/support.rs:143).
3. Reproduces deterministically (not load/timing-dependent) because
   fail_confirmations(3) == CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS (3) exactly -- see design notes.

title: confirmation durability-gap crashes consumer, contradicting jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery test premise
description:
Confirmation durability-gap crashes the whole consumer task with no non-redelivery recovery
path, contradicting the premise of an existing test that expects durability-gap recovery
WITHOUT raw redelivery.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run: `xtask test -p sinexd --test '*' -E 'test(jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery)'`
2. Observe failure: "Error: no message available in stream ... confirmation_d_retry: no message found" after ~11-12s (well inside the test's own Timeouts::MEDIUM budget).
3. Confirmed reproducible standalone (no other tests running, no host contention) — not a resource-contention flake.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Either: durability-gap recovery no longer requires the SAME dead consumer instance to survive (fix option 1 or 3 in the design notes), and this test passes without modification.
- [ ] Or: the fatal-crash-on-durability-gap behavior is confirmed as the intended design, and this test is corrected to assert the crash + redelivery-based recovery it was originally written to test (fix option 2), with its name reverted to reflect that.
- [ ] Whichever path is chosen, the decision and rationale get recorded in this bead before closing.
design:
Discovered while implementing sinex-r6d.12 (aggregate-safe raw-message settlement).

The test `jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery`
(crate/sinexd/tests/event_engine/events_consumer_integration_test.rs) forces
exactly 3 confirmation-publish failures via `fail_confirmations(3)`, which
exactly matches `CONFIRM_PUBLISH_MAX_ATTEMPTS = 3`
(crate/sinexd/src/event_engine/jetstream_consumer/confirmation.rs). This
exhausts every internal retry attempt inside a SINGLE
`publish_confirmed_event_with_retry` call, so it always returns Err.

That Err becomes a `confirmation_durability_gap` (persist.rs /
prepare.rs), which `run_loop.rs::is_fatal_batch_processing_error` treats as
FATAL: the whole consumer task exits via `return Err(e)`. Nothing in the
test harness (`spawn_consumer_and_wait_ready` — a single bare `tokio::spawn`,
no supervisor/restart loop) restarts the consumer. The raw message is
deliberately left unacked ("left_unacked_for_redelivery" per
`confirmation_durability_gap_error`), but since the consumer task is dead,
nobody pulls it again within the test's `Timeouts::MEDIUM` window — the test
times out waiting for the confirmed-events message ("no message found").

Verified via `git log -p -S` archaeology: this test's core assertion
(`deliveries == 1`, i.e. no raw redelivery needed) was introduced in PR #196
(2026, very early history) as a rename from
`jetstream_consumer_redelivers_when_confirmation_publish_fails`
(which asserted `deliveries >= 2`). The rename's rationale is not documented
in that commit body, and no reconciliation/backfill mechanism that would
let a durability-gapped event's confirmation get retried WITHOUT crashing
the consumer currently exists anywhere in crate/sinexd/src (grepped for
unconfirmed/confirmation_backfill/reconcil — nothing matches). Either the
test's premise stopped being true when some later refactor made durability
gaps fatal, or a repair mechanism that used to exist was since removed.

Verified this is NOT caused by sinex-r6d.12 (envelope settlement
coordinator): for this exact single-event-single-message scenario, old code
(`ack_messages.push` skipped on gap, function still returns
`confirmation_durability_gap_error` at the end) and new code (settle_child
skipped on gap, same Err returned) produce byte-identical outcomes — zero
difference in the diff touches this path's fatal/crash semantics.

Fix options (未 investigated in depth):
1. Make durability gaps non-fatal: keep the raw message unacked, let the
   SAME consumer instance retry via an internal backoff loop or a bounded
   local requeue, rather than crashing and relying on redelivery it may
   never get in the fast path.
2. Restore true crash-and-redeliver semantics and fix this specific test's
   `deliveries == 1` assertion instead (revert to expecting redelivery).
3. Add a genuine unconfirmed-event reconciliation sweep at consumer
   startup/restart that republishes from Postgres for events durably
   persisted but never confirmed — this is closer to the test's original
   intent and would also plug a real operator gap (a crashed consumer today
   has NO automatic way to close this gap outside of raw redelivery, which
   this specific failure mode explicitly avoids).

Verification: run in isolation (no contention):
`xtask test -p sinexd --test '*' -E 'test(jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery)'`
acceptance_criteria:
Either: durability-gap recovery no longer requires the SAME dead consumer instance to survive
(fix option 1 or 3 in the design notes), and the affected test passes without modification.
Or: the fatal-crash-on-durability-gap behavior is confirmed as the intended design, and that
test is corrected to assert the crash + redelivery-based recovery it was originally written to
test (fix option 2), with its name reverted to reflect that. Whichever path is chosen, the
decision and rationale get recorded in this bead before closing.
notes:
## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run: xtask test -p sinexd --test '*' -E 'test(jetstream_consumer_republishes_confirmed_event_without_raw_redelivery)'
2. Observe failure: "Error: no message available in stream ... confirmation_d_retry: no message found" after ~11-12s (well inside the test's own Timeouts::MEDIUM budget).
3. Confirmed reproducible standalone (no other tests running, no host contention) — not a resource-contention flake.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Either: durability-gap recovery no longer requires the SAME dead consumer instance to survive (fix option 1 or 3 above), and this test passes without modification.
- [ ] Or: the fatal-crash-on-durability-gap behavior is confirmed as the intended design, and this test is corrected to assert the crash + redelivery-based recovery it was originally written to test (fix option 2), with its name reverted to reflect that.
- [ ] Whichever path is chosen, the decision and rationale get recorded in this bead before closing.

title: Apply the ratified automaton portfolio: default-enabled set, profile gates, filter_subjects narrowing
description: Implementation carrier for the 2026-07-08 ratified rulings on pq5 (entity chain retire-until-needed), vfy (wildcard fan-in verdicts), and nbi.4 (16-automaton verdict table). Target end state: default-enabled = canonicalizer, session-detector, hourly-summarizer, daily-summarizer, health, attention-stream, interval-lift (7 of 16). Default-off: analytics, tag-applier (demand-gate: re-enable when a named consumer exists), entity-extractor, entity-resolver, entity-enricher, relation-extractor (re-entry ONLY as 0vx shadow-lane proposals with a named consumer + a4w.1 extractor evaluation), embedding-producer (until 5v6 lands real model effects). Profile-gated: instruction-reconciler (actuation profile), document-parser (document profile, until 68c.2/nbi.3 replace the trigger path). Summarizers get explicit filter_subjects allowlists instead of broad activity wildcards.
design: TARGETS: crate/sinexd/src/automata/registry.rs (default-enabled flags / AutomatonSpec), nixos/modules/lib/automata.nix + nixos/modules/default.nix (per-automaton defaults currently all-true) + nixos/modules/sources.nix (SINEX_AUTOMATA_ENABLED assembly), crate/sinexd/src/supervisor/mod.rs (unset env currently defaults to all — verify the new default set flows through), stream/runner/automaton_runtime.rs automaton_consumer_config (filter_subjects pushdown for the summarizers). RULES: keep code, delete nothing (retire-until-needed, not prune); existing derived rows stay (archive lifecycle applies later if pruned); Nix examples + README updated to show default/profile intent; the events/sec gauge is n23.3 scope — do not build it here, but do not regress its hooks. PITFALL: SINEX_AUTOMATA_ENABLED behavior must match the Nix surface exactly (nbi.4 AC carried this); check both the module AND the Rust env parsing (operator memory: check both NixOS module and Rust).
acceptance_criteria: Default deployment (fresh nix eval, no overrides) runs exactly the 7 keep automata; SINEX_AUTOMATA_ENABLED unset in the supervisor yields the same 7 (not all-16); profile flags exist and enable instruction-reconciler/document-parser; summarizer consumers show narrowed filter_subjects in sinexctl runtime automata output; the confirmed-stream consumer inventory matches the ratified table; nix automata catalog eval + xtask check -p sinexd + targeted automata tests pass; post-deploy verification note records the running set on sinnix-prime.

title: Transport/DB boundary fault-injection matrix: error-return faults (not crashes) across NATS publish/ack and PG failure modes
description: Verifiability-audit gap (2026-07-08, polylogue-methodology parity): r6d.9 is SIGKILL-only by design; nothing systematically injects ERROR-RETURN faults at the NATS/DB boundary and asserts settlement outcomes. The verified bug corpus is exactly this class: wb1 (DLQ publish failure swallowed before settlement), w4i (mid-record emit failure re-emits persisted siblings), r6d.4 window (b) (failed record skipped by later cursor merge), spool publish-fail paths. Each was found by manual reading; the class has no test generator. Faults to inject: NATS publish error, publish timeout, ack/flush failure, JetStream NAK/redelivery storms, PG connection drop mid-batch, serialization failure under REPEATABLE READ, COPY failure mid-chunk (bisect path), DLQ-publish failure itself.
design: Shape: fault-injection layer at the two boundary traits (NATS client wrapper, DB pool/repositories) — an injectable decorator enabled in test builds, driven by a fault schedule (fail Nth call / fail matching subject / fail with timeout vs error). Assertion vocabulary mirrors r6d.9: after the fault clears, either the operation completed exactly-once-per-occurrence, or durable debt/DLQ evidence exists, or the frontier is provably unadvanced — silent skip and duplicate-without-supersession both fail. Priority scenario families: (1) DLQ-publish failure during material-assembler finalize (wb1 repro); (2) mid-record multi-intent emit failure (w4i repro); (3) failed-record-then-successful-later-record cursor merge (r6d.4 window b); (4) COPY bisect with a poison row + a transport fault in the same batch; (5) spool write-then-publish-fail interleavings. Integrates with the r6d.9 harness runner where process restart is needed, but most scenarios are in-process (faster loop). Red-before/green-after: wb1 and w4i are open — their scenarios must FAIL on current master, becoming their close proofs.
acceptance_criteria: Injectable fault layer exists for NATS + DB boundaries in test builds; the five priority families implemented; wb1 and w4i scenarios fail on pre-fix master (recorded) and their fix PRs cite the passing scenarios; fault schedules deterministic; runs under xtask test --heavy; loss-window matrix (r6d.1) gains a fault-injection column linking each row to its scenario or an explicit not-covered entry.

title: Model-based settlement/frontier state-machine tests: arbitrary interleavings over receipt + commit-frontier invariants
description: Verifiability-audit gap (2026-07-08, polylogue-methodology parity): r6d.9 proves NAMED killpoints (18 fixed scenarios); nothing explores ARBITRARY interleavings of the settlement/checkpoint/catch-up state machines. The Batch-A primitives (r6d.11 DurableEmissionReceipt states, 4as3 CommitFrontier ordering) are exactly the shape model-based/stateful property testing is built for: generate random operation sequences (emit, receipt-complete out of order, checkpoint-due, crash, restart, redeliver, NAK, DLQ, invalidation) against a reference model and assert the invariants hold in every reachable state — the fixed-killpoint harness cannot cover the interleaving space and the bugs this class catches (out-of-order receipt completion racing frontier advance) are precisely the ones manual review missed for months.
design: Shape: proptest-state-machine (or hand-rolled operation-sequence generator, deterministic seed, minimized counterexample on failure) at the UNIT level over the r6d.11 receipt state machine and 4as3 CommitFrontier — no DB/NATS needed: the durable backend is a fake with injectable completion order and crash points. Invariants: (1) frontier never advances past a non-terminal atom; (2) no progress-unlocking state reachable from RawAccepted/mpsc-send alone; (3) crash+restart from any persisted state never loses an atom that lacked a terminal receipt (either unadvanced or receipt-proven); (4) out-of-order receipt completion never reorders the FIFO commit frontier; (5) reflection/best-effort API can never unlock progress. Runs as ordinary xtask test (fast, no --heavy). Complements r6d.9 (integration/crash) — this is the exploration layer under it. Sensitivity proof: seed the generator against the CURRENT (pre-r6d.11) mpsc-only emit model and show it finds the checkpoint-outruns-output violation automatically.
acceptance_criteria: Operation-sequence generator + reference model land beside the r6d.11/4as3 primitives; all five invariant families asserted; failure output = minimized sequence + seed; finds the pre-fix checkpoint-before-output violation automatically (recorded once); runs in default xtask test for the owning crates; r6d.11/4as3 closes cite it.

title: Replay-vs-live derivation equivalence differential: whole-derived-plane agreement, occurrence-keyed
description: Verifiability-audit gap (2026-07-08, polylogue-methodology parity): nothing proves that replaying a corpus yields the SAME derived plane the live pipeline produced. pdq5 invariant 5 covers interval sets only; qky is restore economics, not agreement; r6d.9 proves crash recovery, not derivation determinism. Replay is the load-bearing repair mechanism (fix-forward doctrine: archive cascade + replay), so a silent replay/live divergence — nondeterministic automaton state, ordering-dependent equivalence keys (the ecy class), rule-version skew — corrupts the derived plane exactly when the operator is trying to repair it. Regression-leak likelihood: HIGH once replay is exercised routinely; today it would be discovered only by manual adversarial reading.
design: Shape: e2e differential in tests/e2e (or xtask sandbox scenario). (1) Ingest a fixed synthetic + sampled-real material corpus through the NORMAL pipeline with all default automata; snapshot the derived plane. (2) Fire a full replay cascade over the same materials. (3) Diff the two derived planes keyed by OCCURRENCE identity (source_material_id, anchor_byte) + event_type + semantics_version — interpretation ids and ts_coided are expected to differ (identity model doctrine); payload content, parent occurrence sets, and derived-interval boundaries must not. Divergence report = machine-readable (kind, occurrence key, field-level diff). Known-legitimate divergence classes (rule version bumped between runs) must be declared, not silently allowed. Interacts: ecy (counter keys are a known determinism bug this suite would have caught — use it as the red-before/green-after sensitivity proof), 908 (regime matrix defines what MUST dedup), n9a (supersession changes the diff contract: compare live rows, archived rows both runs). Wire into impact planner so automaton/replay-touching diffs select it.
acceptance_criteria: Differential harness runs under xtask test --heavy; occurrence-keyed diff over ALL derived kinds, not just intervals; proves sensitivity by red-flagging the ecy counter-key defect on pre-fix code and going green after; legitimate-divergence declarations are explicit in the fixture; divergence report is machine-readable and cited by replay-touching bead closes.

title: Cross-provider observation fusion: one canonical composite from multiple witnesses of the same occurrence
description: The purpose sleep-merged actually served — and the general mechanism sinex was missing. When two independent providers witness the SAME real-world episode (Samsung Health and Sleep As Android both record tonight's sleep: SaA carries phase structure, Samsung carries HR-during-sleep), downstream consumers need ONE canonical composite observation, not two overlapping fragments to re-reconcile at every query. This is NOT dedup (908 regime-2 suppresses identical occurrences; here the witnesses are complementary) and NOT rollup (k4c.2 aggregates within a domain-day; here we merge witnesses of one episode). The sinex-native shape: a declared fusion derivation (0vx product class canonical_derived_event) consuming both providers' typed events as parents, matching witnesses by episode identity (time-overlap + subject rules declared per fusion kind; ambiguous matches -> regime-3 adjudication per 908), emitting the composite (sleep.night with phases from SaA + HR/physio from Samsung) with: per-field provenance (which witness supplied which field), a declared conflict policy per field (prefer-provider / widest-interval / adjudicate on disagreement beyond tolerance), agreement level reflected in ClaimSupport (two agreeing witnesses raise source_coverage — the vector was built for exactly this), and LATE-WITNESS revision via the y8v/n9a contract: fused output keys on the episode occurrence, so when the second provider's export arrives weeks later the composite SUPERSEDES rather than duplicates or freezes. First instance: sleep.night over rkv.6 events. Same declared shape later serves weight-from-multiple-scales, location-from-multiple-devices, calendar-from-multiple-accounts. Lynchpin's sleep_merged_summary.csv is parity evidence for the sleep instance, never an input.
acceptance_criteria: Fusion derivations are declared data (witness kinds, episode-matching rule, per-field conflict policy, tolerance thresholds) not bespoke automata per pair; sleep.night lands as the first instance with per-field provenance and both parents; single-witness nights emit with honest single-witness ClaimSupport (no fabricated completeness); disagreement beyond tolerance emits an adjudication candidate, never a silent pick; a late second-provider import supersedes the single-witness composite exactly once (n9a fixture); parity vs lynchpin sleep_merged day-slice recorded in the k4c.3 ledger; baselines/rollups consume sleep.night, not raw fragments.

title: Web-history backfill from raw artifacts: Takeout Chrome JSON + historical browser-DB snapshots
description: OPERATOR CONSTRAINT (named example): lynchpin's unified web-history format is exactly what NOT to absorb. The raw substrate exists: /realm/data/captures/webhistory/browser-dbs/ (historical browser SQLite snapshots collected by chrome-snapshot.sh and friends) and Takeout Chrome BrowserHistory.json under exports/google/. Sinex already has the LIVE browser.history contract (MutableSnapshot over the live DB); this bead adds the HISTORICAL horizon: (1) snapshot-DB import — each archived browser DB registered as material, visits parsed with provider row identity (Chrome visit ids + urls table) as occurrence anchors so overlapping snapshots of the same profile deduplicate by occurrence, not by lynchpin-side unification; (2) Takeout Chrome JSON import with its native record shape. Cross-artifact overlap (same visit in a snapshot DB AND in Takeout) is the regime-2/3 boundary case from the 908 matrix: same provider visit id -> occurrence-suppress; id-less near-matches -> adjudication candidates, never silent merge. The unified-history question ('one browsing timeline across browsers/eras') is answered downstream by QUERY/derivation over typed visit events — unification is a read-model concern, never an ingest format.
acceptance_criteria: Historical snapshot DBs and Takeout Chrome JSON ingest as materials with provider-native anchors; overlapping snapshots dedupe by provider visit identity (fixture with two snapshots sharing a day); cross-artifact same-visit pairs suppress on id match and route to adjudication otherwise; a cross-era browsing-timeline query works over typed events with no unified intermediate anywhere; parity vs lynchpin sources.web day-counts recorded in the ledger; no reference to lynchpin web formats in source code (rg-verified).

title: Google Takeout product family: raw-archive import (My Activity, Keep, contacts, YouTube, purchases)
description: Lynchpin's google_takeout_products module reads its own 'canonical product NDJSON' — a lynchpin intermediate that is NOT a porting target. Sinex ingests the ORIGINAL Takeout archives under /realm/data/exports/google/: per-product parsers over the provider's native formats (My Activity JSON/HTML per product, Keep JSON notes, contacts VCF/CSV, YouTube watch/search history, purchases, Play Store, Tasks), each archive file registered as-is as source material, anchors = record offsets within the provider file, ts_orig = provider-intrinsic timestamps, occurrence identity per the 908 dedup matrix export-re-import row (provider record ids + content hash where Takeout lacks ids — Takeout re-exports OVERLAP heavily, so equivalence keys are load-bearing here). Delivery through the stm static-import glue; per catalog extension rule 1 every product names its consumer derivation (My Activity -> attention/web joins; Keep -> knowledgebase/entity plane; contacts -> person entity a4w.3.13; YouTube -> media consumption). Products land incrementally — one parser per PR — behind one contract family. Lynchpin NDJSON is parity evidence ONLY.
acceptance_criteria: Contract family registered with per-product parsers over raw archive files; materials point at original Takeout paths; a re-imported overlapping archive produces zero duplicate occurrences (fixture); each landed product has a parity note vs the lynchpin NDJSON (counts per day-slice) recorded in the k4c.3 ledger; no code path reads lynchpin canonical NDJSON; at least My Activity + Keep + contacts landed for the first slice, remaining products enumerated in the ledger with verdicts.

title: Judgment staleness: accepted claims re-open when their evidence is superseded
description: pf54 handles PRE-acceptance proposals; nothing handles POST-acceptance: an operator judgment (accepted claim, entity merge, lane promotion) sits on evidence that supersession (n9a), replay, redaction, or purge (new8) can archive or replace — after which the system keeps presenting the judgment as if its grounds were intact. Knowledge-decay honesty: when the evidence set under an accepted judgment changes materially, the judgment is FLAGGED stale-evidence (never silently reverted — the operator's ruling stands until the operator revisits), the ClaimSupport adjudication axis gains a stale-evidence state rendered by the design-language support scale, dependent surfaces caveat it, and the judgment seat offers a low-priority revisit queue (subject to the pf54 no-nag budget). Mechanically this rides the same cascade: the supersession/replay invalidation walk (68c.4 / n9a) already touches descendants — extend the walk to judgment events whose evidence refs intersect the invalidated set.
acceptance_criteria: Supersession/replay/purge cascades mark judgments whose evidence refs intersect the affected set; stale-evidence is a typed adjudication state, rendered distinctly (never as reduced confidence); affected read surfaces caveat claims resting on stale-evidence judgments; revisit queue in the seat ordered by impact, respecting the pf54 budget; the operator ruling is never auto-reverted; fixture: accept a claim, supersede its evidence via n9a, observe flag + caveat + revisit entry with the original ruling intact.

title: Moment marks: zero-friction operator capture of 'this matters, now'
description: The cheapest highest-leverage capture that does not exist: a global hotkey (Hyprland bind -> tiny client) that mints an operator.mark event in under a second, carrying refs to the current context (active window/attention state, foreground terminal, optional 3-second annotation via a popup or voice-note file anchor). Zero friction is the whole spec — if it takes more than a keystroke it will not be used mid-flow. Why it is load-bearing beyond convenience: (1) marks are ground-truth salience labels — the a4w.1 golden fixture and uxd salience training get real 'operator said this mattered' datapoints for free; (2) marks are first-class recall anchors ('jump to my last mark' in recall/TUI); (3) mark+intent composes with tripwires ('mark this and remind me when I am back at the desk'); (4) marks during agent sessions become adjudication shortcuts. Doctrine: marks are operator-provenance events on the activity lane with material anchors to a tiny capture record; no interpretation at capture time.
acceptance_criteria: Hotkey-to-persisted-mark latency under 1s live (measured); mark events carry context refs resolvable via show (active attention span/window state at mark time); optional annotation path (text popup and/or audio-note file registered as material); recall/TUI expose jump-to-mark and marks-in-window; a4w.1 fixture schema accepts marks as salience evidence; a mark+remind compound arms a tripwire in one flow; the whole surface obeys the design language.

title: Prospective memory: context tripwires — intents that resurface when the moment matches
description: The exocortex currently only remembers BACKWARD; an exocortex worth the name also carries the operator's declared future intents and resurfaces them at the contextually right moment — which is exactly what AuDHD working memory drops. Mechanism (all existing planes, no new machinery classes): a tripwire = operator-declared (trigger-pattern, payload-intent, expiry, arm/disarm state) stored as events; a ScopeReconciler-family automaton (the instruction_reconciler shape — desired-state loops already exist for instructions) watches the confirmed stream for pattern matches (attention.span subject matches repo X; source family becomes active; an entity appears; a calendar block starts) and emits a tripwire.fired candidate; delivery respects the o6w notification budget — fired tripwires surface in Today's Resume pane and optionally one push if the operator marked it urgent, never a nag queue. Examples: 'when I next open sinex repo, surface: the y8v veto window'; 'when person P appears in comms, surface: the thing I owe them'; 'if no commit lands in repo Y for 14 days, surface once'. Distinct from 7z0.9 (events trigger AGENT sessions): tripwires trigger OPERATOR resurfacing; 7z0.9 can later consume the same trigger grammar. Distinct from 9cs (predictions): tripwires are intents, not forecasts. Expiry/decay follows the pf54 philosophy: expired tripwires retire to a queryable dormant state with zero noise.
acceptance_criteria: Tripwire declaration surface (CLI + capture-time affordance) with trigger grammar covering at least: subject-match on attention/interval events, entity appearance, source-activity threshold, calendar-block start, and quiet-period (no-event-for-N-days); the watcher automaton emits tripwire.fired with evidence refs to the matching moment; fired intents render in Today/Resume with the original declaration and one-key done/snooze/retire; notification budget honored (push only if declared urgent); expiry retires silently to a queryable dormant list; fixture proves a subject-match tripwire fires exactly once per arm cycle and never after retirement.

title: Pack assembly pipeline: staged, budgeted, caveat-carrying — the seam the budget arbiter plugs into
description: Recall/context assembly is 2,131 lines of hand-rolled staging (commands/context.rs): each stage repeats the tokio::timeout + stage_caveats push + degrade pattern inline (:329-379, :563-581, lineage :652-728), stage budgets are ad-hoc constants, and 7z0.6 (context-budget arbiter) has NO seam to plug into — it would have to edit every stage by hand. The abstraction: PackPipeline = ordered Stage<T> list where a Stage declares {fetch fn, time budget, token/row budget, degrade-caveat id, required|optional}; the runner enforces budgets, collects per-stage caveats and timings into the envelope uniformly, and exposes the build manifest (what ran, what degraded, what was dropped and why) that the coherence review demanded for agent surfaces. recall, context-pack, Today (7z0.1), and orient become pipeline declarations sharing one runner; the budget arbiter becomes a policy handed to the runner instead of code spread across surfaces; x69's bounded diversity top-up becomes a stage swap.
acceptance_criteria: Pipeline type + runner land in sinexctl (or shared view layer); recall and context_pack are migrated as declarations with behavior-identical output plus a build manifest in the envelope; per-stage timeout/caveat/timing boilerplate is deleted from context.rs; 7z0.6 consumes the runner policy hook; Today builds on the same runner; stage budgets are visible in query_echo/manifest.

title: Automaton lifecycle consolidation: one prepare/emit/receipt/commit core under capability flags
description: Four wrapper impls bridge Transducer/Windowed/ScopeReconciler(+adapter automata) onto one Automaton trait through ~800 lines of per-wrapper glue (runtime/automaton/traits.rs), and four in-flight campaigns must each thread through every path: the vxu prepare/commit split, r6d.11 receipt awaiting, 5s6 watermark/timer flush, and 0vx.2 DerivedOutput declaration metadata. Without consolidation each campaign is a 4x cross-product patch (the timer_flush_derived default-empty + per-wrapper override pattern shows the drift shape already). Target: ONE core lifecycle — accumulate(input) -> prepare(outputs) -> emit(awaiting receipts per r6d.11) -> commit(frontier per the CommitFrontier primitive) — with model differences expressed as capability flags/policies (windowing, flush_due, scope keying) instead of parallel wrapper code paths. This is COORDINATION architecture: land the core as part of the first campaign that needs it (vxu or r6d.11), then the other campaigns are single-path changes. Explicitly not a big-bang rewrite — the wrappers shrink to adapters over the core incrementally.
design:
DESIGN (2026-07-08, written with the full ratified Batch-A context; receipt backend (A) is FINAL so this can be concrete):

CORE LIFECYCLE — one generic engine, four strict phases:
1. ACCUMULATE: feed inputs to the model; may mutate only the IN-MEMORY working set; watermark evaluation (5s6 two-mode) happens here and may set flush_due. No progress marks, no persisted-state writes.
2. PREPARE: pure. Produces PreparedOutputs (typed events + 0vx.2 DerivedOutput declarations attached here) + a StateDelta (e.g. accumulator reset, window close) + a ProgressAtom description (AutomatonInputBatch or WindowFlush{flush_id}). MUST NOT mutate persisted state — the session.rs:171/analytics.rs:293 mem::take-at-emit bug class is structurally impossible because take moves to COMMIT.
3. EMIT: submit PreparedOutputs via the r6d.11 emit_batch_durable API; await receipt per progress atom. NoOutputSettled is an explicit receipt (benign no-output paths need durable evidence per vxu AC).
4. COMMIT: only on receipt.unlocks_progress(): apply StateDelta to persisted state, mark inputs processed via CommitFrontier (4as3), checkpoint = frontier payload. Crash before commit = inputs unadvanced, replay re-derives; crash after = outputs provably durable. This IS the vxu reorder, implemented once.

CAPABILITY FLAGS (per model, replacing per-wrapper glue): has_accumulator (Windowed), has_timer_flush (mints flush_id in PREPARE; flush StateDelta applies at COMMIT), scope_recompute (ScopeReconciler: invalidation delivery handles retained until settlement — r6d.7 falls out of the core), historical_catchup. manages_own_checkpoints DISAPPEARS: checkpoint payload is always the CommitFrontier state, so the bridge-KV-inert-for-adapters split (vxu headline) is dissolved rather than patched.

MIGRATION ORDER: Transducer first (stateless; prepare = transform — cheapest behavior-identical proof), then Windowed (accumulate + timer flush + shutdown barrier), then ScopeReconciler (invalidation atoms). Shutdown = drain: refuse final state save past unsettled receipts; final checkpoint covers only the committed frontier (replaces adapter/mod.rs:545-574 save-regardless).

INTERLOCKS: vxu + r6d.7 + 5s6 flush + 0vx.2 declarations all become route-through-the-core rather than 4x wrapper patches (that is the point of this bead); 87hu model-based tests exercise the phase machine directly. PITFALL: one wrapper per PR with behavior-identical tests; do not migrate all three at once.
acceptance_criteria: Core lifecycle type exists; at least two of the three wrapper paths route through it with behavior-identical tests; the vxu prepare/commit split and receipt awaiting are implemented ONCE in the core (wrapper-specific emit/state mutation deleted for migrated paths); capability flags documented; remaining wrapper migration tracked with no dual-maintenance of lifecycle logic for migrated models.

title: Schema expectation IR: apply, converge, strict-diff, and docs as interpreters of one typed source of truth
description: The schema engine is three hand-maintained interpretations of the same defs — apply.rs 2,972 + converge.rs 1,401 + strict_diff.rs 996 lines — and they VERIFIABLY drift: strict_diff's comments claim CREATE-OR-REPLACE-every-apply while apply skips on trigger-name existence (the lcj finding); strict DbCheck accepts extra literals that apply rejects (a 2pl finding); diff covers required-index subsets apply repairs silently. Every new invariant is currently written 2-3 times and the copies disagree. The abstraction: a typed Expectation IR generated from defs (TableExpectation, ColumnExpectation{type,generated,default,collation}, ConstraintExpectation{normalized body}, IndexExpectation{def,predicate,opclass}, TriggerExpectation{timing,mask,when,target,enabled}, FunctionExpectation{def hash}, PolicyExpectation{retention,compression,cagg refresh}) — with apply=make-true interpreter, strict-diff=verify-true interpreter, converge=delta subset, docs=render, over the SAME expectations. 2pl and lcj should be implemented AS the verify interpreter over new expectation kinds, not as more hand-enumerated checks — one mechanism, drift class structurally impossible.
acceptance_criteria: Expectation IR types exist with defs generating them; strict-diff runs as the verify interpreter over the IR for at least the surfaces 2pl/lcj require; apply consumes the same expectations for those surfaces (no parallel hand lists remain for migrated surfaces); a deliberate def change flows to apply+strict+docs from one edit; the stale strict_diff comments are gone because the code can no longer diverge from apply for migrated surfaces.
notes: 2026-07-08 verifiability audit rider: the IR bead should include an interpreter-equivalence differential as part of its AC evidence — apply-from-scratch onto an empty DB vs converge-from-an-arbitrarily-drifted DB must yield identical live introspection digests (pg_dump-normalized or catalog-query digest). That differential is what makes apply/converge/strict-diff-as-interpreters falsifiable and kills the lcj class (comments claiming one behavior while code does another) structurally: the digest test cannot lie. sinex-schema is the thinnest suite in the workspace relative to criticality (23 test fns / 12.7k LOC vs sinexd 1968 / 167k, measured 2026-07-08) — the IR interpreters are where that deficit gets repaid.

title: Tombstone/purge cascade completeness: one operator intent, every surface
description: The lifecycle surface (Live -> Archive -> Tombstone) exists, but the 09 privacy audit verified the cascade is incomplete: the tombstone path deletes audit.archived_events but NOT the archived side tables (archived_annotations/_embeddings/_tagged_items — defs/events.rs:908-1015, apply.rs:2005-2164); document_chunks (ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) and the email mailbox projection retain content unless explicitly rebuilt; CAS blobs, embeddings/cache, and semantic lane outputs are untouched. cem.1/r6d.2 REPORT these residuals; nothing FIXES the cascade. Scope: purge-one-thing (operator points at an event/material/blob) cascades through every DB surface the privacy_invalidation_scope enumerates as mutable, uses the projection-staleness path (68c.4) for rebuildable read models, and prints an honest residual report for the immutable/out-of-band surfaces (NATS retention windows, spool, journald, backups) with time-to-expiry where known. This is regime-3-adjacent UX: destructive, so the command previews the full cascade and requires confirmation; the executed purge is itself recorded in the operations log (the act is durable even when the content is not).
acceptance_criteria: One command previews then executes the full cascade for a given ref: live row, archived readings, archived side tables, chunks, mailbox projection rows, KG rows/annotations/tags, embeddings, lane outputs; rebuildables marked stale via the registry; residual report lists unreachable surfaces with expiry horizons; the purge operation is logged with scope and actor; fixture: planted item purged, per-surface zero-residual checks pass, residual report matches the 09 matrix expectations.

title: Judgment-queue debt policy: proposals age, evidence supersedes, queues stay honest without nagging
description: The curation plane assumes the operator judges proposals, and the no-nag rule (correctly) forbids pressure — but nothing specifies what happens when proposals sit for weeks: unbounded queue growth, judgments over stale evidence, and a judgment seat that opens onto 400 items (guaranteeing avoidance).
