Policy to design: (1) EVIDENCE-DRIVEN INVALIDATION — a proposal whose underlying evidence was superseded/archived/replayed is automatically re-validated or withdrawn-with-record (never silently mutated; the withdrawal is itself an event); (2) AGING — proposals carry age and evidence-freshness in the seat's card; ordering favors decision-ready over merely-old; (3) QUEUE BUDGET — a declared soft cap where the system stops GENERATING low-support proposals (generation backpressure, e.g. raising the proposal threshold) rather than nagging the operator to drain (aligns 2k2 cost-fuse philosophy); (4) EXPIRY DECISION — whether never-judged proposals expire to a durable dormant state after a declared horizon (reversible, queryable) or live forever — operator ruling; (5) metrics: queue depth, median age, withdrawal rate visible in the seat header and reflection lane, never as badges elsewhere.
acceptance_criteria: Policy doc + schema/runtime hooks: superseded-evidence proposals auto-withdraw with a recorded cause; seat cards show age + evidence freshness; generation backpressure engages at the declared budget with a caveat, no operator-facing nag anywhere; expiry ruling recorded; queue metrics in seat header + reflection lane; fixture: supersede evidence under a pending proposal and observe withdrawal, not a stale judgment.

title: Archive growth model and archive read surface: supersession multiplies history — budget it and make it readable
description: audit.archived_events grows with every replay AND (post-n9a) every supersession — the AW grown-duration case alone can archive one reading per re-read cycle per interval. State-is-durable doctrine forbids wiping, but nothing models or bounds growth, and nothing lets the operator READ the history ('what did the system used to believe about X, and when did it change?') outside raw SQL — cem.14 renders one case but no general surface exists. Scope: (1) growth model — measured bytes/rows per replay campaign and per supersession class, projected against store growth, recorded thresholds for when coalescing matters; (2) compaction DECISION — whether repeated near-identical superseded readings of the same occurrence may be coalesced (keeping first/last/change-points) or archives stay verbatim forever (either answer recorded with rationale; provenance completeness is the bar); (3) archive read surface — show <ref> gains an interpretations view listing live + archived readings of an occurrence with supersession timestamps and causes, and lifecycle gains archive stats; (4) w98 backup/scrub covers archive tables explicitly.
acceptance_criteria: Growth measurements committed with projections; coalescing decision recorded (design + operator veto window); show renders the interpretation history of an occurrence (live + archived + cause + timestamps); lifecycle stats include per-class archive volumes; w98 AC references archive coverage; cem.14 consumes the new surface instead of raw SQL.

title: Interval invariant property suite: simulation-tested semantics for the interval/session stack
description: r6d.9 tests CRASH windows; nothing tests interval SEMANTICS systematically — zs6 (heartbeat chop), uzc (tie drops), and the closure defects were all found by manual adversarial reading. A property/simulation harness generates synthetic evidence streams (transitions, heartbeats with gaps/duplicates/reorderings/ties, start-stop pairs with missing counterparts, suspend fences, replay re-deliveries) and asserts invariants over the emitted interval set: (1) per (state_kind, subject) live intervals are non-overlapping; (2) coverage conservation — total intervalized time never exceeds evidenced wall time per stream and gaps beyond the declared slack are never intervalized; (3) watermark monotonicity — no emission moves an already-closed boundary except via supersession; (4) supersession sanity — at most one live interpretation per occurrence key, superseded readings archived, no orphaned descendants; (5) replay equivalence — replaying the same synthetic stream yields the same interval SET by occurrence key (new interpretation ids allowed); (6) tie/out-of-order inputs never silently vanish (interval, debt, or explicit no-op accounting). Runs as ordinary xtask tests with deterministic seeds (seed printed on failure).
acceptance_criteria: Generator + invariant checker land in the automata test tree behind xtask test; all six invariant families implemented; the suite red-flags the CURRENT zs6/uzc defects before their fixes (proving sensitivity) and goes green after; failure output includes the minimized offending stream and seed; wired into the impact planner so interval-touching diffs select it.

title: Demo: import it twice — occurrence idempotence with a receipt
description: The trust demo for regime 2: run the same real export (e.g. a Takeout slice or chat export) through import twice. First run: N events, coverage delta. Second run: ZERO new live rows, and the idempotence report says exactly what happened — M suppressed duplicates with example refs, K superseded revisions if the export grew, J adjudication candidates if identity was ambiguous. Then the contrast shot that teaches the doctrine: REPLAY the same slice and show it is NOT idempotent on purpose — new interpretation ids, new ts_coided, old readings archived, occurrence coordinates unchanged. One demo, both regimes, the whole identity model made visible. Depends on the dedup UX report (08z) and the matrix (908).
acceptance_criteria: One-command packet: import -> report; re-import -> zero-new report with suppression accounting; replay -> new interpretations with archive evidence; a table contrasts the three runs by (live rows, archived rows, ids minted, occurrence keys). Negative control: a genuinely-new record added to the second import is admitted and reported as new. Cold-reader rerun instructions; all claims backed by runnable queries.

title: TUI interaction design: focus model, keybinding grammar, judgment-seat ergonomics
description: The TUI is a workbench of tabs today (tui.rs:72-84) with ad-hoc keys and no designed focus model. Deliverable: an interaction spec + implementation aligning the TUI with the charter and design language: (1) KEYBINDING GRAMMAR: one consistent scheme (vim-family motions; verbs j=judge, d=defer, y=copy-ref, ?=explain-caveat, g/G time jumps) declared in a single keymap table rendered by ? — no per-tab surprise bindings. (2) FOCUS MODEL: pane focus, selection, and drill-down defined once (list -> card -> evidence -> show), with breadcrumbs and O(1) escape back to Today; every drill-down is interruption-safe (return restores exact position). (3) JUDGMENT SEAT (7z0.4 surface): card layout from the design-language mock; one-decision-per-card with visible batch progress ('3 of 9', never a shrinking debt count); defer requires one key and a reason picker; session exit keeps all progress. (4) TIME NAVIGATION: the timeline idiom becomes interactive — anchor jump, window widen/narrow, open-interval live edge visibly provisional. (5) LIVE EDGE: SSE-fed panes mark data age; stale panes say so rather than freezing silently.
acceptance_criteria: Interaction spec committed; keymap table single-sourced and rendered by ?; focus/breadcrumb model implemented across tabs; judgment-seat card flow matches the mock with defer-first ergonomics; timeline pane supports anchor/zoom with provisional live edge; staleness indicators on live panes; charter journey walkthroughs (resume + judgment) pass their step budgets in the TUI.
notes: Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.

title: Design language: visual semantics, affordance grammar, and aesthetics for CLI/TUI/report surfaces
description: UI design proper (operator-requested): Sinex surfaces should be coherent and genuinely pleasant — an exocortex the operator wants to look at — without decoration that lies. Deliverable: a design-language spec (crate/sinexctl/docs/design-language.md) + a shared rendering module, covering: (1) COLOR/WEIGHT SEMANTICS: one palette where color encodes STATUS CLASS only (ok/degraded/absent/stale/provisional/error) and never source identity; ClaimSupport renders as a typed glyph+shade scale that visually cannot be confused with certainty (support is evidence-shaped, not a progress bar); honest-gap rendering is a first-class visual element (hatched/dimmed span with cause glyph), not whitespace. (2) AFFORDANCE GRAMMAR: everything copyable/actionable is visually marked — resolvable refs render with their show-command affordance, dead refs render inert with non_clickable_reason (bm1); interval/timeline widgets distinguish closed / open-provisional / superseded-archived states (y8v vocabulary made visible); every table that truncates says so in-band. (3) DENSITY MODES: compact (agent/pipe), standard, and review (wide, whitespace, for the weekly journey) — chosen by context not flags where possible. (4) TIMELINE IDIOMS: the canonical way to draw intervals, gaps, fences, overlaps, and revisions in monospace — specified once, used by recall, Today, TUI events, and report daily. (5) AESTHETIC STANCE: restrained, high-signal, terminal-native (no box-drawing soup); one accent; motion only for live tails. Mockups: ANSI/ASCII mocks for Today, recall pack, judgment card, dedup report, and a revised-interval timeline committed alongside the spec.
acceptance_criteria: design-language.md committed with palette/status table, ClaimSupport visual scale, gap/interval/revision idioms, affordance grammar, density modes, and the five mockups; a shared render helper (single module) implements the palette + glyph set so surfaces cannot drift; two existing surfaces (recall table + sources status) are converted as the proof and screenshot-diffed in the PR; no surface renders confidence as certainty; TUI/CLI/report all draw intervals via the shared idiom.
notes: Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.

title: Dedup observability and re-import UX: suppression must be visible, imports must report idempotence
description: VERIFIED: admission suppression is metered internally (event_engine.admission_suppressions_total, prepare.rs:277-316) but NO operator surface exposes it — suppressed occurrences are invisible except in telemetry counters, and a re-import gives no account of what it did. With SupersedeOnChange (n9a) landing, three outcome classes (suppressed / superseded / admitted-new) need operator-visible accounting or the dedup machinery is unauditable. UX deliverables: (1) per-source/per-type suppression+supersession counts in sinexctl sources status and a dedicated dedup report (windowed, with example refs); (2) IMPORT IDEMPOTENCE REPORT: any import/scan run ends with 'N new, M suppressed duplicates, K superseded revisions, J adjudication candidates' — the operator's answer to 'I imported the same Takeout twice, what happened?'; (3) regime-3 adjudication queue: ambiguous dup candidates enter the curation-proposal plane (existing judgment seat 7z0.4 — no new surface) with side-by-side evidence and a durable, reversible merge/distinct judgment; (4) suppression caveat: read surfaces covering a window where suppression was abnormal surface it as a caveat, not silence.
acceptance_criteria: sources status + dedup report expose suppressed/superseded/admitted counts per source and event type with example refs; every import/scan CLI run prints the idempotence report line; ambiguous candidates flow through curation proposals with evidence panes and reversible judgments; a fixture imports the same export twice and the report shows all-suppressed with zero new live rows; ViewEnvelope caveat fires on abnormal suppression rates.
notes: Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.

title: Open-state visibility: the current (unclosed) interval/session/episode must be readable
description: Open accumulators live only in automaton checkpoint state — invisible to the event store, recall, and every read surface (recall's session_rows_absent caveat is the symptom). a4w.3.4 declares work.episode THE unit of resumption with boundaries = session detector output, but the CURRENT episode (the one you would resume) by construction cannot exist until it is over: resumption demos therefore score reconstruction of FINISHED work, a different construct. 7z0.1's Resume pane needs the open episode. Options: expose open automaton state via a read-side RPC + ProjectionReadinessView row (status=building), or emit periodic still-open snapshot markers to the reflection lane. Do NOT emit provisional canonical events (would need revision machinery). Lens: construct validity of resumption.
acceptance_criteria: A read surface (RPC + recall/Today integration) renders the current open session/attention state with an explicit open/provisional marker and the evidence refs accumulated so far; canonical event-store rows are NOT minted for open states; recall's session_rows_absent case distinguishes 'no session' from 'session open'; the resumption duel (cem.7) can include open-episode trials.

title: Clock-driven closure and suspend/boot fences: interval ends must not require future activity
description: VERIFIED closure-by-next-arrival across the whole stack: analytics Gap fires only when the NEXT event arrives (analytics.rs accumulate gap check); flush_due defaults false and only hourly/daily override it (traits.rs:249, hourly.rs:178, daily.rs:181) so no clock closes an activity window or session; SessionDetector completes only on a Gap-closed window (session.rs:125-143); interval_lift transitions close only on the next transition (interval_lift.rs:664-681). Consequences: the final interval/session of any activity bout is unqueryable until future activity; suspend/shutdown periods are attributed to the last open state (focus 23:59 + next focus 09:00 = 9h focus interval); AFK evidence does not fence focus/workspace kinds (cross-silo). CAUTION: wall-clock flush must be watermark-aware — during historical replay/backfill, input-time watermarks, not wall time, decide gaps, or replay would chop historical continuity. Lens: composability (silos: AFK vs focus) + construct validity (interval end means 'next event arrived', not 'state ended').
design: TWO-MODE WATERMARK (settled design): WatermarkMode::Live — watermark = max(max_input_ts_orig, now - idle_grace); advances with wall clock; flush_due closes an accumulator when watermark - last_evidence >= gap_threshold. WatermarkMode::Catchup — watermark = max input ts_orig ONLY (no wall-clock advance), so historical replay/backfill can never chop continuity by processing delay. Mode source: the consumer catch-up/lag state the bridge already tracks — Live only when lag < declared threshold; any transition Catchup->Live re-evaluates open accumulators against the new watermark. Fences (suspend/resume, boot, AFK-state transitions per the cross-kind fencing table) are INPUT events, valid in both modes, and close at the fence ts_orig — never at processing time.
acceptance_criteria: flush_due (input-time-watermark-aware) closes analytics windows and sessions after gap_threshold of quiet without requiring a next event; live mode uses wall clock as the watermark, replay/catch-up uses max input ts_orig. Suspend/resume and boot fence events close all open desktop interval kinds at the fence (systemd sleep.target/boot as fence inputs). AFK-state transitions fence focus/workspace intervals per a declared cross-kind fencing table. Fixture: activity then silence -> window+session close within one flush period; suspend mid-focus -> focus interval ends at suspend, not next-morning focus.
notes:
2026-07-07: watermark design settled; implementation follows the y8v/ecy identity work so closed-by-clock intervals carry occurrence-stable keys from day one.
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.

title: ActivityWatch mutable rows never re-read: durations freeze at scan time despite MutableSnapshot declaration
description: VERIFIED: the AW source declares CheckpointFamily::MutableSnapshot with occurrence_anchor bucket_event_timestamp (activitywatch.rs:131) but the SQL reads ORDER BY events.id under a ROWID cursor (activitywatch.rs:308). AW's storage model extends the newest event row's endtime in place via heartbeats — once a still-growing row is ingested, its extension is permanently missed. Ingested durations are systematically short-biased on the freshest rows of every scan. The contract declaration promises mutation-awareness the query does not implement. Lens: construct validity (captured 'duration' = duration-at-scan-moment) + false contract declaration.
acceptance_criteria: The source re-reads a trailing mutability window (rows whose endtime could still grow, bounded by AW heartbeat semantics) or defers ingesting rows younger than a settling threshold; the chosen mechanism is recorded in the contract. Re-observed grown rows flow through the interval revision contract (supersession/correction per the decision bead), never as silent overlapping duplicates. Fixture: grow a row's endtime after first scan; the final stored interval reflects the grown duration exactly once.
notes:
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.
2026-07-08: this bead formally carries the y8v ruling fixture — its close must demonstrate the AW grown-duration case end-to-end (grow endtime after first scan -> exactly one live interval with grown duration, superseded reading in audit.archived_events, descendants re-derived via invalidation).
2026-07-08 session: investigated, not implemented. Confirmed on master: activitywatch.rs source SQL query (line 308 doc comment / actual driver query) is ORDER BY events.id with a ROWID ratchet cursor — no trailing-mutability re-read window exists. The fix requires touching the SAME generic adapter cursor machinery (runtime/parser/adapter_source.rs, the ~2100-line file at the center of the r6d.4/4as3 chain) to add a per-source-configurable 'settling window' (re-scan rows whose endtime could still grow, or defer ingesting rows younger than a threshold), PLUS route re-observed grown rows through the interval revision contract (sinex-y8v, settled design) for supersession rather than duplicate insertion. Given r6d.4/vxu's reorder work against adapter_source.rs was independently assessed this session as too large to rush (see r6d.11 notes), deferring this too rather than making an uncoordinated second change to the same hot file. Recommend sequencing h3g AFTER the r6d.4 checkpoint-frontier reorder lands, not before or concurrently — a settling-window feature added to the OLD merge_cursor_update mechanism would need to be re-done once that mechanism is deleted per 4as3's design ('this deletes merge_cursor_update recursive JSON overlay rather than wrapping it').
2026-07-09 session: re-verified the deferral judgment with a precise mechanism-level proof,
since the bd DEPENDS ON graph shows only sinex-y8v (already closed) as a hard blocker — the
"blocked on r6d.4" framing from the prior session note was a soft sequencing judgment, not a
formal dependency, so it deserved re-checking rather than taking at face value.

CONFIRMED: a naive fix scoped entirely within activitywatch.rs (e.g. adding
`WHERE events.endtime < (now - settling_threshold)` to the source's own base_query, so
still-growing rows are simply not selected until they age in) is NOT a safe, source-local fix
— it introduces a NEW skip-forever bug via the shared cursor mechanism.

Traced the exact wrapping (crate/sinexd/src/runtime/parser/adapters/sqlite_row.rs:392-407):
the generic adapter wraps ANY source's base_query as
`SELECT {rowid_col}, * FROM ({base_query}) WHERE {rowid_col} > ?1 ORDER BY {rowid_col} ASC
LIMIT {batch_size}` — a STRICT, monotonic ratchet over rowid, one global sequence across ALL
of AW's buckets (events JOIN buckets, single SQLite rowid space). AW runs multiple independent
bucket watchers concurrently (window-watcher, afk-watcher, browser-watcher at minimum) whose
inserts interleave in rowid order. If a source-local recency filter excludes a still-growing
row (bucket X's newest insert) from base_query's result set, but the SAME scan includes an
older, already-settled row from a DIFFERENT bucket (bucket Y) that happens to have a HIGHER
rowid (entirely normal — bucket Y just hasn't inserted anything recently, its last row is old;
bucket X just inserted its latest growing row) — the cursor advances to that higher rowid.
Once advanced, the excluded row (bucket X's, lower rowid) can NEVER be re-selected by ANY
future scan, even after it settles, because `rowid > ?1` permanently excludes it. This is
exactly the "occurrence permanently skipped despite never being read" bug class this whole
r6d epic exists to prevent — just found via reasoning before writing the code, not via a
failing test after.

This CONFIRMS (with a precise mechanism, not just a "don't touch the same file twice"
scheduling heuristic) that h3g's fix genuinely cannot be a small, source-local, standalone
PR — it requires the shared sqlite_row.rs cursor adapter to gain real settling-window support
(e.g. cap cursor advancement at min(all candidate rowids' settling eligibility), not just
"exclude ineligible rows from the query"), which is squarely the same territory as the
r6d.4/4as3 checkpoint-frontier reorder. Deferral confirmed correct; reason is now precise
enough that a future session should NOT attempt the tempting source-local shortcut.

title: interval_lift drops out-of-order/equal-ts transitions and leaks open systemd states
description: VERIFIED: (a) AdjacentTransitions silently drops any input with ts_orig <= previous (interval_lift.rs:669-671) — with the 60r bulk-snapshot equal-timestamp problem this loses real boundaries; (b) StartStopPair consumes a stop with ts <= start and returns None (656-658), leaving the open start stuck FOREVER; (c) start-after-start overwrites the open observation, silently discarding an interval (634-636); (d) active_subject_states grows unboundedly for units that never emit an observed stop. Lens: implementability — 'adjacent transitions' has no defined semantics for ties, reordering, or missing counterparts, so the code guessed (drop).
acceptance_criteria: Tie/out-of-order policy defined and implemented per rule shape (deterministic tiebreak for equal ts; out-of-order handled or routed to durable debt, never silent drop). Stop-with-ts<=start closes with zero duration or emits debt instead of leaking the open start. Start-after-start emits the implied close (restart fence) instead of discarding. active_subject_states is bounded (staleness eviction with an emitted truncation marker). Tests cover each branch; interaction with 60r timestamp repair noted.

title: interval_lift heartbeat merge is arithmetically wrong: gap measured from first heartbeat, closes include the idle gap
description: VERIFIED: merged heartbeats never advance the observation timestamp (refresh_metadata_from interval_lift.rs:351-356 touches label/attrs only) while the merge gap is computed against self.ts_orig = the FIRST heartbeat (activitywatch_gap_to:367-369, merge check:703-706). A continuous heartbeat stream therefore chops into fixed ~30-35s intervals (the merge window degenerates into max-interval-length instead of gap-tolerance), inflating interval counts for continuous activity. Separately, when the gap exceeds the window, close_with ends the interval at the NEXT event's ts_orig (371-404 via 711) — the entire idle gap is attributed to the previous subject. For heartbeat-class evidence (absence of heartbeat = absence of evidence) the end must be last-seen (+ bounded slack), not next-seen. Lens: construct validity — the emitted 'interval' measures merge-window arithmetic, not activity.
acceptance_criteria: Merging advances a last_seen timestamp; gap tolerance compares against last_seen; a continuous same-subject heartbeat stream produces ONE interval per activity bout. Over-window closes end at last_seen + declared slack constant, never at the next event's ts_orig. Fixture: 10-minute 5s-heartbeat stream -> one interval of ~10min; heartbeat, 2h silence, heartbeat -> first interval ends within slack of its last heartbeat.

Semantics-version bumps mark older projection rows stale before read surfaces claim readiness. VERIFY: xtask test -p sinexd -E 'test(projection_registry_replay_invalidation|projection_registry_redaction_invalidation|projection_registry_semantics_bump)'.

The surface cannot report ready when registry status is stale/failed/partial/absent unless it explicitly returns caveats. title: Query units for evidence-graph entities and relations
description: Post-k6s half of the lv8 split: entity and relation query units backed by the evidence graph. Session/summary/tag units stay in lv8 and no longer wait for the graph. Source: new-gpt-pro 05 graph red team.
