Guardrails are the point: preregistration hash before the experiment starts (no outcome-peeking redesign), civil-time-correct bucketing, baseline-relative effects, multiple-comparison control when several metrics are declared, and the standing no-narratives doctrine — the output is typed effect estimates with CIs and evidence refs, interpretation stays with the operator. This turns the exocortex from a recorder into an instrument for self-science while structurally preventing the p-hacked self-quantification garbage the genre is famous for.
acceptance_criteria: Experiment descriptor supports N-of-1 crossover and ITS designs (phases, washout, carryover declaration); metrics resolve from derived-plane queries with civil-time bucketing; preregistration hashing gates arm start; runner emits effect estimates with CIs, per-phase evidence refs, and a validity section (adherence, contamination, missing-coverage from jdp) — zero interpretive prose; one real pilot experiment executes end-to-end and its packet is reproducible by a cold reader.

title: Declarative state-evidence engine: interval lifting as registry rows, not match arms
description: interval_lift is half-declarative (INTERVAL_LIFT_RULES catalog) but process() is a per-source match where every payload type hand-builds its StateObservation and every rule shape re-implements ordering/close logic (interval_lift.rs:541-682). The incoming semantic fixes each multiply across every branch: zs6 last-seen tracking, uzc tie policy, 5s6 fences and watermark closure, ecy start-anchored keys, y8v supersession. The engine: (1) EvidenceAdapter registered per (source, event_type) mapping payload -> StateObservation (subject, label, attributes, occurrence anchor) — registration macro like source contracts; (2) ClosurePolicy as data per state_kind: AdjacentTransitions | HeartbeatBout{merge_window, slack} | StartStopPair{tie_policy} | ObservedDuration, each implemented once; (3) the cross-kind FENCE TABLE (which evidence kinds close which state kinds: AFK fences focus/workspace, suspend/boot fences all desktop kinds) as declarative data consumed by the shared closure core; (4) key derivation and supersession stamping centralized so ecy/y8v semantics exist in exactly one place. New interval source = one adapter + one catalog row. SEQUENCING: the P1/P2 fixes (zs6/uzc) land in the current shape first — this refactor consolidates after 5s6/ecy define the target semantics; do not gate the fixes on it.
acceptance_criteria: Evidence adapters + closure policies + fence table exist as registries; all five current rules migrate with behavior-identical fixtures (post-fix semantics); the shared core owns ordering, ties, watermark closure, keys, and supersession stamping exactly once; adding a synthetic new transition source in a test requires only an adapter + row; interval_lift.rs process() match is deleted.
notes: 2026-07-08 scope note from the subsumption purpose sweep: the closure-policy vocabulary should generalize sessionization itself — gap-based episode grouping over ANY point-event stream is a policy row (activity sessions today; spotify listening sessions, browsing sessions, comms threads tomorrow), so per-domain session automata never multiply. The session-detector's activity-specific consumption becomes one instantiation of the generic policy once this engine lands (coordinate with 0vx.7's sessionization lane proof).

title: Demo: kill it mid-thought — crash-no-loss under the receipt barrier
description: The durability demo the r6d campaign earns: drive live ingest + derivation, SIGKILL sinexd at killpoints sampled from the r6d.9 harness catalog (mpsc-before-flush, checkpoint-vs-emit, invalidation mid-recompute), restart, and prove with queries that nothing silently vanished: every source record either unadvanced or terminally settled, every automaton input reprocessed-or-settled, counts reconciled against the receipt ledger. Distinct from cem.2 (outage self-DIAGNOSIS): this proves loss-freedom, cem.2 proves detection. The showable artifact is the reconciliation table going all-green after each kill. Depends on r6d.11 + r6d.9 (and honestly demonstrates the r6d epic exit criteria).
acceptance_criteria: One-command run executing >=5 distinct killpoints; per-killpoint reconciliation table (expected vs found, receipts vs rows) all green; a deliberately-reverted fix run shows the harness catching the regression (red control); packet includes exact kill locations and rerun instructions.

title: Demo: the system changes its mind honestly — interval revision end-to-end
description: Showcase the y8v revision contract on the real AW grown-duration case: capture a live window interval while ActivityWatch is still extending the row; show the initially-ingested (short) interval consumed by a downstream product (attention span / project attribution row); trigger the re-read; show supersession — one live interval with the grown duration, the short reading in audit.archived_events, downstream products re-derived via invalidation, and the recall timeline rendering the revision (superseded state per the design-language idiom). The narrative beat: 'the exocortex learned it was wrong and can show you both readings and when it switched.' Depends on n9a + h3g + 68c.4.
acceptance_criteria: Scripted packet: before/after queries proving exactly one live interval, archived superseded reading with timestamps, re-derived descendants (old versions archived), and a rendered timeline distinguishing the states. Negative control: an identical re-read (no growth) suppresses and changes nothing. Verification commands runnable by a cold reader.

title: UX charter: operator journeys, budgets, and ergonomic law for every Sinex surface
description: The coherence review fixed vocabulary and envelopes; this bead owns the operator experience as a designed artifact. Content (committed as docs/ux-charter.md, enforced by AC hooks in surface beads): (1) JOURNEYS with step + latency budgets: morning (cold shell -> three answers, 1 command, <1s render); resume (anchor -> pack -> first action, <=2 interactions, recall <2s per 1wm SLO tier); judgment (opt-in seat, batch of 8-12, defer first-class, exit anytime with progress kept); incident triage (alert -> evidence -> probe commands, no dashboard spelunking); import (command -> idempotence report -> coverage delta); weekly review (brief + baselines, zero prep). (2) NOTIFICATION BUDGET: push is earned, not default — morning brief (ambient artifact + one notification), capture-stall alerts (u3n), cost fuse (2k2); everything else is pull. No badges, no streaks, no queue-zero framing anywhere (standing AuDHD rule, already in 7z0.1). (3) TRUST CALIBRATION: every rendered claim carries its cheapest-verification affordance one action away (show command / ref); uncertainty renders as typed caveats, never prose hedging; the operator must always be able to answer 'why is the system saying this' in <=2 steps (bm1 matrix is the substrate). (4) ERGONOMIC LAW: any daily-loop action <=1 command or keystroke; interruption-safe by construction (every flow resumable after abandonment, no modal state that rots); friction is budgeted like latency. (5) DEGRADATION: daemon-down, cold-start, partial-coverage each have a designed rendering (7z0.7 owns cold-start; this charter owns the daemon-down and partial-coverage patterns: coverage strip on every timeline, probe suggestion on every failure).
acceptance_criteria: docs/ux-charter.md committed with the six journeys (steps, budgets, owning surfaces), the notification budget table, the trust-calibration rule, and the ergonomic law; each journey names its owning bead or gets one; 7z0.1/7z0.7/1wm/bm1/u3n cite the charter instead of restating; at least one journey (morning) is walked end-to-end against the charter budgets on the live dev runtime and the result recorded.
notes:
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): this bead originates in the interval adversarial audit ledger .agent/scratch/023-interval-adversarial-audit-2026-07-07.md (rulings encoded via PR #2407). The ledger is accelerant, not authority — re-verify its file:line anchors against master on claim.
2026-07-08 reprioritization sweep (sinex-h7cc): P1 -> P3. Content is pure
design/meta/legibility (UX charter doc, journeys/budgets/ergonomic law) —
CONVENTIONS.md's own priority ladder assigns that class P3, not P1. Not part
of sinex-my5's current active-round lanes (A/B/C/D as of the 2026-07-08 round
note) either. Downgrading does not deprioritize the underlying UX work that
already has surface-level beads (7z0.1/7z0.7/1wm/bm1/u3n) — it corrects the
charter DOC bead's own priority to match its own content class.

title: Preflight: git file-change extraction shape and fixture payload
description: Cheap discovery kept parallel while durable rkv.13 waits behind 0vx/68c projection discipline. Source: new-gpt-pro 05 graph red team.
acceptance_criteria: A fixture-repository command captures per-file path/status/line statistics for representative commits including renames and merges; the note records event-vs-projection implementation candidates; no durable schema/projection table is added.

title: Decision: low-blast schema lattice gaps — grants, extension versions, PG enums, collations, extra objects
description: Per-surface decision: enforce, report-only, or explicit non-goal for excess grants (apply grants but never revokes), extension version drift, future CREATE TYPE AS ENUM (no type inventory exists), collation contracts, extra schemas/tables under managed schemas. Single-operator/pre-release context lowers blast; record the acceptance rationale where non-goal. Source: new-gpt-pro 08 findings 9-10 + category matrix.
design:
PROPOSED RULING (2026-07-08, per-surface, encoded for operator ratification; single-operator pre-release context = low blast, so report-only beats enforcement machinery everywhere the failure is visible):

1. EXCESS GRANTS: report-only. strict-diff gains an excess-grant report row (grants present live but not declared); no auto-revoke — a wrong revoke can lock the daemon out, a wrong grant is visible and inert on a single-user host. Falsifier for the non-goal half: a second DB role appears.
2. EXTENSION VERSION DRIFT: pin-minimum + report. Declare minimum versions in defs; strict-diff reports older-than-minimum or major-version-ahead. No auto-upgrade (extension upgrades are deploy-time acts owned by the NixOS module).
3. PG ENUMS (CREATE TYPE AS ENUM): explicit non-goal + tripwire. Zero enums exist by design (domain enums live in Rust newtypes; DB stores text). Enforcement = forbidden-pattern lint (xtask check --forbidden) rejecting CREATE TYPE AS ENUM in schema defs, so the non-goal cannot erode silently. Cheaper than building a type inventory for a set of size zero.
4. COLLATIONS: documented non-goal. Single host, single locale, no collation-sensitive ordering contracts. Falsifier recorded in crate/sinex-schema docs: satellite hosts (rkv.7) or any ORDER BY on user text crossing a consumption contract reopens this.
5. EXTRA SCHEMAS/TABLES UNDER MANAGED SCHEMAS: report as drift. strict-diff lists unmanaged objects inside managed schemas (core/raw/audit/reflection/sinex_schemas/sinex_telemetry) — these are either abandoned experiments or manual surgery; both deserve visibility. Not auto-dropped (state is durable; wipes are design failures).

Implementation rider: all five land as verify-interpreter rows over the 32p2 expectation IR when it exists (2pl guidance applies); if claimed before 32p2, implement as report-only strict-diff additions and note the IR migration on 32p2.
acceptance_criteria: Each surface has a recorded decision. Enforced surfaces gain strict-diff coverage; non-goal surfaces get a documented why in crate/sinex-schema docs.
notes: RATIFIED 2026-07-08 (operator, decision-brief batch): all five per-surface verdicts FINAL. Bead stays open as its own implementation carrier (report rows + forbidden-pattern tripwire + docs), preferably as 32p2 IR interpreter rows.

title: Telemetry/Timescale strict drift: view+CAGG definitions, refresh policies, compression, reflection hypertable settings
description: check_telemetry_drifts() checks relation kind + CAGG registration only (apply.rs:295-347); refresh policies use if_not_exists and are never compared (apply.rs:2530-2551,2781-2802); compression SQL only runs when no settings row exists so a bad segmentby survives (defs/events.rs:339-357 documents the pathological-metadata risk, apply.rs:2477-2493); strict Timescale coverage is core.events chunk-interval + retention only (strict_diff.rs:813-915) — reflection.events retention/compression drift is silent. Source: new-gpt-pro 08 findings 5-7.
acceptance_criteria: Strict diff compares pg_get_viewdef for telemetry views/matviews against source SQL or stable hashes; CAGG definitions + refresh policy offsets/schedule; core.events AND reflection.events hypertable chunk interval, retention, compression enabled/segmentby/orderby. Tests cover wrong current_system_state definition, wrong refresh schedule, wrong compression segmentby, missing reflection retention policy.

title: xtask status honesty: bg launcher exit is not proof, timeout exit-code split, stderr-blind JSON diagnostics
description: Bundled P2s from new-gpt-pro 03: (a) --bg launcher returns success for started/attached/queued/fresh (coordinator.rs:1626-1983) — only jobs wait/exit_code is proof; automation gating on launcher exit ships failed jobs; add a proof-mode (--bg --wait or xtask gate) and proof_status:incomplete in launcher JSON. (b) Timeout lifecycle: detached reaper writes 124 (reap.rs:157-178) but internal cargo timeout maps to exit 1 (lib.rs:608-617,866-913); reaper spawn failure only warns — unify on 124 and make watchdog spawn failure fatal for bg proof commands. (c) Cargo stderr is inherited, not captured: stderr-only failures (manifest/tool/lock errors) produce a synthetic diagnostic with stdout tail only (cargo_diagnostics.rs:527-603) — pipe stderr and include a bounded tail in XTASK_UNPARSED_CARGO_FAILURE.
acceptance_criteria: xtask gate (or --bg --wait) provides a single-command sound proof path; launcher-only JSON marks proof_status incomplete. Timeouts surface as 124 in both foreground and detached paths; finite-watchdog spawn failure is fatal for proof-producing bg commands. Synthetic cargo-failure diagnostics include a bounded stderr tail; JSON/bg consumers see the real cause without reading job stderr logs.

title: git.file.changed projection: file-level change facts for overlap joins
description: Commit-level git events cannot prove file overlap — work correlation currently over-joins by time (audit section 9 failure: an agent session near a commit gets credited even when it touched a different repo/files). Add a file-level projection/read model over existing git commit materials: git.file.changed {commit_sha, repo_path, path, previous_path, change_type, lines_added, lines_deleted, binary, path_root, file_category}, plus git.ref.mentioned for issue/PR refs in messages. Symbol-level diffs are explicitly out of scope (later, if ever).
acceptance_criteria: File-change facts derivable/queryable for existing git commit history (projection or derived events — pick per 0vx layer discipline and record which); exposed in at least one consumer (sinexctl context around a commit, or the a4w.3.9 change-episode leg); a test proves file overlap distinguishes same-time-different-files from same-files. VERIFY: query on dev store over a known commit.

title: Prior-art autopsy series: why they died, what to steal (web research)
description:
Analysis #9: one-page autopsies, each answering 'why did it die/plateau + what does Sinex steal/avoid': Perkeep (consumption never matched the store — Sinex's named risk; treat their history as the cautionary spine of pi1), rewind.ai/screenpipe (screen-capture volume economics + trust — feeds content-axis budget), ActivityWatch (ecosystem stagnation; watcher model vs Sinex sources), HPI/karlicoss (config-as-typed-modules UX — the closest CLI relative), Datomic (as-of surface language for a11), Salsa/Build-Systems-à-la-Carte (red-green + durability tiers for qky), W3C PROV (export mapping for legibility, events trace --format prov). U12 (016/02-uncovered-designs.md:186) carries the steal-pointers; this executes it with current web sources.

## Acceptance Criteria
Autopsy notes committed (scratch/research or ej9 material); each names concrete design consequences stamped onto its beneficiary bead (pi1, ej9, qky, a11, content-axis); claims dated with sources.
acceptance_criteria: Autopsy notes committed (scratch/research or ej9 material); each names concrete design consequences stamped onto its beneficiary bead (pi1, ej9, qky, a11, content-axis); claims dated with sources.
notes: 2026-07-07: the dr-01-prior-art-autopsies DeepResearch run FAILED (brief did not attach). The ready-made prompt at /realm/inbox/gpt-pro-handoff/sinex/dr-01-prior-art-autopsies-lifelogging-systems.md is the execution vehicle for this bead — re-dispatch it rather than writing a new brief.

title: Event-driven agent handlers: Sinex events trigger persistent agent sessions
description:
Rawlog intent (06-23 21:04 'secretary' + 06-28 22:37): an agent that ambiently knows what is happening — a new rawlog entry, a capture stall, a finished import — WITHOUT being prompted, because Sinex events drive it. Operator sketch: 'sinex events directly driving handlers, including coding agents... persistent/long-term coding agent sessions tasked with reacting to things in a loop.'

Implementable shape (no magic): a subscription-dispatch bridge — operator-declared handler registrations {filter: SinexQuery-fragment over confirmed events, action: notify|spawn-session|run-command-template}, consuming the confirmed stream (same machinery as SSE bus), with the r7h verb-registry security stance (operator-declared handlers only, schema-validated, agents cannot self-register beyond their write-scope).

## Acceptance Criteria
Handler registry (data, runtime-configurable); at least two live handlers proven: (a) new operator.rawlog.entry event -> notification/context-injection to a live agent session, (b) capture-stall event (u3n) -> push alert; dispatch latency measured; every dispatch is itself an event (actuation provenance, instruction-reconciler pattern); AuDHD filter: handlers deliver information/context, never nudges.
design: Reuses: confirmed-stream subscription (sse_bus pattern), instruction-reconciler expectation matching (automata/instruction_reconciler.rs generic matcher), r7h verb registry for the action leg, runtime-config surface for registration. The agent-session leg starts as notify/inject (hooks, kitty control) — spawn-session comes only after the notify leg proves the loop. Relates: r7h, u3n, 9vn, runtime-config bead.
acceptance_criteria: Handler registry (data, runtime-configurable); at least two live handlers proven: (a) new operator.rawlog.entry event -> notification/context-injection to a live agent session, (b) capture-stall event (u3n) -> push alert; dispatch latency measured; every dispatch is itself an event (actuation provenance, instruction-reconciler pattern); AuDHD filter: handlers deliver information/context, never nudges.

title: Decision: agent-session content embedding ownership (avoid double-embedding)
description:
Polylogue has its own embedding pipeline (semantic search over sessions); Sinex 5v6 activates event/document embeddings. Agent-session content would be embedded TWICE (once per system) with different models/stores. Decide ownership: (a) Sinex embeds (Polylogue queries Sinex semantic search when configured — consistent with substrate role, but couples Polylogue search quality to Sinex presence); (b) both embed independently (simple, wasteful, divergent results); (c) shared effect-ledger: embeddings are recorded model effects keyed by content hash — either system reuses the other's recorded vectors when present (dedup without coupling).

## Acceptance Criteria
Recorded decision with the cost math (corpus size x embedding cost, measured); implementation beads filed on the chosen side(s); if (c): the effect-key contract for embedding reuse documented in both repos. Operator sign-off (cross-repo resource decision).
design: Recommendation lean: (c) preserves Polylogue independence (its standalone pipeline keeps working) while making co-deployment cheaper — and it is exactly the ModelEffectRecord composite-key pattern 5v6 establishes (provider/model/input-hash). Gate on 5v6 landing so the cost math uses real numbers.
acceptance_criteria: Recorded decision with the cost math (corpus size x embedding cost, measured); implementation beads filed on the chosen side(s); if (c): the effect-key contract for embedding reuse documented in both repos. Operator sign-off (cross-repo resource decision).
notes:
PROPOSED RULING (2026-07-08, encoded for operator ratification — cross-repo): adopt (c) shared effect-ledger. Embeddings are recorded model effects keyed by (provider, model, content-hash) — the ModelEffectRecord composite-key pattern 5v6 establishes; either system reuses the other side vectors when present, neither depends on the other existing. Polylogue standalone semantics preserved (its pipeline keeps working with zero Sinex); co-deployment stops double-paying. Cost math AC stays gated on 5v6 real measurements before this closes. (a) rejected: couples Polylogue search quality to Sinex presence, violating the polylogue-on-sinex doctrine (standalone stays functional forever). (b) rejected: permanent double cost + divergent results for zero benefit. Follow-up on ratification: file the effect-key contract bead on the polylogue side and dep-link both.
RATIFIED 2026-07-08 (operator, decision-brief batch): (c) shared effect-ledger is FINAL as the ownership shape; bead stays open only for the 5v6-measured cost math + the effect-key contract documentation. FOLLOW-UP OWED: file the polylogue-side effect-key contract bead (reference sinex-4j2.8) next polylogue session — not filed cross-repo from this session.

title: Identity bridge: stable cross-refs between Polylogue ids and Sinex object refs
description:
Deep integration needs stable two-way navigation: a Polylogue session/message id resolves to its Sinex objects (material, anchored events) and a Sinex event ref resolves back to the exact Polylogue session/message — surviving re-export (new export file, same logical message) and replay (new event ids, same occurrence). The mapping key is the occurrence identity (session_id + line/provider identity per 4j2.1 decisions), never event ids or export file paths.

## Acceptance Criteria
sinexctl show accepts a polylogue ref form (e.g. polylogue:session/<id>[/message/<n>]) and resolves to the material + live events; the reverse ref (sinex event -> polylogue deep link) is renderable in evidence cards; both survive a re-export + replay round-trip test (same logical message resolves to the same occurrence before and after). VERIFY: round-trip test on dev.
design: Builds on SinexObjectRef/show resolution (bm1 owns the resolution matrix — coordinate, this adds a kind rather than forking resolution). The polylogue deep-link format is Polylogue's authority (cross-repo); Sinex stores only the occurrence-derived components it already has. No new tables: resolution computes from raw.source_material_registry metadata + occurrence columns.
acceptance_criteria: sinexctl show accepts a polylogue ref form (e.g. polylogue:session/<id>[/message/<n>]) and resolves to the material + live events; the reverse ref (sinex event -> polylogue deep link) is renderable in evidence cards; both survive a re-export + replay round-trip test (same logical message resolves to the same occurrence before and after). VERIFY: round-trip test on dev.

title: Analysis: checkpoint vocabulary — five position/state mechanisms, one concept audit
description:
Streamlining audit 2026-07-06: five checkpoint-ish mechanisms coexist: (1) stream Checkpoint enum (runtime/stream/checkpoint.rs:6) — scan positions; (2) CheckpointState/CheckpointManager + NATS-KV persistence + history/stats (runtime/checkpoint.rs:66,:367,:917 — 900+ lines); (3) adapter cursors (AdapterModuleState.cursor, opaque per-adapter); (4) parser-local checkpoints (AdapterModuleState.parser_checkpoint, JsonValue snapshot/rollback); (5) automaton bridge checkpoints (KV, last-processed-event, automaton_runtime.rs). Each is defensible in isolation (source position vs module state vs bridge position are different things) — the audit question is whether the LAYERING is principled or accreted: can (3)+(4) collapse into (1)s vocabulary; does (5) duplicate (2)s machinery; is the KV+file dual persistence in (2) still needed post-reflection-lane?

## Acceptance Criteria
A written layering statement (which mechanism owns which recovery contract, with the crash-window matrix r6d.1 as the correctness frame); any collapse implemented or explicitly rejected with reason; the glossary/docs name the surviving mechanisms. NOT a rewrite mandate — an audit that either tightens or justifies.
design: Do AFTER r6d.9 (crash harness) exists — recovery-contract claims need its scenarios to verify. Relates: r6d.1 (loss-window matrix), r6d.4 (cursor/emit ordering).
acceptance_criteria: A written layering statement (which mechanism owns which recovery contract, with the crash-window matrix r6d.1 as the correctness frame); any collapse implemented or explicitly rejected with reason; the glossary/docs name the surviving mechanisms. NOT a rewrite mandate — an audit that either tightens or justifies.

title: Write the risk register into the target-vision claim ledger
description:
U11 (016/02-uncovered-designs.md:168-184): the four invalidation risks for the central bet (context windows beat derivation; LLM speed makes lanes ceremony; single-operator never generalizes; capture breadth stalls structurally) each with counter + residual truth — argued nowhere durable. Write them into .github/target-vision-claim-ledger.md as first-class claims with their counters, so future architecture debates start from the recorded argument.

## Acceptance Criteria
The four risks + counters + residual truths in the claim ledger with dates; rl4's doctrine page cross-references the register; xtask docs check passes if the ledger is a generated-adjacent surface. VERIFY: ledger diff.
acceptance_criteria: The four risks + counters + residual truths in the claim ledger with dates; rl4's doctrine page cross-references the register; xtask docs check passes if the ledger is a generated-adjacent surface. VERIFY: ledger diff.

title: Agent annotation DSL: four line-anchored forms ingested as agent.annotation events
description:
U8 (016/02-uncovered-designs.md:130-140): a minimal grammar agents emit mid-prose at zero tool-call cost — @@claim(id?):, @@todo(due?):, @@obs:, @@ref(kind:id): — one per line, no nesting, greppable. Polylogue extracts with span provenance (session/message/offset = material anchor); Sinex ingests via the integration.polylogue lane as agent.annotation events with material provenance to the session export. Explicitly NOT: free-form XML, multi-line blocks, more types (extend only after volume proves need).

## Acceptance Criteria
Grammar documented (Polylogue + Sinex sides); Polylogue extraction with span provenance; agent.annotation events land with material anchors; a query surface lists annotations by kind/id; round-trip demo: annotation written in an agent session appears in Sinex query within one sync. VERIFY: round-trip on dev.
design: DECISION (settled in U8): the four forms and the exclusions are the spec — do not grow the grammar in v1. Cross-repo: extraction lives in Polylogue, ingestion in Sinex 4j2.1 vocabulary; sequence with/after 4j2.1.
acceptance_criteria: Grammar documented (Polylogue + Sinex sides); Polylogue extraction with span provenance; agent.annotation events land with material anchors; a query surface lists annotations by kind/id; round-trip demo: annotation written in an agent session appears in Sinex query within one sync. VERIFY: round-trip on dev.

title: Experiment-hosting substrate: declared arms, preregistered metrics, paired analysis
description:
Blueprint 018 §2: the closed 9j9 campaign proved a two-arm comparison (recall-v2 vs committed baseline) as a one-off. Generalize into a small experiment-hosting substrate: declared arms, preregistered metrics, paired analysis over the same evidence window — agent-buildable, so future capability claims (retrieval hit@k in cem.5, lane duels in cem.6, blinded resumption in cem.7) reuse one harness instead of bespoke scripts.

## Acceptance Criteria
An experiment descriptor (arms, metric definitions, evidence window) + runner producing a paired-analysis artifact; cem.5 or cem.6 executed THROUGH it as the proving case; 9j9's one-off documented as the precedent it generalizes. VERIFY: one real experiment run end-to-end via the substrate.
acceptance_criteria: Descriptor schema sinex.experiment/v1: experiment_id, claim, preregistered_at, fixtures (store epoch, query set, gold set, coverage snapshot, generator exclusions), declared arms (generator, command, inputs, constraints), metrics (kind: retrieval|pack|ranking|duel|label_quality|latency, primary flag), analysis (unit, paired_by, bootstrap resamples, CI, extra tests), stopping_rule (min_n, max_n, looks_every, keep_if, kill_if), outputs. Runner executes arms over the same fixture, records exact commands/config/store epoch, writes per_item_jsonl, paired_deltas_csv, summary.json, report.md. Supports retrieval (hit@k/MRR/nDCG), structured pack metrics (weighted gold-segment coverage, critical recall, citation resolvability, gap-honesty F1, source-family coverage, support fidelity, self-observation leak rate, finite degraded completion), salience metrics (nDCG@10, precision@5, top3-useful, pairwise preference, term traceability/non-fabrication), duel metrics, label-quality metrics (weighted kappa, Jaccard, rank correlation, defer rate). Paired bootstrap over the declared unit; sign-test/discordance for binary outcomes. Preregistration enforced by hashing descriptor+query set+gold set+coverage snapshot before arm execution. Sequential looks with recorded stopping-rule firing. cem.5 plus at least one pack or salience experiment execute through the substrate before close.
notes: 2026-07-07: five ready-to-run descriptor YAMLs (recall_golden_v1, cem5 hybrid retrieval, recall_pack_quality_v1, uxd_salience_ranking_v1, cem7 duel) committed in new-gpt-pro sessions/06 — lift them verbatim when implementing.

title: Demo corpus depth audit: fixtures exercise every construct the demos claim
description:
Blueprint 018 §2: each cem demo carries Claim/Instrument/Falsifier; this audits that the fixture/data corpus behind each demo actually exercises the claimed constructs (e.g. the planted-secret demo needs both positive and negative controls IN the corpus; the Tuesday-reconstruction demo needs a day with real gaps to show honest-gap rendering). Prevents demos that pass on convenient data.

## Acceptance Criteria
Per selected demo: a corpus statement (what data, which constructs it exercises, which edge it deliberately includes); gaps fixed by extending fixtures; the audit recorded in each demo bead's notes. VERIFY: corpus statements present on every cem child before its demo ships.
acceptance_criteria: Per selected demo: a corpus statement (what data, which constructs it exercises, which edge it deliberately includes); gaps fixed by extending fixtures; the audit recorded in each demo bead's notes. VERIFY: corpus statements present on every cem child before its demo ships.

title: Capture-surface threat posture: per-source sensitivity declaration + verification
description:
Blueprint lens L-4: Sinex captures keystrokes, clipboard, window titles, screen frames — live, from a running human. Distinct from the redaction feature (optional by design): this is about the capture INSTRUMENT. Each source declares its maximum sensitivity (privacy_tier exists as Level-6 declaration metadata) but nothing verifies a source cannot exceed its declaration, and the blast-radius stance (what leaks if the store leaks) is documented only in the threat-model doc.

## Acceptance Criteria
Per-source sensitivity declaration becomes verified (Level 4+): a test/lint per capture family asserting emitted payload fields stay within the declared tier (e.g. window-title source cannot emit clipboard content); screen/clipboard secret-class handling stated (field exclusion or suppression path); the at-rest encryption posture doc cross-references the per-source declarations; blast-radius statement per sensitivity tier in the threat model. VERIFY: the verification tests + updated threat-model doc.
design: Existing anchors: nixos/modules/security-threat-model.md + at-rest-encryption.md; privacy_tier/ProcessingContext declarations on SourceContract (Level 6 today — this bead promotes declaration accuracy to an enforced level, exactly the L-2 enforcement-level lens move). Do NOT build redaction features here (privacy is opt-in by doctrine); this is capture-instrument honesty. Relates: r6d.2 (cleartext shadow audit), e5p, media session gate (session_gate.rs precedent for capture gating).
acceptance_criteria: Per-source sensitivity declaration becomes verified (Level 4+): a test/lint per capture family asserting emitted payload fields stay within the declared tier (e.g. window-title source cannot emit clipboard content); screen/clipboard secret-class handling stated (field exclusion or suppression path); the at-rest encryption posture doc cross-references the per-source declarations; blast-radius statement per sensitivity tier in the threat model. VERIFY: the verification tests + updated threat-model doc.
notes: 2026-07-07 DeepResearch input: dr-10-threat-model-at-rest.report.md — attacker-class-first threat model for a single-operator total-capture archive; which encryption layers retain value once the machine is unlocked and the archive searchable. Use for the blast-radius statements this bead requires.

title: Content axis as one capability: unified material/anchor/extraction contract
description:
Blueprint 018 §2 / 017 Axis A: browser page content, email bodies, screen-OCR, and clipboard content are all 'content capture' — one capability with N adapters, not N independent source designs. Unify under one material/anchor/extraction contract (CAS-backed content material, byte/logical anchors, extraction lane producing document/chunk events) with an up-front volume budget (018 §3.4 decision: transition/point capture + CAS material, tunable cadence, hard volume ceiling before lighting).

## Acceptance Criteria
One documented content-capture contract (material policy, anchor scheme, extraction path, volume budget mechanism); at least two content adapters (e.g. browser page + clipboard) implemented against it sharing the extraction lane; the volume-budget decision recorded with the operator; screen-OCR (a4w.3.7) and email bodies (di7) explicitly declared as later adapters of THIS contract, not parallel designs. VERIFY: two adapters producing through one lane on dev; storage-growth analytics showing budget enforcement.
design: The document layer already exists (document_parser automaton, core.documents, chunking, doc search) — the content axis is adapters INTO that layer, not a new layer. Volume control precedent: xtask analytics storage-growth (#2312) is the measurement instrument. DECISION REQUIRED (operator): the volume budget numbers + whether screen-OCR lights at all before AT-SPI (rkv.8 may obsolete most OCR need — the anti-OCR source). Relates: rkv.3 (browser content = first adapter), rkv.8, a4w.3.7, di7.
acceptance_criteria: One documented content-capture contract (material policy, anchor scheme, extraction path, volume budget mechanism); at least two content adapters (e.g. browser page + clipboard) implemented against it sharing the extraction lane; the volume-budget decision recorded with the operator; screen-OCR (a4w.3.7) and email bodies (di7) explicitly declared as later adapters of THIS contract, not parallel designs. VERIFY: two adapters producing through one lane on dev; storage-growth analytics showing budget enforcement.
notes: 2026-07-06 cost envelope (analysis #8, xtask analytics storage-growth on dev store): 12-month projection ALREADY 2.14TB with browser.history alone = 93.0% of registered material bytes — the volume problem exists BEFORE the content axis lights. Numbers for the budget decision: any content adapter must state projected bytes/day against this baseline; the browser.history dominance also suggests the FIRST content-axis win is taming/deduping what already flows, not adding capture. Note: 'compression configured, 0 chunks compressed' on dev is partly EXPECTED (auto compression policy deliberately removed from core.events post-#2182 — do not re-add it blindly; compression is operator-explicit there), but 2 chunks >=4GiB (max 39GB) on dev suggests re-checking chunking/mega-material state (lb8 territory).

title: Design: person entity — identity resolution across email, git-author, handle, messaging
description:
Meta-gap 018 §7.1: the people dimension has no design, only capture blockers. Everything social (comm.thread, relationship.activity, project.attribution people-splits) presumes a PERSON entity resolved across identity facets — email address(es), git author strings, platform handles, messaging contacts — but no entity model, resolution rule set, or adjudication flow exists for persons. This is a design round producing the model + implementation beads, NOT implementation.

## Acceptance Criteria
A design doc (scratch/research or crate docs): person-entity shape, facet-evidence model (which observations assert identity-equivalence and at what tier), resolution rules (mechanical: same email; heuristic: name similarity; adjudicated: operator merge), privacy stance (persons are the most sensitive entity class), and the promotion path through the authority seam. Implementation beads filed + linked. Operator sign-off on the model. VERIFY: design doc committed + beads exist.
design: Constraints already settled elsewhere: nothing auto-promotes to canonical person identity (adjudication doctrine); confidence carries evidence provenance (8cr tiers apply to identity-equivalence claims); entity substrate decision (pq5) gates whether this builds on the existing chain or a fresh lane. Prior art: entity resolution literature is deep — survey briefly (web) at execution time rather than inventing. Relates: pq5, a4w.3.11, di7 (email is the person-corpus unlock), rkv.1 (github handles).
acceptance_criteria: A design doc (scratch/research or crate docs): person-entity shape, facet-evidence model (which observations assert identity-equivalence and at what tier), resolution rules (mechanical: same email; heuristic: name similarity; adjudicated: operator merge), privacy stance (persons are the most sensitive entity class), and the promotion path through the authority seam. Implementation beads filed + linked. Operator sign-off on the model. VERIFY: design doc committed + beads exist.

title: LLM budget policy automaton: ledger consumer that pauses lanes at threshold
description:
Meta-gap 018 §7.5 + U9 failure class 5: llm.budget.ledger exists as a kind and 5v6 will emit it, but nothing CONSUMES it — no spend policy, no cap, no alert. A budget automaton reads ledger events, aggregates spend per lane/provider/window, and pauses lanes (emit llm.budget.exceeded + lane disable via the control plane) at operator-set ceilings. Without this, activating the LLM plane has an uncapped cost failure mode (U9 #5: cost blowout).

## Acceptance Criteria
Budget automaton consumes llm.budget.ledger; per-lane/window spend aggregation; at threshold emits llm.budget.exceeded AND actually pauses the lane (verifiable via lane status); ceiling configurable at runtime (runtime-config surface once it exists, env until then); fixture test: synthetic ledger events cross threshold -> lane paused. VERIFY: fixture run + lane status flip.
design: Depends on 5v6 (the ledger emitter). Lane pause mechanism = SemanticLaneStatus via set_lane_status (semantic repo :252) or SINEX_AUTOMATA-level gating — prefer lane status (it is data, resumable, auditable). Failure routes for the whole semantic plane are cataloged in U9 (016/02-uncovered-designs.md:142-155) — implement #5 here, the rest belong to 0vx lane robustness.
acceptance_criteria: Budget automaton consumes llm.budget.ledger; per-lane/window spend aggregation; at threshold emits llm.budget.exceeded AND actually pauses the lane (verifiable via lane status); ceiling configurable at runtime (runtime-config surface once it exists, env until then); fixture test: synthetic ledger events cross threshold -> lane paused. VERIFY: fixture run + lane status flip.
notes: 2026-07-07 audit integration (audit §13): budget rows need token classes + provenance, not raw totals: fresh_input, cached_input_read, cache_write, output, reasoning tokens; list_equivalent_cost vs subscription_credit_estimate vs actual_billed (with pricing_source). Pause decisions by cost class and lane priority — a cache-read-heavy lane must not be paused ahead of an output-heavy expensive lane. Now sequenced AFTER 5v6 (consumes its real ledger events) via graph grooming.

title: Analysis: derivation composition audit — is the algebra closed in practice?
description:
Blueprint lens L-6: the lineage DAG supports N-level derived-of-derived structurally (UUIDv7 monotonicity), but live automata mostly do not compose — daily reads raw activity rather than hourly rollups; the entity chain is the only real 4-stage composition (and its consumption is pq5's question). Audit which derivations SHOULD consume other derivations and do not; verify provenance-join walks multi-level lineage correctly for evidence citation (trace to material roots through 2+ derived levels).

## Acceptance Criteria
A matrix (bead note or scratch): per automaton, current inputs vs closure-correct inputs, with fix beads filed for real inversions; one test proving multi-level lineage walk cites correctly through >=2 derived levels; hourly->daily composition either fixed or explicitly justified. VERIFY: matrix + the lineage test.
design: Cheap analysis: read the registry input_event_type declarations (all 16 in automata/) against the L2/L3 layer intent. The lineage-walk proof reuses trace machinery (sinexctl events trace / trace_lineage MCP). Relates: dcs (algebra), a4w.3.4 (work.episode consumes attention = the flagship closure case).
acceptance_criteria: A matrix (bead note or scratch): per automaton, current inputs vs closure-correct inputs, with fix beads filed for real inversions; one test proving multi-level lineage walk cites correctly through >=2 derived levels; hourly->daily composition either fixed or explicitly justified. VERIFY: matrix + the lineage test.

title: Temporal-quality propagation: joins inherit the worst input ts_orig quality
description:
Blueprint lens L-3: a time-join between a RealtimeCapture source (ts_orig good to the ms) and an InferredMtime source (good to a day) joins intervals of wildly different precision. The join must carry a confidence derived from the WORSE input quality — and that quality flows into the evidence tier. Today ts_quality exists per-event (temporal ladder) but no derivation propagates it: interval-lift and the composites emit outputs with no quality statement.

## Acceptance Criteria
Derived events produced by time-joins carry a propagated temporal-quality field = min(input rungs); interval-lift stamps it; at least one composite (attention.stream or a4w.3.2) surfaces it; the evidence-tier field (8cr) incorporates temporal quality in its tier derivation for time-join outputs; documented in derivation_catalog. VERIFY: fixture with mixed-quality inputs produces min-rung output; test asserts propagation.
design: Carrier: the same DerivedOutput/synthesized-metadata path as 8cr (do together or immediately after — same files). ts_quality already on Event (events/mod.rs:71) and rungs in TemporalSourceType; propagation is a fold over parents. Interacts: 8cr (tier), interval_lift.rs rules, dcs (catalog formalization).
acceptance_criteria: Derived events produced by time-joins carry a propagated temporal-quality field = min(input rungs); interval-lift stamps it; at least one composite (attention.stream or a4w.3.2) surfaces it; the evidence-tier field (8cr) incorporates temporal quality in its tier derivation for time-join outputs; documented in derivation_catalog. VERIFY: fixture with mixed-quality inputs produces min-rung output; test asserts propagation.

title: Operator runtime-config surface: hot-tunable recall/derivation/privacy/budget knobs
description:
019 §3b/§4 runtime-config lens: Sinex has a control plane (source/lane/replay control subjects) but no operator-facing runtime config surface. The UX-bearing knobs — recall default window, per-source salience weights, source mute ('silence fs for an hour'), session-gap threshold, lane enable/disable, epoch pinning, evidence-tier floor, LLM spend ceiling, capture-volume caps, private-hour toggle — should be hot-reloadable via one 'sinexctl config runtime' surface, not deploy-time Nix edits.

## Acceptance Criteria
A typed runtime-config document (DB or KV backed) with get/set/list via sinexctl + MCP; at least three knobs live end-to-end (recall window, source mute, lane enable) with hot effect (no restart); every knob names its consumer (standing rule: no config without a reader); changes are events (config.changed, material or operator provenance). VERIFY: set knob -> observe behavior change on live dev runtime without restart.
design: Do NOT create a parallel config system: source_session_state (core.source_session_state control plane, PRs #2178-#2181) is the precedent — media live-session ops already do runtime enable/disable/pause. Generalize that pattern. Privacy knobs route through the existing privacy.* policy tables. Interacts: vfy (lane gating), 5v6 (spend ceiling), uxd (salience weights).
acceptance_criteria: A typed runtime-config document (DB or KV backed) with get/set/list via sinexctl + MCP; at least three knobs live end-to-end (recall window, source mute, lane enable) with hot effect (no restart); every knob names its consumer (standing rule: no config without a reader); changes are events (config.changed, material or operator provenance). VERIFY: set knob -> observe behavior change on live dev runtime without restart.

title: Context-budget arbiter: one budget across recall, context packs, and orientation
description:
Blueprint 018 §2: recall, context_pack, and MCP orientation are three independent renderers deciding what enters an agent's context; nothing arbitrates total budget or priority across them. One arbiter decides allocation (by evidence value, freshness, and caller-declared budget), so an agent asking for 8K tokens of context gets the best 8K across surfaces, not three uncoordinated dumps.

## Acceptance Criteria
A budget parameter flows through recall/context/orient MCP+CLI surfaces; one arbiter module owns allocation with a measured-quantity priority (no invented weights — salience from uxd once it lands); output states what was dropped for budget (honest-gaps rule). VERIFY: same query at two budgets shows principled truncation with drop caveats.
design: Sequence AFTER uxd (salience scoring) and the recall surface stabilizing — the arbiter consumes salience, it does not invent its own ranking. Likely home: the shared context/query substrate in sinexctl (context.rs family) + a budget field in the MCP tool schemas. Relates: uxd, x69, 68c (read-model readiness feeds priority).
acceptance_criteria: A budget parameter flows through recall/context/orient MCP+CLI surfaces; one arbiter module owns allocation with a measured-quantity priority (no invented weights — salience from uxd once it lands); output states what was dropped for budget (honest-gaps rule). VERIFY: same query at two budgets shows principled truncation with drop caveats.
notes:
2026-07-07 audit integration (audit §18): context packs need a build manifest to avoid context soup: {build_id, question/purpose, selection_strategy, window+expansion rules, included families, omitted families with reasons, coverage summary, claim/support table, token budget + truncation policy, refs verification status}; Markdown and JSON must render from the same intermediate struct. A context pack is a report artifact, never an authority table.
2026-07-07 DeepResearch input: dr-06-agent-memory-context-packs.report.md — July-2026 SOTA on agent memory, context-pack construction, token-budget arbitration, event-driven activation, and long-horizon anti-patterns, shaped for the local-first provenance-aware architecture. Coherence review (sessions/10) adds: no cross-tool truncation manifest exists today; sinex_orient is unbounded; ViewEnvelope lacks a standard drop/budget manifest field — the arbiter owns those.
2026-07-08: the arbiter plugs into the pack-assembly pipeline runner (x5mg) as a policy — without that seam this bead would hand-edit every stage of every surface. Now gated on it.

title: Cold-start contract: honest empty states + derivation bootstrapping order
description:
Blueprint lens L-ii: every consumption surface is designed against the operator's warm 80M-row store. On a fresh deployment (horizontal expansion, wiped store, or the public demo) recall/timeline/briefs return empty. The honest-gaps rule demands empty states that say WHY ('capture started 2h ago; sessions need ~1 day of events; attention stream materializes after interval-lift processes backlog') plus a documented bootstrapping order (which derivations become useful when).

## Acceptance Criteria
Each major surface (recall, timeline, brief, sources status) renders a stated-cause empty state on a fresh dev store (verifiable via xtask reset + fresh runtime); the bootstrapping order (source -> L2 -> L3 availability timeline) is documented; ViewEnvelope caveats carry 'cold-start' readiness where applicable. VERIFY: fresh-store run of recall + timeline shows cause-stated empties, not bare zeros.
design: Builds directly on the 42n ViewEnvelope/caveat machinery (landed) — this is a caveat-vocabulary + rendering pass, not new plumbing. Pairs with 8ve (change.episode cold-start demo: the FIRST useful derivation on any deployment is its own construction). Relates: 2bk, 8ve.
acceptance_criteria: Each major surface (recall, timeline, brief, sources status) renders a stated-cause empty state on a fresh dev store (verifiable via xtask reset + fresh runtime); the bootstrapping order (source -> L2 -> L3 availability timeline) is documented; ViewEnvelope caveats carry cold-start readiness where applicable. ADDITIONS (2026-07-07): explicit cold-start caveat ids or typed variants — cold_start.no_sources, cold_start.readmodels_empty, cold_start.derived_lanes_unavailable; fresh-store fixtures cover CLI table AND JSON/YAML for recall, events query/timeline, docs search, sources status/readiness/coverage, metrics report today, and MCP sinex_context_pack/sinex_search_events; a table/TUI empty state may never be only 'No events'/'No sources' etc — stated cause + at least one readiness/cold-start caveat when coverage is unmeasurable; zero sources renders as unmeasurable coverage, not zero-error healthy; MCP automatic empty/null caveats remain but producer-specific cold-start caveats take precedence. VERIFY: fresh-store run of recall + timeline shows cause-stated empties, not bare zeros.
notes: 2026-07-07 evidence: caveat enum lacks cold-start ids (views/common.rs:208-243); recall table exits before caveat output when event cards are empty (context.rs:425-431); source summary computes zero-coverage-error for an empty source set (views/sources.rs:302-321). Several table renderers drop envelope caveats entirely (fmt/envelope.rs:53-74).

title: Partial-record re-emit duplicates: multi-intent record with mid-record emit failure re-emits persisted siblings on retry
description:
Verified seam (grok 2026-07-06): in AdapterBackedSource drain (crate/sinexd/src/runtime/parser/adapter_source.rs:1456-1505), a record can parse into multiple intents; if intent K emits successfully and intent K+1 fails emit, record_processed=false so the cursor does NOT advance (correct, no loss) — but the already-emitted K events proceed through the pipeline and persist. On retry the record re-emits ALL intents, duplicating the first K for any source that does not stamp equivalence_key (only OccurrenceIdentity::Uuid5From sources do; admission.rs:505-525 suppression is keyed on it). Consequence: at-least-once duplicates concentrated exactly at emit-failure boundaries, invisible to sources without occurrence identity.

## Steps to Reproduce
Craft a parser returning 2+ intents for one record; inject emit failure on the second (test hook or NATS payload-cap overflow); drain, restart drain; observe duplicate first-intent rows in core.events with distinct ids, same (source_material_id, anchor_byte).

## Acceptance Criteria
Either (a) per-record emit becomes all-or-nothing (buffer intents, emit after all conversions succeed — emit failures then leave zero persisted siblings), or (b) every multi-intent source stamps occurrence identity so admission suppression covers the retry, or (c) the seam is documented as accepted at-least-once with the r6d.8 classification citing it. A focused test proves the chosen behavior.
design: Option (a) is the cheap local fix: collect converted events for the record into a Vec, then emit sequentially only after all intent_to_event conversions succeeded; a mid-emit transport failure still duplicates (transport is at-least-once by contract) but conversion failures no longer do. Full fix pairs with r6d.4 (durable-emit-before-cursor) — coordinate, do not conflict: both touch the same drain loop. Relates: sinex-r6d.8 (loss/replayability classification), sinex-e5p item 7 (dedup fail-open).
acceptance_criteria: A multi-intent record either commits all sibling outcomes as one durable progress atom, or records durable partial-debt/operation state that prevents silent duplicates on retry. Sequential emit-after-conversion is not sufficient unless the retry path is protected by equivalence-key suppression or another durable occurrence-identity proof (admission dedup only fires when equivalence_key exists — admission.rs:183-188,505-516; sources without it are never suppressed by that path).
notes:
2026-07-07 red team: random UUIDv7 event ids are NOT idempotency. Partial terminal success + partial failure must create durable debt or rely on proven occurrence dedup before retry.
Accelerant pointer (2026-07-08): execution packet = .agent/scratch/new/sinex_urgent_correctness_systematic_v2.md section 3 (shared with r6d.4); red-team report = new-gpt-pro/01-batch-a-receipt-red-team.report.md. Re-verify line anchors against master on claim.

title: Lifecycle restore should support material-scoped/stdin archive recovery
description: During ActivityWatch replay recovery for sinex-60r, restoring 246 archived original events required selecting ids from audit.archived_events and piping them through xargs into `sinexctl ops lifecycle restore`; a first zsh variable attempt passed the newline UUID list as one invalid argument. Add an operator-safe bulk restore surface, such as `ops lifecycle restore --source-material <uuid>`, `--operation <id>`, or explicit stdin/list-file handling with clear JSON reporting. The goal is not to hide replay failures; it is to make archive compensation/recovery reproducible without shell word-splitting traps.
acceptance_criteria: sinexctl ops lifecycle restore supports --source-material <id> (all archived events of a material) and --ids-from - (stdin UUID list, newline-separated); both paths validate UUIDs with a count summary + confirmation gate; the sinex-60r 246-event recovery scenario replayed via the new flag in one command. VERIFY: e2e or live-dev restore of a small archived set.

title: Audit stale devloop branch garden
description: Local branch inventory still contains many old pr/sinex-devloop/* branches that are not merged by Git's graph because the devloop train was squash-reconciled through later PRs, especially #2262. Build a branch-by-branch ledger that maps each old branch to the first-parent PR or Beads issue that superseded it, preserves any unique residual patch as a new issue/PR, then remove only branches whose value is proven redundant after explicit destructive-action confirmation.
acceptance_criteria: Branch ledger committed (scratch or .agent/artifacts): every pr/sinex-devloop/* + stale feature branch mapped to superseding PR/bead or flagged unique; unique residual diffs preserved (PR or bead note) before deletion; stale branches deleted; git branch listing on the checkout matches the ledger. Extends dkq (worktrees) to branches.

title: Multi-device single-user sync: capture satellites to one substrate
description: Resolve the horizontal-expansion trust tension (018 meta-gap): the operator's phone + laptop + server as capture satellites feeding one substrate; host as a dimension (already on every event) plus a host.identity entity. NOT multi-tenant, NOT federation, NOT CRDT - single user, single logical writer per material, so conflicts are structurally impossible. The single-trust-domain non-goal is preserved (one user, many devices).
acceptance_criteria: Design round output: satellite trust/transport model (auth, spool-and-forward, host identity) recorded as a design doc + implementation beads; NOT implementation. Done when the operator signs the design and the follow-up beads exist. (Vision bead — do not start implementation from this bead.)
notes: 2026-07-07 DeepResearch input: .agent/scratch/new-gpt-pro/dr-07-local-first-multidevice-sync.report.md — long-term solo-maintainable multi-device sync design survey (not migration-minimizing), grounded in the NATS/JetStream + Postgres shape.

title: Ambient surfaces: standing digests and resume briefs (push, not pull)
description: The sessionstart-sinex-recall hook already pushes context on session start; generalize to oracle-style standing digests (episode summary, coverage-error report, derivation-health board) plus resume briefs. The habit-loop half of the 7z0 UX epic - Sinex surfaces, rather than only answering when queried.
acceptance_criteria: At least two standing push surfaces live: a scheduled digest (episode/coverage/derivation-health) delivered to an operator-visible channel, and a resume brief on session start richer than today's recall preamble; each carries ViewEnvelope caveats; operator confirms the habit loop is on (uses them for a week without prompting). Depends on a4w.2 report substrate.

title: Counterfactual replay: re-derive a past window under an alternate regime, diff
description: Replay is already non-idempotent (re-derives under CURRENT rules) and the shadow-lane diff already A/Bs entity/relation regimes. Generalize to interventional what-if: re-derive the whole derived plane over a past window under regime B, diff vs live. The artifact: 'here is what my derived plane WOULD look like with this new rule, before I commit'. Immutable material makes any past window re-interpretable without loss.
acceptance_criteria: An operator can run: replay a past window under regime B into a shadow lane (no canonical writes), get a structured diff vs live derived plane (counts + exemplar rows per changed kind), and discard or keep the lane; documented as the pre-commit evaluation flow for rule changes. Gated on 0vx lane generalization + early-cutoff replay economics.

title: Reflection lane: an LLM proposes new derivation kinds as curation proposals
description: An LLM lane reads the derived plane and proposes NEW derivation kinds as CurationProposals; the taxonomy grows by adjudicated proposal, not hand-coding. This composes the three bold bets: deriv-dsl makes a proposal expressible as data, counterfactual-replay makes it evaluable before promotion, curation makes promotion safe. A system that extends its own ontology under adjudication.
acceptance_criteria: An LLM lane produces at least one CurationProposal for a NEW derivation kind (expressed as cyj DSL data) that passes adjudication and runs in a shadow lane; nothing auto-promotes; the proposal carries evidence refs for why the kind should exist. Gated on cyj + 7z0.4 judgment seat + a real LLM client (5v6 establishes the effects pattern).
notes: 2026-07-06 rawlog framing (06-28 22:17): the operator calls this class 'dreaming' — background processing that builds agent memory from the session/evidence archive ('if this works... great demo'). Keeps the implementable shape this bead already has: scheduled recorded analyses (k4c.1) over archives producing PROPOSALS, adjudicated before becoming standing memory/derivations. The slop-worry counter (06-28 22:22): the hard substrate (provenance, effects, adjudication) is what makes agent-built layers non-slop — tight loop + volume of use is the operator's stated methodology.

title: Split the 3012-line sinexctl mcp.rs god-file
description: crate/sinexctl/src/mcp.rs is 3012 lines - the largest source file in the workspace - hosting the entire MCP tool surface (~60 tools) in one module. Review friction and merge-conflict hotspot; the agent-facing contract lives here so it changes often.
acceptance_criteria: mcp.rs (3401 lines) split into tool-family modules (protocol/catalog/schemas/envelope/redaction + tools/{orient,events,sources,docs,semantic,telemetry,ops,system}) with a thin registry; zero tool renames or schema changes (snapshot tools/list + internal tool_catalog() before/after, tests fail on any diff except module paths); each module under ~800 lines; mcp tests pass unchanged. ADDITIONS (2026-07-07): a generated MCP tool matrix checked in tests — tool name, backing RPC, default limit, max limit, server-enforced clamp y/n, truncation caveat y/n, CLI equivalent, vocabulary exceptions; fix the two verified schema/struct mismatches — sinex_llm_prompts advertises source_identifier but LlmPromptsArgs ignores it (mcp.rs:345-351,1426-1441) and sinex_source_materials implements source_identifier but omits it from the schema (mcp.rs:373-380,1488-1502). VERIFY: xtask test -p sinexctl -E 'test(mcp)'; diff of tools/list output pre/post split.
notes: 2026-07-07 per-tool verdict table for all 68 tools (keep / fix+bound / merge / rename) is in new-gpt-pro sessions/10 final answer — use it as the checklist when splitting; several telemetry tools forward optional limits without server-side clamps.

title: context_pack MCP tool: project scoping degraded to a source-prefix guess (#1095 DTO unfinished)
description: The sinex.context_pack MCP tool — an AGENT-FACING surface (the widest way agents consume Sinex) — does not actually scope by project. crate/sinexctl/src/mcp.rs:~2808: project_path is meant to drive a project-scoped query but the real context-pack DTO from #1095 was never finished, so the code falls back to treating the path as a SOURCE-PREFIX hint: 'if the path parses as an EventSource, filter sources to that string'. For any real project path (e.g. /realm/project/sinex) this silently matches nothing or the wrong thing, and the agent receives a context pack that looks scoped but isn't — the exact 'misleading surface' failure mode (standing rule 6: honest gaps). This is distinct from project.attribution (a4w.3.3, the deep entity-join that would make project scoping CORRECT): even before that lands, the tool must not pretend to scope. Options: (a) honest degradation — if project_path can't be properly resolved, return unscoped results WITH an explicit caveat naming the limitation and the blocking work (#1095 / a4w.3.3), rather than a silent wrong filter; (b) wire real project scoping once project.attribution exists. Do (a) now (cheap, honest), (b) when the substrate lands.
design: DECISION: mirror the recall honesty pattern — never silently mis-scope. Immediate fix: when project_path is present, attempt real resolution (project entity if project.attribution is live; else the documented fallback), and whenever the result is not a true project scope, attach a structured caveat to the context-pack output ('project_path scoping unavailable: returning source-hint/unscoped results; blocked on #1095 context-pack DTO / a4w.3.3 project.attribution') so the consuming agent knows the scope is approximate. Real scoping (b) becomes a follow-up wired to a4w.3.3. Check the other MCP tools in the same pass for sibling silent-degradation (nbi.6 covers the inert LLM read surfaces; this is the context-pack instance).
acceptance_criteria: context_pack with a project_path either scopes correctly or returns results carrying an explicit scope-limitation caveat naming the blocking work; no path silently produces a wrong-but-plausible filter; a test asserts the caveat appears when real scoping is unavailable; the #1095 follow-up for true project scoping is linked to a4w.3.3.
close_reason: Implemented context_pack project_path honesty on this branch: project_path now resolves to an explicit scope state; true project scoping is not claimed until #1095 / sinex-a4w.3.3 lands; real filesystem paths return unscoped packs with context_pack.project_scope_unavailable caveat; source-like strings return source-hint filtered packs with the same scope-limitation caveat; no-project_path remains uncaveated unscoped. Verification: CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_TEST_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask test -p sinexctl -E 'test(context_pack)' job 2001435 passed; CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=1 CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_UNITS=16 xtask check -p sinexctl job 2001436 passed.

title: Application-level semantic hooks: neovim buffer events + kitty deep remote-control (editor/terminal semantics)
description: From the operator's multi-level-capture design conversation: the highest-semantic capture happens INSIDE applications, above the compositor. Two named targets not yet sourced (browser is rkv.3; terminal COMMANDS are shell.atuin already): (1) NEOVIM — a Lua autocmd hook emitting buffer/edit semantics (BufEnter/BufLeave/BufWrite: file path, filetype, mode, cursor, project) — the editor's view of what was being worked on, which fs-watcher (file.modified) cannot see (fs sees the write; nvim sees the intent, the navigation, the time-in-buffer before saving); feeds work.episode/change.episode with editor-grain attention. (2) KITTY deep remote control — beyond command capture: focus events with cwd + foreground processes + scrollback state (kitty's remote protocol exposes these), giving terminal ATTENTION and working-directory context that shell history alone lacks. The operator's framing: 'different levels of abstraction... correlated in all kinds of fun ways later' — each level is deliberately partial; the join is where value emerges. The multi-level-redundancy design principle (capture the same act at compositor + app + system level, eliminate redundancy downstream) is Sinex-native: overlapping interpretations of one occurrence, coalesced/tombstoned via the lifecycle+defrag machinery (lb8) when one level provably subsumes another — record that as doctrine, not a new mechanism.
design: DECISION: each app hook is an EXTERNAL PRODUCER (nvim Lua -> systemd-cat/NATS bridge; kitty python-kitten -> bridge) publishing EventIntent — same external-producer pattern as polylogued/hyprland-plugin, sinexd hosts nothing. Kinds by role: editor.buffer.focused / editor.buffer.written (Activity+Content role, Point; file ref for entity-join to project.attribution and fs events; cursor/mode as payload); terminal.focus.observed (Activity role; cwd + foreground_processes for project entity-join, complementing shell.atuin command events). Occurrence keys from (app session, buffer/window id, timestamp). ts_orig = the in-app event time (RealtimeCapture). REDUNDANCY DOCTRINE (record in the derivation catalog, do not build new machinery): the same real act may be captured at multiple levels (nvim BufWrite + fs file.modified + hyprland focus); these are distinct interpretations of related occurrences, NOT duplicates to suppress at ingest — they carry different information; downstream coalescing/eviction (lb8 defrag, lpz events-are-cache) removes a level only when another provably covers its information, as an explicit operator-visible lifecycle op. START SMALL per the operator's own 'add only what annoys you not having' — nvim buffer hook first (highest editor-semantic value, trivial Lua), kitty deep hook second. PITFALL: do not let app hooks re-capture what a lower level already has well (kitty COMMANDS stay atuin's job — capture terminal ATTENTION/cwd here, not re-log commands).
acceptance_criteria: A neovim editing session produces buffer-focus/write events with file+project refs queryable and joinable to the same window's fs events; a kitty pane's cwd and foreground process are captured as terminal attention distinct from the atuin command log; both run as external producers; the multi-level redundancy doctrine is written into the derivation catalog; no app hook duplicates a lower level's well-covered data (kitty does not re-log commands). VERIFY: nvim + kitty bridges emit to dev NATS; cross-level join query (nvim buffer x fs modified x window focus for one edit) returns coherent multi-level evidence.

title: Compositor-plugin telemetry tier: focus-change reasons, input latency, render/damage perf (Hyprland-internal)
description: From the operator's capture design conversation: a Hyprland PLUGIN can access data available NOWHERE else — the compositor internals. Distinct from the IPC socket2 fix (sinex-60r, which gives clean real-time focus/workspace/window transitions from the socket); this is the deeper tier that requires living inside the compositor. Genuinely-unique signals the operator flagged as most valuable: (1) FOCUS-CHANGE REASONS — not just that focus changed but WHY (mouse click / alt-tab / workspace switch / window close) — turns attention.stream from 'what had focus' into 'how attention moved', which is qualitatively richer for interruption.event (a4w.3.2) and work.episode; (2) input latency (keypress -> window receipt) and compositor render/damage/frame perf — a machine-health signal tied to the actual desktop, feeding machine.context (a4w.3.5) ('that felt laggy' becomes measurable); (3) window geometry/animation/group operations. The operator also settled the storage question in-conversation: single events table with event_type as the virtual-table discriminator + partial indexes handles 144Hz fine — which is exactly Sinex's existing model (one hypertable, typed payloads, event-type routing), so NO separate table is needed; high-frequency perf events are just a declared high-volume class.
design: DECISION: this is an OPTIONAL deep tier layered above sinex-60r, not a replacement — 60r's socket2 driver is the baseline attention source and ships first; the plugin tier is additive and Hyprland-specific (accept the coupling: operator is single-user on Hyprland, extension.md-style external-producer if/when portability matters). Shape: a Hyprland plugin (C++) as an EXTERNAL PRODUCER publishing EventIntent to NATS (same pattern as polylogued) — sinexd does not host it; the plugin writes to a shm/socket bridge that publishes. SPLIT BY VOLUME CLASS + SEMANTIC ROLE into distinct kinds so the high-frequency perf lane never drowns the semantic lane (the telemetry-lane-separation lesson, x9i, applied at capture): desktop.focus.changed (Activity role, Point, carries the REASON enum — the high-value low-volume signal); desktop.render.sampled (Reflexive/machine role, high-volume — declared volume class, its own retention, feeds machine.context, NOT the activity plane); desktop.input.latency (machine role, sampled not per-event). Interval lifting (focus spans) stays L2 (catalog rule 4) — the plugin emits transitions with reasons, not spans. PITFALL: do not emit per-frame at 144Hz into the main event stream — sample/aggregate render perf at the source (e.g. per-second percentiles) or route to the reflection lane; raw per-frame is capture abuse. The operator's single-table conclusion is correct for Sinex and needs no schema change.
acceptance_criteria: Focus changes carry a reason (mouse/alt-tab/workspace/close) distinguishable in queries; a laggy interval is visible as elevated desktop.render latency joinable to what was focused; the perf lane's volume does not appear in activity-plane recall (separated like telemetry); the plugin runs as external producer with sinexd unaware of its process; render sampling is bounded at source (no raw 144Hz in the stream). VERIFY: plugin emits to dev NATS, events queryable; volume-class check that render events route to the reflection lane.

title: AT-SPI2 semantic capture: accessibility widget trees as lossless digital text (the anti-OCR source)
description: From the operator's comprehensive-capture design conversation (2026-07-03 download): the standout insight was that AT-SPI2 accessibility APIs expose complete widget trees — 'so much semantic data', 'why isn't this shit already done'. This is the CONTENT role's answer without OCR: on-screen text as actual digital strings (labels, text fields, document content, menu items) with structural context (widget hierarchy, roles, the owning application), not lossy pixel recognition. It fills exactly the catalog's weakest role (Content = 'weak: documents live, screen-OCR partial'). Complementary to OCR (a4w.3.7), not redundant: AT-SPI2 gives exact text for accessibility-aware toolkits (GTK/Qt apps expose it); OCR remains the fallback for canvas/pixel surfaces (some Electron, games, video). The operator's own framing: hook toolkit accessibility to 'supplant lossy OCR' where digital text already exists; route by surface type downstream (AT-SPI2-capable window -> skip OCR).
design: DECISION: a continuous SourceDriver subscribing to the AT-SPI2 D-Bus registry (org.a11y.atspi) for focus/text-changed/document events, emitting semantic text observations anchored to the active window interval. Kind: ui.text.observed (Content role, StateObservation/Point) carrying text content BY MATERIAL ANCHOR (accessibility snapshots can be large — register the snapshot as material, event references it; never embed bulk, catalog rule 2), widget role, app identity, and the window ref for entity-join to attention.stream. Occurrence key = (window, widget path, content hash) so an unchanged field re-observed does not duplicate (equivalence key). ts_orig = observation time (RealtimeCapture — it is live perception). PRIVACY: this reads on-screen text including passwords fields and private content — declare Sensitive tier; password/secret widgets (role PASSWORD_TEXT) suppressed at source; relies on the central redaction chokepoint for the rest. VOLUME: potentially high on text-heavy focus churn — bounded by change-detection (only on text-changed/focus events, not polling) and volume-classed before joining wildcard fan-in. SURFACE-ROUTING: emit a capability signal (window is AT-SPI2-covered) so the OCR lane (a4w.3.7) can skip covered surfaces — the operator's 'we know we have it here, don't bother with OCR' routing. PITFALL: AT-SPI2 coverage is app-dependent (native GTK/Qt strong, Electron/Wayland-native variable); the source must render coverage honestly (which windows it can and cannot see) rather than implying total capture.
acceptance_criteria: Focused text in a GTK/Qt app (e.g. a document, a form field) is captured as digital text queryable via sinexctl, anchored to the window interval and resolvable to the app; password-role widgets produce no cleartext (test); an AT-SPI2-blind window (canvas/pixel app) renders as an explicit coverage gap, not silent absence; re-observing unchanged text does not duplicate occurrences; a covered window emits the OCR-skip capability signal. VERIFY: driver test against a known-accessible app fixture + live dev capture; privacy suppression test for PASSWORD_TEXT role.
notes: 2026-07-06 intent provenance (chat archaeology): AT-SPI2 semantic capture is ORIGINAL design intent, not late speculation — June-2024-era claude.ai sessions contain the discovery ('AT-SPI2 gives complete widget trees') and the operator reaction ('why isn't this shit already done... it seems genuinely trivial'), predating Sinex itself (RecordKeeper era). It is also the anti-OCR keystone: lossless digital text makes most screen-OCR unnecessary (content-axis bead defers OCR partly on this). Weigh this provenance in the next sequencing round.

title: Phase B: Sinex-primary evidence — Polylogue SQLite demotes to rebuildable read model
description: The long-term conceptually-correct form (epic backend trajectory): Sinex holds the evidence (session materials, attachment blobs, normalized observation events, provenance); Polylogue's SQLite becomes what Sinex doctrine calls a consumer-maintained projection — Polylogue-owned, Polylogue-shaped, rebuildable from the substrate. Polylogue keeps: its ontology (authority over what session/message/compaction/work-packet mean), its read models and indexes (FTS, embeddings, session topology — SQLite remains the right store for these), its UX and CLI, and full standalone operation (no Sinex -> Polylogue ingests exports into its own store exactly as today; the substrate is an upgrade, not a dependency). What changes with Sinex present: the archive's ground truth is the Sinex material registry + CAS (session JSONL, attachments, provider exports registered once, referenced everywhere); Polylogue's normalization writes observations to Sinex (sibling vocabulary child) and maintains its SQLite BY CONSUMING them (or its own parse — implementation choice, but the rebuild test is the contract); deletion/privacy actions flow through Sinex lifecycle (tombstones/archive) so the evidence plane has one authority for retention.
design: DECISIONS: (1) the contract is the REBUILD TEST, not a storage trait: polylogue rebuild --from-sinex reconstructs the SQLite read models from Sinex materials+events and a parity diff against the incumbent store comes back explainable-empty — that command existing and passing IS Phase B; internal storage abstraction is Polylogue's own business (polylogue-repo work, filed there when execution starts). (2) No dual-truth: while both stores exist, writes have one direction per data class — materials/observations: Polylogue producer -> Sinex; read models: Sinex/parse -> SQLite; never SQLite -> Sinex backfill except the one-time historical import. (3) Polylogue features that are pure read-model (marks, read-state, analysis notes) REMAIN SQLite-native but their actions also emit polylogue.annotation.* observations (vocabulary child) so the evidence plane sees operator behavior — the read state itself is not evidence, the act of reading is. (4) Sequencing: strictly after the vocabulary child proves stable at full grain and the moment-search child proves the substrate answers questions Polylogue's own store cannot — Phase B without demonstrated substrate value is architecture for its own sake (construct-validity gate).
acceptance_criteria: polylogue rebuild --from-sinex passes the parity test on the real archive; Polylogue standalone mode still fully functional (its suite green with no Sinex configured); retention/deletion of a session flows through Sinex lifecycle and the rebuilt read model reflects it; the one-direction write rule holds (no code path writes SQLite-derived data back as Sinex evidence).

title: Satellite capture hosts: horizontal expansion beyond sinnix-prime, with a multi-host VM suite
